title: Österreichischer Staatsschatzschein über Fünftausend Kronen (Austrian State Treasury Note for Five Thousand Crowns)
description: A bearer treasury note issued by the Austrian state for 5,000 Kronen, dated September 1, 1920 in Vienna, bearing serial number 008255 in Series 1920b. The note is designated as tax-free (steuerfrei) and interest-bearing, with provisions for redemption by the state debt administration. The document features elaborate green and pink decorative borders with floral motifs, a double-headed Austrian imperial eagle at the top center, and ornamental medallions at each corner. Issued in the immediate aftermath of World War I and the dissolution of the Habsburg Empire, this bond represents the new Austrian republic's efforts to raise capital during a period of severe economic instability and hyperinflation.
creator: Republic of Austria, Österreichische Staatsschuldenverwaltung
notes: Austrian State Treasury Note, Serie 1920b, No. 008,255, 5,000 Kronen, tax-free. Vienna, September 1, 1920. Signed by the Staatsschuldenverwaltung.
transcription:
# Austrian State Treasury Note - 5,000 Kronen

## Original (German):

**Header:**
Serie 1920b          5000 K          № 008255

Steuerfrei 6%iger, tilgbarer, lebenszeitlich kündbarer

**Title:**
Österreichischer Staatsschatzschein über Fünftausend Kronen

**Body Text:**
Die Staatsentleihasse in Wien zahlt an den Inhaber dieses auf Grund des Gesetzes vom 22. Juli 1920, St. G. Bl. Nr. 330, ausgegebenen Schatzscheines an dem durch die Kündigung bestimmten Tage, spätestens am 1. September 1926, den Betrag von 5000 Kronen. Der Inhaber ist berechtigt, diesen Schatzschein jederzeit für den Betrag des Tages der Kündigung nachfolgenden vierten Kalendermonates zur Rückzahlung zum Umtausch zu bringen. Die Kündigung erfolgt unter Vorbehalt des Zinsen nach Massgabe der Oster-äussrer Bankt Hauptnote Wien und in Filialen in Österreich sowie bei dem Postlesamtamt Wien und bei den Postämtern, im Auslande bei den bekanntgegebenen Poststeilen. Dieser nicht die einzige Kündigung auf dem Schatzscheine endgültig gemacht. Dieser Schatzschein wird mit sechs Prozent für jede Periode für die Dauer gefüllt. Die Zinsen werden ohne abug einer Renteusteuer jährlich im nachstem am 1. März und 1. September dieses Jahres an den Inhaber bei je Österr. Sparkasse gehörigt Schiene bei der Staatsentleihasse in Wien ausgezahlt. Die Verzinsung erfolgt mit Rückzahlungsgebühr des Kapitalbetrages. Der Schatzschein ist gegen Einlösung nach den zum fälligkeitstage noch nicht fällig geworbenen Annehmungen juristisch haften. Sollen die zum fälligkeitstage entfallenden Stilzinsen werden Einlösung des laufenden Anleihebetrages beglitet, der Vortrag der fälligen noch nicht fälligen Zinsschutsbeträge in Äußung gebracht. Die Rückzahlung des Kapitalcs erfolgt ohne Gebühren, Gebühren oder sonstige Wege wählt der Staatsentleihasse in Wien. Die Abmeldung dieser Schatzscheines erfolgt durch Verordnung in Äußung bei Sinnen je dem Daten, im Anlizung des Kapitals binnen 30 Tahren vom Publizitätsgege an. Dieser Schatzschein ist mit 12 Zinsfäldern, von denen der erste am 1. März 1921 fällig wird, versehen.

Wien, am 1. Dezember 1920.

Der Staatssekretär für Geldangner

[signature]

Dieser Schatzschein ist aufseglich begeben und im Handschrift der Staatssach eingetragen.

Der Präsident des Staatsrechnungshofes

[signature: "Ueeh"]

**Bottom section:**
Zur Rückzahlung für den 1. ..................... 192... gültig.

........................ am .......................... 192....

---

## English Translation:

**Header:**
Series 1920b          5000 K          № 008255

Tax-free 6% redeemable, cancellable for life

**Title:**
Austrian State Treasury Note for Five Thousand Crowns

**Body Text:**
The State Loan Office in Vienna pays to the bearer of this note, issued on the basis of the law of July 22, 1920, St. G. Bl. No. 330, on the day determined by cancellation, at the latest on September 1, 1926, the amount of 5,000 Crowns. The bearer is entitled to exchange this treasury note at any time for the amount of the day of cancellation following the fourth calendar month for redemption. Cancellation takes place with the reservation of interest according to the Austrian Bank main note Vienna and in branches in Austria as well as at the main post office in Vienna and at post offices; abroad at the publicly announced post offices. This is not the only cancellation on the treasury note made final. This treasury note is credited with six percent interest for each period for the duration. The interest is paid annually without deduction of income tax on March 1 and September 1 of this year to the bearer at each Austrian savings bank belonging to the State Loan Office in Vienna. Interest accrues with redemption fees for the capital amount. The treasury note is subject to redemption according to the conditions that are not yet due on the maturity date. Should the interest due on the maturity date be withheld from redemption, the outstanding portion of the due interest not yet paid remains due. Repayment of the capital is made without fees, charges or other costs to the State Loan Office in Vienna. The notification of this treasury note is made by decree in Vienna for 30 years from the date of publication. This treasury note has 12 interest coupons, the first of which is due on March 1, 1921.

Vienna, December 1, 1920.

The State Secretary for Finance

[signature]

This treasury note has been issued provisionally and registered in the register of the State debt.

The President of the State Audit Office

[signature: "Ueeh"]

**Bottom section:**
For repayment on the 1st. ..................... 192... valid.

........................ on .......................... 192....

---

## Notable Markings:

- **Serial Number:** № 008255
- **Series:** 1920b
- **Denomination:** 5000 K (Kronen)
- **Issue Date:** December 1, 1920 (Wien/Vienna)
- **Maturity Date:** September 1, 1926
- **Interest Rate:** 6% per annum
- **Status:** Tax-free (Steuerfrei)
- **Signatures:** Two official signatures present (State Secretary and President of State Audit Office)
- **Decorative Elements:** Double-headed Austrian imperial eagle at top center; elaborate green and pink ornamental borders with floral motifs and corner medallions
- **Interest Coupons:** 12 coupons attached (first due March 1, 1921)

title: Burmese Government Bond
description: A Burmese government bond written entirely in Burmese script, featuring green and purple decorative borders with traditional floral and geometric motifs. The document displays the serial number 008358 in red numerals on both sides and a central circular vignette with the denomination printed in Burmese characters. The footer contains a date in the Burmese calendar system, suggesting issuance during the 1940s when Burma operated as a nominally independent state under Japanese occupation. The design incorporates traditional Burmese decorative elements including intricate petal and leaf motifs framing the central text, in a style consistent with official financial instruments of the era. This is a rare surviving example of financial documentation from wartime Burma, notable for its exclusive use of the indigenous script at a politically turbulent moment in Burmese history.
creator: Government of Burma
notes: Approximate date ca. 1943-1945 based on historical context of Japanese-occupied Burma

title: 5% Vutreshen Durzaven Zaem ot 1941g. za Narodnata Otbrana (5% Internal State Loan of 1941 for National Defense, Kingdom of Bulgaria)
description: A bearer bond issued by the Kingdom of Bulgaria ("Tsarstvo Bulgaria") for the 5% Internal State Loan of 1941, explicitly designated for national defense, valued at 5,000 leva, Series B, serial number 022882. The bond is issued under the authority of the Main Directorate of State and State-Guaranteed Debts and signed by senior officials. The document features a cream and brown color scheme with ornate circular decorations at each corner and a central panel prominently displaying the denomination of 5,000 leva alongside the 5% interest rate. Issued during World War II when Bulgaria was aligned with the Axis powers under Tsar Boris III, this bond was intended to fund military expenditure. The phrase "Na Prinositelya" (to the bearer) confirms it was a negotiable bearer instrument payable to whoever presented it.
creator: Kingdom of Bulgaria, Main Directorate of State Debts
notes: Approximate issue date; exact date not visible on document

title: Hoe de Assurantie-Compagnie van Rotterdam kan werden gevodert, en dat niemant zou verliezen (How the Insurance Company of Rotterdam Can Be Promoted, and That No One Would Lose)
description: Page 1 of 7 – The first page of a seven-page Dutch pamphlet proposing the establishment of a Rotterdam insurance company during the speculative bubble of 1720, presenting numbered articles (1 through 7) outlining how investors could participate without risk of loss. The text is set in a simple typographic style with an ornamental initial capital, characteristic of Dutch commercial printing of the early eighteenth century. This document was included in "Het Groote Tafereel der Dwaasheid" (The Great Mirror of Folly), a famous Dutch compilation documenting the speculative mania of 1720. The title's promise that "no one would lose" is characteristic of the optimistic—and often misleading—claims made by company promoters during this financial bubble. The pamphlet reflects the proliferation of prospectuses during this period, when hundreds of speculative ventures were promoted across the Dutch Republic.

transcription:
# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

(1)

Hoe de Assurantie-Compagnie van Rotterdam kan worden gevordert, en dat niemant zou verliezen.

1. Zynde de Inburgering van deze Compagnie ter forma van twaalf milliuenen geldschat op den 22 Juny 1720, is't met de zelfde gewegt een nooyt gehoorde oploup.

2. Een ieder weet dat de winsten der Assurantie zeer hondert guldenen in vier en twintig geleagers, dat men 't cheeit-vogtoot tot 200 guldeng, en voor een uyt of 250 opite gegeven te per Cent premie, om te inogen eylchem tot hondert en eenige guldeng.

3. Niemand zou gisloogen willen als in Vrankryk en Engellang met Achten in Compagnie-tschappen ons onlange syn godoam, hesben bij vele der Impotelle rewinacht, al was 't al wildt, dat naar Adele hete, zod dat zy alle rationaaluenten verwerpen, en zeggm, dat men in Achten naar doen moet toeloogen.

4. Maar ten minste dart is lets, waar in de Achten moeten werden gewaardert. En waar va 'al die waardering geslachten, als in't Fonds, Inkomeu, of eenig voorrecht, of apparente hope van dien, welke men de Achten val te.

5. Beschouw nu dese Compagnie, 't Fonds, of Capital, is too re gegeven, niels alleen a fluivers per 100 guldens. Eenig bijzonder, of profitabel voorrecbt boven particuliere, of hopes, dat tve hecft re niet 't minste, dat behent is.

6. Haar Inkomem zal syn 't profyt van de Assurantie, dat syn hier niet boven 6 a 7 Assuradiene, die naam mogeu hebben, dat him windt zeer Geld in 't jaar zou kunnen waardig maaken, zoo dat'al zeer verre van de winsten der Directeuren veel te spreeken, zijnde al zeer whinnen, hoe groot dan hier te reckenen de windt van dese Compagnie?

[unsigned] De Compagnie zal ook Assuradene bayren &c. voor brans a 4 duivers per hundert guldeng, last hiet van Goclthaert werden, voor vyftig millioenen (dat haashvck is, of 't zal zyn) in 's jaars hondert duysent guldeng premic.

A [signature mark] S. Dar

---

**English translation:**

(1)

How the Insurance Company of Rotterdam Can Be Promoted, and That No One Would Lose.

1. Since the establishment of this Company in the form of twelve million guilders of capital on June 22, 1720, it is with the same weight an unheard of commotion.

2. Everyone knows that the profits of Insurance are very hundred guilders in four and twenty lodgers, that one 't cheeit-vogtoot to 200 guilders, and for one uyt or 250 opite given to per Cent premie, in order that each one unto hundred and some guilders.

3. No one would guess willing as in France and England with Achten in Company-associations our unlange syn godoam, hesben by many of the Impotelle rewinacht, even if it were al wildt, that toward Adele hete, zoo that they all rational [?] renounce, and say, that one in Achten toward then must toeloogen.

4. But at least there is something, wherein the Achten must be valued. And where va 'all this valuation genera, as in't Fonds, Income, or any privilege, or apparent hope of this, which one the Achten val te.

5. Consider now this Company, 't Fonds, or Capital, is too re given, not alone a fluivers per 100 guilders. Any particular, or profitable privilege above particuliere, or hopes, that tve hecft re not 't least, that behent is.

6. Its Income shall be the profit of the Insurance, that is here not above 6 a 7 Assuradiene, which names may have, that him wind very Money in 't year would be able to worthy make, zoo that 'al very far from the winnings of the Directors much to speak, being al very whinnen, how great then here to reckon the wind of this Company?

[unsigned] The Company shall also Assuradene bayren &c. for brans a 4 duivers per hundred guilders, let hiet of Goclthaert become, for fifty millions (that haashvck is, or 't shall be) in 's years hundred thousand guilders premic.

A [signature mark] S. Dar

title: Conditien van de Maatschappy tot het Asseureeren van Scheepen en Goederen, Binnen de Stad Middelburg (Conditions of the Company for Insuring Ships and Goods within the City of Middelburg)
description: A single-page printed broadsheet presenting the conditions of a maritime insurance company established within the city of Middelburg in the province of Zeeland, the Netherlands, set in two columns with numbered articles covering membership, share subscriptions, claims procedures, and governance. Middelburg was a major Dutch trading port and the capital of Zeeland, making it a natural center for maritime insurance ventures during the speculative climate of 1720. The two-column layout and Roman typeface are typical of Dutch commercial printing of the early eighteenth century, and the numbered articles present the company's terms in a formal legalistic manner intended to convey legitimacy. This document is likely associated with the speculative companies promoted during the Dutch financial bubble of 1720 and is preserved in the "Groote Tafereel" collection. The broadsheet format and dense text suggest it was intended for public posting or distribution to prospective investors.
creator: Maatschappy tot het Asseureeren van Scheepen en Goederen, Middelburg
notes: Likely from Het Groote Tafereel der Dwaasheid (1720); exact page count uncertain

# ENGLISH TRANSLATION:

**CONDITIONS OF THE COMPANY FOR THE ASSURANCE OF SHIPS AND GOODS WITHIN THE CITY OF MIDDELBURG**

[The document contains 20+ numbered articles establishing the terms of a maritime insurance company, including:]

**I.** The capital shall consist of 1000 pound shares, with each share-holder holding between 300 and 3000 Flemish pounds in shares.

**II.** Further details regarding the registration of shares.

**III.** No one shall obtain further shares unless they are properly registered.

**IV.** The Last Directors through the Report shall submit matters to [the commissioners], who shall handle affairs on a day-to-day basis; those Assurances from the Adherents that are properly filed shall be handled by the Commissioners...

**V.** The Commissioners shall be residents within Middelburg.

**VI.** All Positions shall be registered, and either the Commissioners or what is established shall prepare all Positions.

**VII.** All Petitions from those applying as Contributors and all Positions shall be submitted per Bank or Cash, in the Names of all the Commissioners, and all Positions shall be freely released, at minimum through the arrangement of the Commission members.

**VIII.** The Petitions from one tongue shall be noted, and the Effects, as they are distributed, shall be paid according to the Petitions.

**IX.** No Effects shall be sold or exchanged without express consent of the Commission.

**X.** The ongoing Petitions and Interest shall remain in the Box or Bank for the governance of the Treasurers.

**XI.** The Commissioners shall appoint Advisors on all matters.

[Articles XII-XX continue with additional governance and procedural matters, but become increasingly difficult to read with certainty]

title: Reglement op de Wisselbank Binnen Utrecht (Regulations for the Exchange Bank within Utrecht), 1720
description: Page 1 of 7 – The title page of a seven-page document establishing the Exchange Bank ("Wisselbank") within the city of Utrecht, adopted by the city council on 7 October 1720 and formally published on 11 October by Jacob van Poolsum, the official city printer ("Stads Drukker"). The page displays the Utrecht city coat of arms flanked by heraldic supporters, marking the document as an official civic publication. Exchange banks modeled on the Amsterdam Wisselbank were established in several Dutch cities during the early eighteenth century as stabilizing financial institutions for commerce. This document was issued at the height of the 1720 Dutch financial bubble, reflecting the city's effort to create a reliable monetary institution amid speculative chaos. The formal civic authorization underscores the role of Dutch municipal governments in regulating financial activity during the early modern period.

The Burgomasters and Vroeschap of the City of Utrecht have deemed it good; for the benefit and convenience of commerce, to establish at the Town Hall here, a public money or exchange bank, under the direction of Commissioners chosen from His Excellency; which shall commence on the first of November 1720, under the following conditions:

I.

His Excellency declares for the security and complete assurance of every one (on behalf of the State) to be responsible and accountable for all the money that is lent at the Bank; and each person shall be able to withdraw his money from it at all times.

title: Inventaris van de Effecten, behorende aan de Colonie de Barbice (Inventory of Assets Belonging to the Colony of Berbice)
description: A Dutch inventory document listing the assets ("effecten") of the Colony of Berbice (present-day Guyana), including 895 enslaved people described as large and small ("groote en kleyne Slaven"), cacao plantations, Fort Nassau, military equipment including sixty cannon pieces, ships, weapons, livestock, medical supplies, and a church. The document is formatted as a numbered list of asset categories with quantities and estimated values, reflecting the bookkeeping conventions of Dutch colonial enterprise. The page number 574 visible at the bottom indicates this is an extract from a larger bound volume, likely related to speculative colonial investment promoted during the 1720 Dutch financial bubble. Berbice was a Dutch colony on the northern coast of South America administered under a chartered company structure, and this inventory reflects the entanglement of colonial enterprise, enslaved labor, and early modern financial speculation. The document is a stark record of the commodification of enslaved people alongside material assets in Dutch colonial accounting.
creator: Colony of Berbice / Dutch West India Company
notes: Extract from larger bound volume (page 574); likely from Het Groote Tafereel der Dwaasheid (1720) or related colonial prospectus

title: Project tot het ruineeren der Turkze Rovers van Miers, Tunis, Tripoly en Sale (Project for the Ruin of the Turkish Pirates of Algiers, Tunis, Tripoli and Sale)
description: Page 1 of 8 – The title page of a Dutch pamphlet proposing a project to eliminate ("ruineeren") the Barbary pirates ("Turkze Rovers") of Algiers, Tunis, Tripoli, and Sale, framed as beneficial not only for the security of Dutch shipping but also for the expansion of navigation and commerce across these trade routes. The title emphasizes dual objectives: protecting Dutch merchant vessels from North African piracy and freeing Dutch captive subjects held in the Barbary states. The document is addressed to the States General and other authorities of the Dutch Republic, presenting the project as a matter of national economic and humanitarian interest. Barbary piracy had long posed a serious threat to European Mediterranean and Atlantic trade, and numerous proposals for its suppression circulated in the early eighteenth century. This pamphlet represents the intersection of Dutch commercial ambition, maritime security concerns, and the speculative project culture of the 1720 period.

title: Temple Mills (South Sea Bubble Playing Card, King of Diamonds)
description: A playing card from the English South Sea Bubble satirical deck depicting Temple Mills, showing workers at a water mill with figures carrying sacks while a king of diamonds is inset in the upper left corner. The satirical verse reads: "By these Old Mills, Strange Wonders have been done / Numbers have Suffer'd, yet they Still Work on; / Then tell us which have done the Greater Ills, / The Temple Lawyers, or the Temple Mills." The card draws a sardonic comparison between the exploitative practices of lawyers practicing at the Temple in London and a fraudulent speculative venture promoting the Temple Mills site as an investment opportunity. The South Sea Bubble playing card deck was published around 1720 to mock the proliferation of speculative schemes and the gullibility of investors during the financial frenzy that preceded the collapse of the South Sea Company. Each card in the deck depicts a different fraudulent or impractical scheme, using satirical verses and engraved illustrations to ridicule both the promoters and their victims.

# Transcription

**Title (top of card):**
Temple Mills

**Caption (bottom of card):**
By these Old Mills, Strange Wonders have been done
Numbers have Suffer'd, yet they still Work on;
Then tell us which have done the Greater Ill,
the Temple Lawyers, or the Temple Mill.

**Notable markings:**
- Portrait of a playing card figure (King of Diamonds) in upper left corner, wearing ornate period clothing with a red diamond symbol
- Engraved illustration showing Temple Mills with brick buildings, a water wheel on the right, figures in 18th-century dress in the foreground, and architectural structures in the background
- This is identified as part of a set of South Sea Bubble satirical playing cards from Great Britain

---

# Transcription

**Title (top):** Puckle's Machine

**Text on wheels (top right):**
- Left wheel: Round Bullets [illegible] [illegible]
- Right wheel: Square Bullets [illegible] [illegible]

**Main caption (bottom):**
A rare invention to Destroy the Crowd,
Of Fools at Home instead of Foes Abroad.
Fear not my Friend, this terrible Machine,
They're only Wounded that have Share therein.

**Card designation (top left):** Eight of Diamonds (spade symbols visible)

**Notable markings:**
- Playing card suit symbols (spades/diamonds) in top left corner
- Two mechanical wheel designs illustrated at top center
- Engraved illustration showing figures operating a cannon-like machine with various people in period dress (17th-18th century clothing)
- Building structure visible in background
- This is part of a South Sea Bubble satirical playing card series

---

# Transcription

**Title (top of image):**
An inoffensive way of emptying Houses of Office

**Caption (bottom of image):**
Our fragrant Bubble, would the World believe it,
Is to make Humane Dung, Smell Sweet as Violet:
None Sure before us, ever durst prefume,
to turn a't—d, into a Rich Perfume.

**Notable markings:**
- Decorative border frame around entire image
- Small spade symbol (♠) in top left corner near crowned royal figure
- Engraved illustration in woodcut/etching style
- Period spelling conventions preserved (e.g., "prefume" for "perfume," "durst" for "dared," "a't—d" for censored word)

---

# Transcription

**Title:** Baftard Children

**Text at bottom:**

Love on ye jolly Rakers, and burcome Dames, —
A Child is safer than venereal flames ;
Indulge your Senses with the Sweet offence, —
We'll keep your Baftard at a Small expence .

**Notable markings:**

- Suit symbol (Spade) visible in top left corner
- Decorative illustration showing a woman in a doorway (top left) and multiple figures in period dress in an urban courtyard setting (center/bottom)
- Brick buildings depicted in background
- Engraved/etched style typical of 18th-century playing cards
- Aged paper with visible wear and discoloration

---

# Transcription

**Title:** Raddith Oil

**Text at bottom:**

Our Oily project with the Caping Town,
Will Surely for a time go Smoothly down,
We Sonr and Presf. to carry on the Cheat,
to Bite Change Alley is not Fraud but Wit.

**Notable markings:**

- Portrait vignette in upper left corner showing a figure in period dress holding what appears to be a staff or scepter, with a spade symbol above
- Main illustration showing a coastal/waterfront scene with multiple figures engaged in various activities, including wading in water, working with equipment, and managing goods. A fortified town or settlement is visible in the background
- Horizontal line hatching used throughout for shading and depth
- Engraved in the style of early 18th-century satirical playing cards

---

# Transcription

**Title:** Whale Filthery

**Body Text:**
Whale Fishing, which was once a gainfull Trade,
Is now by cunning Heads, a Bubble made;
For round the Change they only Spread their Sails,
And to catch Gudgeons, bait their Hooks with Whales.

**Notable markings:**
- Heart symbol (red) in upper left corner, indicating this is the King of Hearts from a playing card set
- Decorative border frame around entire image
- Engraved illustration showing a whaling scene with ships, boats, whales, and coastal landscape
- Figure of a King in ornate period dress depicted in upper left corner vignette
- Historical engraving style with cross-hatching technique

---

# Transcription

**Title (top of page):**
Cureing Tobacco for Snuff

**Caption (bottom of page):**
Here Slaves for Snuff, are sitting Indian Weed,
Whilst their O'erseer, does the Riddle feed.
The Dust arising, gives their Eyes much trouble,
to shew their Blindness that Espouse the Bubble.

**Notable markings:**
- Red wax seal or mark in upper left corner
- Decorative vignette in upper left showing a woman's profile
- Engraved coat of arms or maker's mark visible near bottom left of main scene
- Fine line engraving throughout typical of playing card design
- Page number "7" (implied from context of 51-card set)

---

# Transcription

**Title:** Holy Island - Salt

**Text at bottom:**

Here by mixt Elements of Earth and Water,
They make a Mud, that turns to Salt hereafter;
To help the Project on among Change Dealers!
May all bad Wives like Lot's become Salt Pillars!
Since crowds of Fools delight to be Salt Sellers.

**Notable markings:**

- Red heart symbol in upper left corner (indicating this is the King of Hearts suit from a playing card deck)
- Engraved illustration showing allegorical figures and salt-making processes
- Figure of a suited king in period dress in upper left corner
- Landscape scene with figures engaged in salt production in the main composition
- This is page 8 of 51 from a set of South Sea Bubble satirical playing cards

---

# Transcription

**Title (top of page):**
Furnishing of Funerals to all parts of Great Britain

**Verse (bottom of page):**
Come all ye Sickly Mortal! Die apace,
And Solemn Pomps your Funeral shall Grace,
Old Rusty Hackney! Shall attend each Hearse,
And Scare-Crow in Black Gowns compleat the Farce.

**Notable markings:**
- Playing card suit symbol (red diamond) in upper left corner
- Decorative illustration of a Queen of Diamonds card in upper left
- Engraved illustration showing a funeral procession approaching a church with clergy, pallbearers, and mourners
- Historical woodcut/engraving style typical of early 18th-century satirical playing cards
- Page notation visible in document context: "Page 9 of 51"

---

# Transcription

**Title:** Corral Fishery

**Main Text:**
Corral that Beautious product only found,
Beneath the Water, and above the Ground,
If Killʼd for as it ought from thence might Spring,
A Neptunes Pallace for a British King.

**Notable markings:**
- Diamond suit symbol in upper left corner
- Engraved portrait of a figure in period dress (King of Diamonds) in upper left
- Ship with full sails in the harbor scene
- Multiple figures on shore examining coral and shells
- Coastal landscape with mountains in background
- This is page 10 of 51 from a set of South Sea Bubble Playing Cards (King of Diamonds)

---

# Transcription

**Title (top):** Irish Sail Cloath

**Caption (bottom):**
If good S.t Patricks Friends should raise a Stock,
And make in Irish Looms true Holland's Duck,
Then shall this Noble Project by my Soul—
No longer be a Bubble, but a Bull.

**Notable visual elements:**
- Woodcut illustration showing a figure in royal dress (top left, labeled as Irish nobility)
- Interior scene depicting three figures holding a large piece of fabric/cloth
- One figure stands elevated on a stool or platform
- Two large windows with latticed panes visible in background
- Period engraving style typical of early 18th-century satirical playing cards

---

# Transcription

**Title (top):**
Lending Money upon Bottom-Ree

**Main text (bottom):**
Some lend their Money for the sake of More,
And Others borrow to Encrease their Store,
Both these do oft Engage in Bottom Ree,
But Gwie Sometimes the Bottome of the Sea.

---

## Notable visual elements:

- **Upper left:** Playing card suit symbol (Club) with an ornate portrait of a Queen wearing elaborate period dress and headdress
- **Central image:** Detailed engraving depicting a harbor scene with:
  - Buildings with distinctive architecture on the left
  - Multiple sailing vessels in the water
  - Figures engaged in trading/financial activities on the shore
  - A lighthouse or fortification structure in the background
  - Various maritime and commercial activity
  
- **Overall condition:** Aged, worn paper with visible wear and discoloration consistent with historical document age
- **Style:** Woodcut or engraving typical of 18th-century satirical playing cards related to the South Sea Bubble financial scandal

---

# Transcription

**Title (top):** The Freeholder

**Main text (bottom):**
Come all ye Spendthrift Prodigals, that hold
Free Land and want to turn the Same to Gold; —
Wil Buy your all, provided you'll agree
To Drown your Purchase Money in South Sea. —

**Notable markings:**
- Club suit symbol (♣) in upper left corner
- Playing card format with engraved illustration showing a figure in period dress (top left) and a scene of multiple men in 18th-century clothing gathered around a table with large sheets of paper (center)
- Aged paper with visible creasing and wear

---

# Transcription

**Title:** River Douglas

**Text at bottom:**

Since Bubbles came in vogue, new Arts are found,
to cut thro' Rocks, and level rising Ground,
that murm'ring Waters, may be made more Deep,
to drown the Knaves, and lull the Fools asleep.

**Notable markings:**

- Small spade symbol (suit marker) in upper left corner
- Red circular stamp or seal impression in upper left corner
- Border frame around entire image
- Engraved landscape illustration showing the river, rocky terrain, multiple figures engaged in construction/engineering work, and buildings in the background

---

# Transcription

**Title:** Grand Filhery

**Text at bottom:**

Well might this Bubble claim the Style of Grand,
Whilst they that rais'd the same could Shift by Land;
But now the Town does at the Project Pish,
They've nothing else to cry but Stinking Fish.

**Notable markings:**

- Two spade symbols in a box on the left side of the card
- This is a playing card from a satirical deck mocking the South Sea Bubble financial crisis
- Engraved illustration showing a large merchant ship with multiple masts and sails, accompanied by smaller rowing boats in the water
- The ship appears to be depicted in a style typical of 17th-18th century maritime engravings

---

# Transcription

**Title:** Cleaning the Streets

**Main Text:**
A Cleanly project, well approv'd, no doubt,
By Stroling Dames, and all that walk on Foot:
This Bubble well deserves the Name of best,
Because the Cleanest Bite of all the rest.

**Notable markings:**
- Three spade symbols in a vertical column on the left margin (indicating this is the King of Diamonds from a South Sea Bubble playing card set)
- Decorative flourishes at the end of lines
- Period engraving/woodcut style illustration showing a street cleaning scene with a cart, workers, and buildings

---

# Transcription

**Title:** Filth Pool

**Text at bottom:**

How famous is the Man, that could contrive,
To serve this Gluttonous Sonn with Fish alive?
But now we're bubbl'd by his Fishing Pools,—
And as the Men catch Fish the Fish catch Fools.—

**Notable markings:**

- Four spade symbols visible in the upper left corner (suit indicator for playing card)
- Engraved illustration in classical style showing a large merchant vessel with multiple masts and sails, accompanied by smaller boats. Large fish and sea creatures are depicted in the water below, with sailors engaged in fishing activities.
- This is a satirical playing card from the South Sea Bubble era, using the "Filth Pool" as metaphorical commentary on financial speculation and fraud.

---

# Transcription

**Title (top):** York Buildings

**Main text (bottom):**
You that are blest with Wealth, by your Creator,
And want to drown your Money in Thames Water;
Buy but York Buildings, and the Cistern there,—
Will sink more Pence, than any Fool can spare.

**Heraldic element (top left):**
Shield containing five trees (spade symbols)

**Notable markings:**
- This is a playing card from the South Sea Bubble series
- Identified as Five of Spades
- Heraldic coat of arms in upper left corner featuring five trees arranged in a shield
- Flag or banner visible in the building illustration reading "York Building"
- Hand-colored engraving with period satirical commentary on the York Buildings Company investment scheme

---

# Transcription

**Title (top of card):**
Insurance on Lives

**Main text (bottom of card):**
Come all ye Gen'rous Husbands, with your Wives,
Insure round Sums, on your precarious Lives;—
That to your comfort, when you're Dead & Rotten
Your Widows may be Rich when you're forgotten

**Notable visual elements:**
- Suit symbol (top left): Six spades arranged in two columns
- The image depicts an insurance office scene with multiple figures, including what appears to be an insurance agent or clerk at a desk on the right, and several men and women (husbands and wives) on the left waiting or conducting business
- Windows with cross-hatched panes are visible in the background
- The architectural style and clothing suggest early 18th century
- This is a satirical playing card from the South Sea Bubble era, mocking the insurance and financial schemes of the period

---

# Transcription

**Title (top of image):**
Stockings

**Text at bottom of image:**
You that delight to keep your Sweaty Feet,
By often changing Stocking Clean and Sweet,
Deal not in Stockin Shares, because I doubt
Those that buy most, e'erlong will go without.

**Heraldic device (top left):**
Seven of Spades - displayed as a suit symbol with seven spade pips arranged in traditional playing card fashion

**Notable markings:**
- Historical engraving/woodcut style typical of early 18th-century South Sea Bubble satirical playing cards
- The image depicts a domestic scene with multiple figures engaged in stocking-related activities, including someone at a spinning wheel
- Architectural elements include thatched-roof buildings and a garden with plants
- The heraldic spades in the upper left corner indicate this is the Seven of Spades from a satirical deck

---

# Transcription

**Title (top):**
Welch Copper

**Card suit (top left):**
Nine of Spades (nine spade symbols arranged in traditional playing card pattern)

**Main text (bottom):**
This Bubble for a time may currant pay,
Copper's the title but 'twill end in Brass;
Knaves oy it up, Fools Buy, but when it fail,
The lofing Crowd will Swear, Cots Spluttt Nail

**Notable visual elements:**
- Historical engraving depicting a landscape scene with multiple figures engaged in trading/commercial activity
- Man with a dog in the foreground
- Figures on horseback and standing figures conducting transactions
- A banner or flag labeled "Pice Faciat" (or similar) visible in the scene
- Vegetation and landscape elements typical of 17th-century engraving style
- Aged parchment appearance with visible wear

**Notable markings:**
None (no visible signatures, stamps, or serial numbers)

---

# Transcription

**Title (top of card):**
Providing for and employ-ing all the Poor in Gr: Britain

**Caption (bottom of card):**
The Poor when manag'd, and employ'd in Trade, —
Are to the public Welfare usefull made ;
But if kept Idle from their Vices spring
Whores for the Stews, and Soldiers for the King

**Notable markings:**
- Suit symbol: Ten of Spades (represented by ten spade symbols arranged in the upper left corner)
- This is a satirical playing card from the South Sea Bubble series, depicting various poor people engaged in labor and trade activities
- The engraved illustration shows multiple figures working with various tools and materials, including a water wheel, building, and various workers in different trades
- The card is aged and shows typical wear consistent with an 18th-century historical document

---

# Transcription

**Title (top):**
Hemp & Flax

**Main text (bottom):**
Here Hemp is Sown'd for Stubborn Rogues to Die in,
And foster Flax, for tender Skins to Lye in:
But Should the usefull Project be defeated;
the Knaves may prosper but the Fools are cheated.

**Notable markings:**
- Red heart symbol in upper left corner
- Engraved illustration in classical style depicting workers in a field with buildings, trees, and birds in sky
- Border frame around entire card
- Hand-written or cursive script for the poetic text at bottom

---

# Transcription

**Title:** Manuring of Land

**Text at bottom:**

A Noble Undertaking but abus'd,
And only as a Tricking Bubble us'd,
Much they Pretend to, but the Publish Fear,
They'll never make Corn Cheap, or Horse Dung Dear.

**Notable markings:**

- Two red hearts in the upper left corner (indicating this is a playing card - Two of Hearts)
- Engraved illustration showing two men working around a large tree, with rural landscape and figures in background
- Border frame around entire image
- Classical engraving style with crosshatching for shading

---

# Transcription

**Title:** Coal Trade from Newcastle

**Main text at bottom:**

Some deal in Water, Some in Wind like Fools,
Others in Wood, but we alone in Coals;
From such like Projects, the declining Nation,
May justly fear a fatal inflammation.

**Notable markings:**

- Three red hearts in shield/crest in upper left corner
- Engraved border around entire image
- Historical playing card format (King of Diamonds, from South Sea Bubble series)
- Dated as page 25 of 51 in the playing card set
- Hand-drawn or engraved illustration showing workers, horses, a large cylindrical structure (coal pit or storage), and a merchant vessel in background
- Aged parchment/paper with visible wear and discoloration

---

# Transcription

**Title (top right):**
Water Engine

**Text (bottom of card):**
Come all ye Cully, my Water Engine buy,
to Pump your flooded Mines, and Cole Pit dry;
Some Projects are all Wind, but ours is Water,
And tho at present low, may rise hereafter.

**Notable markings:**
- Four red heart symbols in the upper left corner (indicating this is the Four of Hearts from the playing card deck)
- Engraved illustration showing two men operating a water pumping engine near a body of water, with buildings and landscape in the background
- Border line around the entire card
- Aged, worn paper with visible creasing and discoloration consistent with historical artifact

---

# Transcription

**Title (top right):**
Royal Fishery of Great Britain

**Verse (bottom):**
They talk of distant Seas, of Ships, and Nety,
And with the Stile of Royal Gild their Baits;
When all that the Projectors, Hope or Wish for,
Is to catch Fool, the only Chubs they Fish for.

**Notable markings:**
- Suit symbol: Five of Hearts (top left corner, displayed as five red heart shapes arranged in standard playing card pattern)
- Decorative border frame around entire image
- Engraved illustration depicting a coastal fishing scene with multiple sailing vessels, workers on shore, and maritime activity
- Mountains visible in background
- This is identified as part of a satirical playing card series related to the South Sea Bubble financial scandal

---

# Transcription

**Header (top right):**
Erecting Houfes of Office in N. Britain for Strangers & Travellors

**Main text (bottom):**
A usefull Project merrily advanc'd,
Sho chiefly by Tom-turdmen countencanc'd,
Design'd to fweeten the North British Nation,
And put Clofe Stool, and Bed Pan, out of fafhion.

**Notable markings:**
- Six red hearts arranged vertically on the left side (characteristic of a playing card suit)
- Engraved illustration in the center depicting a landscape scene with figures, buildings, water, and various activities
- Ornamental scrollwork at the top of the illustration frame

---

# Transcription

**Title (top right):**
Building Ships to left to freight

**Suit indicator (top left):**
Seven of Hearts [displayed as seven red hearts arranged in a standard playing card pattern]

**Main text (bottom of card):**
Who but a Nest of Block-heads to their cost,
Would build new ships for freight, when trade is lost?
To raise fresh Barks, must surely be amusing,
When Hundred rot in Docks, for want of using.

---

## Notable markings:

This is a satirical playing card from the South Sea Bubble series, using verse to critique the economic speculation and poor business decisions of the era. The engraving style is typical of early 18th-century British financial satire.

# Transcription

**Title (top of image):**
Drying Malt by the Air —

**Text (bottom of image):**
Of all the Windy Projects now in vogue,
to fleece the Fool, and feed the cunning Rogue,
the Malting Bubble, seems to be most fair, —
Because our Malt Stery-own they work by Air.

**Notable visual elements:**
- Eight of Hearts playing card suit indicator (upper left corner, featuring 8 red heart symbols arranged in the traditional card layout)
- Engraved illustration depicting a brick building with a figure standing at a window holding a rake or similar tool, overlooking a courtyard with what appears to be malt spread on the ground for air-drying
- Decorative border frame around the entire card
- Period engraving style typical of early 18th-century satirical playing cards

# Transcription

**Header:**
English Copper & Brass

**Main Text (at bottom of image):**
the Headlong Fool that wants to be a Swopper,
Of Gold and Silver Coin, for English Copper, —
May in Change Alley, prove himself an Ass,
And give Rich Mettle, for adulterate Brass. —

**Card Suit Indicator:**
Nine of Hearts (shown in upper left corner with nine red heart symbols)

**Artist signature:**
S. [illegible] (partially visible at bottom of illustration)

- This is a satirical playing card from the South Sea Bubble era

# Transcription

**Title (top of card):**
Exporting Timber from Germany

**Main text (bottom of card):**
You that are Rich, and hasty to be Poor,
Buy Lumber Export from the German Shoar.
for Galloufes, built up of Forraign Wood,
If rightly us'd, may do Change Alley good.

**Notable markings:**
- Ten of Hearts suit indicator (shown as 10 red hearts in upper left corner)
- Engraved illustration showing a German port scene with timber loading operations, vessels, dock workers, and coastal fortifications
- Period engraving style typical of early 18th-century satirical playing cards

# Transcription

**Top of card (title):**
St. J.n L——ts Imp-rovement of Land

**Center banner (held by figures):**
an Act against Bubbles

**Bottom text (caption):**
The famous Knight that is the sole Projector Of the New Bubble, is a South Director, But would be better taken at his Hand, To raise Poor South Sea than Improve Poor Land.

**Notable markings:**
- Red diamond suit symbol in upper left corner
- Playing card engraving in 18th-century style
- Part of a 51-card set satirizing the South Sea Bubble financial crisis

# Transcription

**Title:** Greenland Trade

**Main Text:**
This Project was to catch, to Cut and Boil Vilge Whales, and other Monstrous Fish to Oil, A Stinking Bubble, th'e of late so Dear, Yet now the Greatest Sharers stink, for Bear.

**Notable markings:**
- Red diamond suit symbol in upper left corner (indicating this is a playing card from the Two of Diamonds)
- Engraved illustration depicting two figures on a shore with a large cauldron, a sailing vessel in the background, and coastal landscape with cliffs

# Transcription

**Title:** Insurance on Horses

**Main Text:**
You that keep Horses to preserve your Life,
And Pads to please your Wives, and Mistresses,
Insure their Lives, and if they Die we'll make
Full Satisfaction, or be bound to Break.

**Notable markings:**
- Four of Diamonds suit symbol (red diamond) in upper left corner
- Playing card format typical of South Sea Bubble satire cards from early 18th century
- Engraved illustration showing men examining dead horses with standing figures and a mounted horse in background
- Historical print with hand-colored elements (red diamonds on card suit)

# Transcription

**Title (top):** Bahama Islands

**Caption (bottom):**
Rare fruitfull Isles, where not an Able can find
A verdent Lust, or Thistle, to his Mind!
How then, must those Poor Silly Asses fare,
that leave their Native Land to Settle here?

**Suit indicator (top left):** Five of Diamonds (shown as five diamond symbols arranged in traditional playing card pattern)

title: Dutch Windkaart: King of Spades
description: This engraved playing card depicts the King of Spades (Heer) from a Dutch satirical card deck mocking the speculative bubble mania of 1720. A gentleman in fashionable dress stands holding a balance scale, weighing a shovel and sheet of paper against a purse of gold coins. The Dutch verse reads: "De schop, en't blad papier in de eene schaal gelegen, / Kan, door de braversshoop, een goud beurs overwegen" (The shovel and a sheet of paper in one pan of the scale / Can, through a gambler's luck, outweigh a purse of gold). The card satirizes speculative investment in stock company shares ("Acties"), suggesting that worthless paper backed only by luck could appear to rival real wealth.

notes: From the Dutch Windkaarten (Wind Cards) satirical playing card deck, ca. 1720 (Netherlands)
transcription:
# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Heer

De schop, en 't blad papier in de eene schaal gelegen,
Kan, door de Graversshoop, een goud beurs overwegen.

**English translation:**

Lord/Sir

The spade and the sheet of paper placed in one scale,
Can, through the Engraver's heap, outweigh a purse of gold.

---

**Notable markings:**

- Title at top: "Heer" (Lord/Sir)
- Spade symbol (♠) in top right corner
- Engraved illustration in woodcut or engraving style typical of 17th-century Dutch playing cards
- Text formatted as couplet/verse at bottom of card
- Part of "Pasquins Windkaart" series from "Het Groote Tafereel" (The Great Theatre)

---

# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Vrouw

Men zet de schop vrij neer, ons
spelen is verbruijd
De blinde razernij van Quimquampoix
heeft uit.

**English translation:**

Woman [Lady]

One sets down the spade freely, our
playing is exhausted
The blind madness of Quincampoix
has ended.

**Notable markings:**

- Title at top: "Vrouw" (Woman/Lady)
- Spade suit symbol (♠) in top right corner
- The text at bottom appears to be in 17th/18th century Dutch
- This is identified as "Queen of Spades" from the Pasquins Windkaart (Wind Chart) playing cards series from "Het Groote Tafereel" (The Great Theater)
- The reference to "Quimquampoix" (Quincampoix) appears to be referencing the Mississippi Bubble financial crisis

---

# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Kauegl

Prins Freddryks
mars, naar Vrianen

Ik slaprins Freddryks mars, om met
al de Schiegeleken.
Als wrind zoldaeten, naar Vrianen toe
te trekken.

**English translation:**

King [of Spades]

Prince Frederick's
march, toward Vriane

I sleep Prince Frederick's march, in order to meet
all the [soldiers/troops].
As wind soldiers, toward Vriane to
proceed.

---

**Notable markings:**

- Title "Kauegl" at top
- Spade suit symbol (large, solid black spade) in upper right
- Engraved illustration of a figure in 17th-century dress holding a large barrel/drum, with a feathered hat
- Background landscape showing a fortified city and military encampment
- This is page 3 of the Pasquins Windkaart (Pasquin's Wind Cards) from *Het Groote Tafereel*, a Dutch satirical playing card series
- The engraving style is characteristic of Dutch woodcut/engraving from the 17th century

---

# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Heer

Laurier Konning

't Heb de Achtes ceirst bedagt, maar een krâde Artiekans, Smit al mijn lof omwer, en schent mijn lauswer krans.

**English translation:**

Lord

Laurier King

I have the Eights first considered, but a crude Articans [or similar], Smite all my praise around, and shame my Laurel wreath.

---

**Notable markings:**

- Heart symbol containing the word "Heer" (Lord/Sir) in the upper right corner
- Border frame around the entire composition
- Historical engraving/etching style typical of 17th-century Dutch playing cards
- The figure is dressed in period clothing with elaborate decorative details
- Small cherub or putti figures visible in the background scene

---

# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Vrouw

De Actie ketel disteleerd,
Dat het papier in Goud verkeerd

Myn Lauwersman zolleerd om ieder
te bedotten,
Stookt goud uit klad papier, en
maakt't Geld al vol zotten.

**English translation:**

Woman

The active kettle is distilled,
That the paper turns to gold

My Laurius man will swear to fool everyone,
Cooks gold from scrap paper, and
makes all the money foolish people.

---

**Notable visual elements:**

- Heart symbol (♥) in upper right corner
- Engraved portrait of an elegantly dressed woman in 18th-century attire, holding what appears to be a financial document or certificate
- Landscape background with trees and water
- Period woodcut/engraving style
- Border frame around entire composition
- This appears to be from "Pasquins Windkaart" (Pasquin's Wind Card), a satirical playing card series mocking financial speculation and the South Sea Bubble era

---

# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Knegel of Boef

Uit Wanhoop.

Min boeye hart, 't geen door een reeks van Ackestrekken, Mij deed naar voordeel zien, word nu van spijt doorsteken.

**English Translation:**

Knave of Hearts

Out of Despair.

My boyish heart, which through a series of tricks seemed to me advantageous, is now pierced through with regret.

---

**Notable markings:**
- Heart symbol (♥) in top right corner
- Decorative border frame around entire image
- Landscape background with trees and terrain
- Figure depicted wearing 17th-century Dutch clothing with hat and holding what appears to be a heart-shaped object

---

# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Ik ben beschermd, Heer
Daar ieder kermd.

'k Ben voor de Zuid en West hun
windgeblaas beschut
Verwijl een laauwe Goest 't Heel Al
helpt in den dut.

**English translation:**

I am protected, Sir,
While everyone complains.

I am sheltered from the South and West winds' blast
While a lukewarm breeze helps the whole [thing] slumber.

---

**Notable markings:**

This is part of the "Pasquins Windkaart" (Pasquin's Wind Chart) playing cards series from *Het Groote Tafereel* (The Great Theatre). The image depicts a figure in 17th-century Dutch clothing holding diamond-shaped wind cards, dressed as the King of Spades (Heer/Lord). The illustration uses period engraving technique with crosshatching and line work typical of Dutch satirical prints.

---

# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Vrouw

Door sterk te blazen breck't het glas,
Net als t'met de Actie-bubbels was.

**English translation:**

Woman

By blowing hard the glass breaks,
Just as it was with the Stock-bubbles.

---

**Notable markings:**

- Title at top right: "Vrouw" (Woman)
- Diamond suit symbol (♦) in top right corner
- Decorative border frame around the entire image
- Engraving in classical style with fine line work (hatching/cross-hatching)
- Landscape background elements including foliage
- The figure depicted is an elegantly dressed woman in 17th/18th century attire with elaborate hairstyle and dress, shown blowing through a pipe at a glass bubble

---

# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Knegt

't Papier zo duur
Verdrookt door
't ruin'

Mijn Actiebrief verteend, hij kangeen
zon verdragen;
't Is ruin voor kleiner tijdj als al
de Bubbelylagen.

---

**English translation:**

Knave/Servant

The paper so expensive
Dries out through
The ruin

My stock certificate is ruined, he cannot
bear the sun;
It is ruin for a shorter time as all
the bubble schemes.

---

**Notable markings:**

- Title at top: "Knegt" (Knave/Servant)
- Diamond symbol in upper right (representing the suit)
- Sun with radiating lines in background
- Engraved illustration showing a figure in period dress (17th century) holding documents
- Landscape with rolling hills in background
- This appears to be page 9 from "Pasquins Windkaart" playing cards, part of "Het Groote Tafereel" (The Great Theater), a Dutch satirical work
- Text references financial/economic themes, likely commentary on the Dutch economic bubble of the period

---

# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Heer

cerst gelukkig,
Nu dronkkig.

K.Ben Directeur geweest, maar tot
mijn ongeluk, het geld droop door de zak, en het
mir niets als dronk.

---

**English translation:**

Sir

First lucky,
Now drunk.

I was a Director, but to
my misfortune, the money dripped through my pocket, and
nothing for me but drink.

---

**Notable markings:**

- Title at top: "Heer" (Sir)
- Club symbol (♣) in upper right corner
- Signature or artist mark visible in lower right area: "[artist mark]"
- This appears to be page 10 from a 55-page playing card series titled "Pasquins Windkaart" (Pasquin's Wind Cards) from "Het Groote Tafereel" (The Great Picture Book)
- Card designation: King of Clubs

---

# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Vrouw
Met Ikarus, en Facton,
Vergnoegelynum
man zijndediezon

Schoon't lot van Ikarus mijn man
valt tot een deel,
Ik hou nog moet, zo lang ik met
de klavers speel.

**English translation:**

Woman
With Icarus and Phaeton,
Pleasure[?]
man being the sun

Though the fate of Icarus [befalls] my man
in part,
I still have courage, as long as I [play]
with the clubs.

**Notable markings:**

- Club suit symbol (♣) in upper right corner
- Engraved illustration of a woman in elaborate 17th-century Dutch dress, holding a child and gesturing to a basket containing figures
- Classical mythological references (Icarus and Phaeton) in the text
- This appears to be page 11 of a 55-page playing card set called "Pasquins Windkaart" from "Het Groote Tafereel"
- The document is catalogued as the Queen of Clubs

---

# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Kneght

't Geluk brengt mij
Uit slavernij.

Mijn Aotiestar beeft mij van
knegt tot Heer gemaakt,
Schoon menig Heer daar door tot
breed' len is geraekt.

---

**English translation:**

Knight

Fortune brings me
Out of slavery.

My godfather has made me from
servant to Lord,
Although many a Lord has been
reduced to rags by it.

---

**Notable markings:**

- Title at top: "Kneght" (Knight)
- Club suit symbol (♣) in upper right corner
- This is identified as page 12 of 55 from Pasquins Windkaart playing cards from *Het Groote Tafereel*
- Engraved illustration in classical Dutch style with period clothing and maritime imagery (ship visible in background)
- Chain and shackles depicted at figure's feet

---

# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Men maakt zich frulks kwijt
het stinkt zelf daar het leid.

Weg met dit klad papier, 't geen
ieder een verlegen
Op straat werpt, wil ik meen in
lethas' poel te vegen.

**English translation:**

One gets rid of oneself so quickly,
it stinks there where it lies.

Away with this scrap paper, which
everyone ashamed
Throws on the street, I think to sweep into
Lethe's pool.

---

**Notable markings:**

- This is page 13 of 55 from "Pasquins Windkaart" playing cards from "Het Groote Tafereel"
- Identified as the King of Spades in the Dutch card series
- Engraved illustration with satirical/moralistic verse typical of 17th-century Dutch satirical prints
- Shows a figure in period dress with a hat using a shovel near buildings and a wheel
- A teardrop or leaf shape appears at the top of the image

---

# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Door mijne werd [unclear] gezien
Van al de Com-
pagnen:

Ik ben't diet al ondekt, en't klad

title: English Lottery Trade Card: Fortune's Carriage
description: This printed lottery trade card features a wood-engraved vignette of a horse-drawn carriage bearing the royal cypher "GR" (Georgius Rex), driven by Fortune, who raises a lottery ticket aloft. The text promotes a State Lottery offering prizes including three of £30,000 and four of £20,000, totaling £314,460 "In Stock and Money," sold by Swift at 1 Poultry, 12 Charing Cross, and 31 Aldgate High Street, London. The drawing date noted is 7th November. These small promotional cards were distributed widely to advertise British State Lottery drawings, which operated as a major source of government revenue from the late 17th century until their abolition in 1826.

creator: Swift & Co. (lottery agents)
notes: English State Lottery trade card / promotional broadside, ca. 1790–1826 (Great Britain). Issued by Swift & Co. or related lottery agents at Poultry, Charing Cross, and Aldgate High Street, London.
transcription:
# Transcription

Fortune invites you to choose
One, Two, or all the
Three Prizes of £50,000.
Four Prizes of £220,000.
&c &c making
a Grand Total of
£314,400
In Stock and Money.

SWIFT & T

11 Poultry, 12 Charing Cross &
31 Aldgate High Street

(All the Capitals
May be Drawn) 7th Nov't

DB

---

# Notable markings:

- Royal crown emblem at top of carriage illustration
- "FR" monogram on the side of the carriage
- Initials "DB" at bottom left
- Decorative scrollwork and flourishes throughout the document
- Engraved illustration of a horse-drawn lottery carriage with a winged figure (Fortune) riding on top

---

# Transcription

**LADY SNEERWELL.—School for Scandal.**

"There is no possibility of being witty without a little ill-nature; the *malice* in a *good thing* is the *barb* that *makes it stick*."

---

To say that your Ladyship's wrong I won't dare—
'Tis a dangerous subject to handle;
Gain but Fortune's favours, then what need you care?
Not a fig for the whole "School for Scandal."

---

The Lottery will be drawn 14th THIS MONTH, (January) and following Day.

---

## Notable markings:

The document shows age-related wear and discoloration typical of early 19th-century paper. No visible signatures, stamps, or serial numbers are present. The engraving depicts a well-dressed woman in Regency-era clothing holding what appears to be a fan or similar object.

---

# Transcription

**MONSIEUR MARPLOT.**

HERE comes Monsieur Marplot, half famish'd, from France,
To eat our Twelfth-Cake and teach John Bull to dance;
But all his fine bows, his congees, and grimaces,
Will ne'er introduce him to Johnny's good graces.
Should Britain's StateLott'ry now grant him a Prize,
He'd fix on the City's rich Freeholds his eyes:
But st-p, Monsieur Meagre, by gar, understand,
Old England won't spare you one inch of its land.

---

## Notable markings:

- Handwritten notation in lower left corner: "616 90"
- Document shows age-related wear and creasing
- Engraved illustration in period satirical style showing a thin, caricatured Frenchman in fashionable dress holding a fan

---

# Transcription

**QUEEN.**
**TITANIA.—A Midsummer Night's Dream.**

On the moon's silver beams, the fairy Titania,
Is yet, the gay Queen of delight,
To visit you, daughters and sons of Britannia,
An usher to preside for the night!
Now obey my commands, let the hours glide sweetly,
And each take their cake and their wine,
Let song, jest, and dance, go on jocund and featly,
And each toast his charmer divine;
And as your time flies, while fondly conversing,
Let the Lottery come in for its share,
Remember the Tickets are Fortune's dispersing,
Then haste and buy one for your fair.

---

**Notable markings:**

The page shows wear and aging consistent with early 19th-century printing. The illustration features an engraved winged fairy figure (Queen Titania) with ornate classical drapery, depicted in the neoclassical engraving style typical of lottery trade cards from this period. The document appears to be page 4 of a 45-page collection of lottery advertisement broadsides dated ca. 1800-1826.

---

# ALGIERS

Lottery begins January 21, 1817.
Two Grand Prizes of 20,000 Guineas;
And FORTY other Capitals!
All in Sterling Money!—No Stock Prizes!

MOHAMMED STABDALLA
BLOODHOUNDO ALI CUT-THROATO.

"By Mahomet's beard I swear,
"Of that Cake I'll have my share;
"Share?—I mean I'll have it all!
"All your Shares, both large and small!
"If this modest claim should fail me,
"By my whiskers! I'll impale ye!
"More than that—(my fury rises)—
"I'll seize on all your Lottery Prizes!"

---

**Notable markings:**

The document features a satirical engraving of a turbaned figure depicted as a caricature, standing before a harbor with sailing vessels. The figure is drawn in a grotesque style typical of early 19th-century satirical lottery advertisements. The entire piece is framed in a double-lined rectangular border.

---

# TIMOTHY TANDEM

In tandem or curricle, drive as you will,
You cannot drive well, lest your pockets you fill;
'Tis money alone sets you four-in-hand going,
'Tis money that makes you both witty and knowing.
Tim. Tandem, 'tis known, tho' he merrily grins,
Seldom laughs with a grace, unless when he wins;
Yet, of dame Fortune, he e'er wants a lift,
He treats his dear self with a Lottery gift:
Which never yet fail'd to insure him a tin,
And produce him some work for the smack of his whip,
Which doubtless next January, the *eighteenth*, he'll try;
And likewise all friends, known to you and to I.

The Lottery will be drawn 18th & 26th This Month Jan.

---

**Notable markings:** 
- Handwritten notation in margin (upper left): "b 1046" [or similar]
- Engraved illustration depicting a man in period dress (early 19th century) driving a tandem carriage, wearing a wide-brimmed hat and decorative coat, holding reins

---

# Transcription

LINGO.—Agreeable Surprise.

"I'm here, Domine Felix."

___

Your learning, good Lingo, is vast and profound—
But if you'd be thought really wise,
Court Fortune, whose gifts, when her wheel shall go round,
May prove an "Agreeable Surprise."

___

The Lottery will be drawn 14th THIS MONTH,
(January) and following Day,

---

**Notable markings:**

- Handwritten text in upper right corner (appears to read "Domine [Felix?]" or similar - partially legible)
- Blue-grey mounting board surrounding the cream-colored trade card
- Engraved illustration showing a gentleman in 18th-century dress (coat, breeches, stockings) in a gesturing pose
- Document appears aged with foxing and staining consistent with early 19th-century paper

---

# Transcription

**CELESTINA QUAVERSIDES.**

This damsel, whose strains are me'odious and clear,
The Syrens' true notes may repeat thro' the year,
May please like sweet Philomel, tuned to delight,
And add a fresh charm to the joys of Twelfth-Night.
But Music's vibrations are sounds that expire,—
More permanent blessings are those I admire;
While from the State Lottery bright gold we can have,
And Land from the City,—'tis those that I crave.

---

## Notable markings:

- Archival reference number in upper left corner: **610594**
- Engraved illustration of a woman in classical dress holding a stringed instrument, standing beside a Gothic-style tower or building structure

---

# Transcription

**FARMER CUT-CLOSE.**

THE jolly Farmer lends his willing hand,
By his industry to enrich our land;
Equally willing is he to regale
At Christmas, or Twelfth-Day, with cake and ale;
He tries the Lottery, for it's free to all,
And gains Five Thousand, though his risk was small;
Sells off his stock, enabled to retire
At ease, an independent country squire.

---

**Notable markings:**

A reference number "[1043]" appears in the bottom right corner of the document.

---

# Transcription

**BETSY BUXOM.**

When honest Jack is forc'd from home,
To quit his Bet, abroad to roam;
What are the pangs that are not felt;
Their souls united almost melt:
But sorrows—hence—far hence from me,
I sing of mirth and revelry.
"Laughter, holding both its sides,"
As the Prizes, Joy divides;
Bestow'd by Fortune's lib'ral hand,
At COOPER'S-HALL, in England's happy land!

• Lottery will be drawn 18th & 26th This Month Ju[ly]

---

**Notable markings:**
- Reference number in upper left corner: 616559
- Engraved illustration of a woman in Regency-era dress holding what appears to be a handkerchief, with decorative lacing detail on her gown
- The document is printed on aged paper with worn/damaged edges, consistent with early 19th-century lottery broadside advertisements

---

# Transcription

**VULCAN.**

WHO's this with his budget? 'Tis Vulcan—I'll
A wager he's come for a slice of Twelfth Cake:
He once forg'd the thunder-bolts hurl'd by great
Jove,
And marry'd the prostitute Goddess of Love;
Expos'd in a net, once with Mars did he link her,
Then knock'd up his shop, and now strolls as a
tinker;
Though Fortune he courts in the Lott'ry, they say,
And hopes for a Prize on next Valentine's Day.

---

**Notable markings:**
None visible (this appears to be a printed broadside/trade card from a collection of lottery advertisements, circa 1800-1826)

---

# Transcription

**LIEUTENANT CHEERLY.**

That the Sailor loves fighting we very well know,
But he seldom succeeds without striking a blow;
Yet the battles of Fortune are quietly won,
Without either bloodshed, or firing a gun;
And, with no other noise than the Thousand Pounds
calling,
Your Prizes you get without grappling or hauling;
Which, when they're obtain'd, with their cargoes
complete,
You may sail when you please with next LOTTERY
FLEET.

The Lottery will be drawn 18th & 26th This Month.

---

## Notable markings:

- Reference number in upper left corner: "616854"
- Engraved illustration of a sailor in military dress uniform holding aloft a ship's wheel or vessel, wearing a plumed hat and ornate coat
- Period typography and layout typical of early 19th-century lottery broadsides
- Document shows signs of age with worn edges and cream-colored paper stock

---

# Transcription

**CHARON.**

HERE's CHARON, the Ferryman, come for his fee !
Let's try if the surly old rogue will agree
To postpone my passage still longer, and take
A plate of roast beef, or a piece of Twelfth Cake ;
'Tis hard to depart from a table well spread,
O'er Stygian streams to be row'd with the dead !
More willing would I be to gain, if I stay,
A Prize in the Lott'ry next Valentine's Day.

---

## Notable markings:

The image shows an engraved illustration of Charon (the mythological ferryman) in 18th-century dress, holding a long pole/oar, standing in or near his boat. The figure wears a hat, ornate coat, and boots. The engraving style is characteristic of period lottery advertisement broadsides. No visible signatures, stamps, or serial numbers are present on this particular page.

---

# Transcription

**GERMANY.**

Lottery begins January 21, 1817,
Two Grand Prizes of 20,000 Guineas;
And FORTY other Capitals!
ALL IN STERLING MONEY!
NO STOCK PRIZES!

BARON von DONDER-DRONK SMOKE-OFF
BEHOLD, at Fortune's invitation,
A hero of the German nation;
He's smok'd you out, and to partake
Of British cheer and British cake,
Has left his native land to roam,
And gain a Prize in Britain's home,
To care and grief he bids defiance,
A Lottery Prize is his reliance.

---

**Notable markings:**

- Engraved illustration of a German gentleman in period dress holding a long pipe, wearing a coat with decorative buttons, a cap, and holding a walking stick
- Ornamental rectangular border frame around the illustration
- Text in red ink for the lottery announcement section
- Text in black ink for the poetic verse at bottom
- Archival reference marks visible in margins (appears to show collection numbering)

---

# Transcription

**POMONA.**

POMONA, the Goddess, now comes to display
Her fruits, on an apple-stall spread, this Twelfth Day:

The crops of her orchard most surely must fail,
When thus she's reduc'd to be trading retail:
Small profits from dealing are slender supplies,
To those which next Valentine's Day may arise
From Fortune's State Lott'ry; for then she intends
With Two Hundred Thousands to welcome her friends.

---

## Notable markings:

None visible (standard lottery advertisement broadside from ca. 1800-1826)

---

# Transcription

**HERCULES.**

HERE's HERCULES, fam'd for his wonderful feats,
Reduc'd to a pavior, now mending the streets;
He rams till he's faint; so, his spirits to cheer,
Because it's Twelfth Night, has a pot of good beer.
Poor dog! was he prudent to husband his mite,
How soon might he be in a much better plight!
The Lott'ry, which draws on next Valentine's Day,
Maymake the rogue happy without more delay.

---

**Notable markings:**

None visible (this appears to be a lottery advertisement broadside/trade card from circa 1800-1826)

---

# Transcription

**QUEEN.**

The lass who holds this Ticket, we declare,
Our Twelfth-Night Queen, and hail the happy Fair;
But she who gains a Twenty Thousand Prize
In the State Lott'ry, or whose chance shall rise
A noble Freehold from the City's Store,
May be content, should Fortune grant no more.

---

## Notable markings:

- Handwritten notation in upper left corner: "2166" (appears to be an archival reference number)
- Handwritten inscription at bottom of page (largely illegible but appears to be curator or collector notation)
- Ornamental decorative border frame around the Queen's portrait

---

# Transcription

**PANDORA.**

**MISCHIEVOUS PANDORA** will find her mistake,
If she comes expecting a piece of Twelfth Cake,
I like not the baggage; she may, if she pleases,
Withdraw from our treat with her box and diseases.
'Tis open'd, alas! and the plagues fly about,
While Hope lies behind, and is loth to come out.
Hope! sure I mistake, 'tis a Ticket I see,
A Prize in the Lottery—I hope it's for me.

---

## Notable markings:

None visible. This is an engraved broadside/trade card featuring an illustration of Pandora holding a "Lottery Ticket" box, with symbolic elements (daggers, skull, serpent, crown, coins) scattered below her figure representing the consequences of opening Pandora's Box. The document shows age-related discoloration and wear consistent with early 19th-century paper.

---

# Transcription

**Christmas Sport.**

"Who'll play To-Night at Blind-Man's buff?"
"I will," says JANE,—"but don't play rough:"
And I'll be Blind-Man first," says NED—
"So stand now clear,—mind how you tread;
"And if a danger I come near,
"Cry out—Roast Beef! Roast Beef! for fear."

[ENGRAVING: Classical allegorical figure of Dame Fortune seated in a carriage, blindfolded, with attendant figures]

Tho' Dame Fortune is blind, yet her favors she shows
To her friends all alike, and no difference knows;
So that all who would gain them this Lottery, they must
Apply (or they'll lose them) 'fore JAN. TWENTY-FIRST.

---

**Notable markings:**
- Reference number visible in upper left margin: "1579"
- Horizontal rule dividers separating sections
- Engraved illustration in classical style depicting Dame Fortune

---

# Transcription

**ANGER.**
*The Rubber lost by a Revoke.*

[Engraving of Lady Pennypoint standing beside a table with two lit candles]

**LADY PENNYPOINT.**

With a *knave* I was caught, and put quite to the blush,
All *honours* were *play'd*, yet of hearts I'd a *flush*,
But my partner, poor deal, was as cross as Old Nick,
And 'twas by his *revoking* he lost the odd trick.
If my lead he'd return'd, and left shuffling alone,
I'd have *won* the first point, and the *game* was our own.
My only resource to some Office to *pack*,
Take a *rubber* with Fortune, on *loo* turn my back.

Lottery begins 21st This Month (Jan.)—2 of 20,000 Guineas, and
40 other Capitals—all Sterling Money (no Stock Prizes.)

---

**Notable markings:**

- Handwritten signature or initials in upper left margin: "Yle" or similar
- Reference number in lower left: "3/1605"
- The document is printed on aged cream-colored paper with visible wear and foxing

# GREATNESS.

KING OF THE ROUND TABLE.

I'm King! and, as such, like to see
My subjects jolly, plump, and free:
I love to see their cheeks disclose
The lily mingling with the rose.
I'll strike the hag Oppression dumb--
I'll shew her my tremendous bomb!--
Thus gratified my warmest wishes,
The poor shall have the loaves and fishes--
Each Lottery Office will supply
Fresh droits from Fortune's Admiralty.

Lottery begins 21st This Month (Jan.)--2 of 20,000 Guineas, & 40 other Capitals--all Sterling Money (no Stock Prizes.)

---

**Notable markings:**

- Reference number in lower left corner: "y/1045"
- Engraved illustration of a corpulent crowned figure with ornate decorative elements
- Printed on aged cream-colored paper with visible wear and creasing

# Transcription

**RAPTURE.**
*A Member rehearsing his Speech.*

---

**SIR WILLIAM COURTEOUS.**

Hear him! hear him! Order! Order!
All the Court is in disorder!
I echo, Sir, the *Public voice*—
What i hold here's the *People's choice*!
A number's loss stood forth of late,
And SWIFT was chosen candidate
The loans of Fortune to supply
From the rich New Year's Lottery,
In Sterling Money—(*Hear him, hear him!*)
The *Ayes* have got it. (*Chair him, chair him!*)

---

Lottery begins 21st This Month (Jan.)—2 of 20,000 Guineas, and 49 other Capitals—all Sterling Money (no Stock Prizes.)

**POLICEMAN.—Hollah! What are you two boys talking about, eh? I think you are entering into a very wicked conspiracy to overturn the Government. I have just overheard ye talking about the Bank. Now I con't want you to say anything that will prejudice yourselves on your trial; at the same time I am willing to hear anything you have got to say in explanation of your conduct concerning your talk about the Bank. I have no doubt you meant the Bank of England.**

**FIRST BOY.— Bank of England! What be that? Whoy, it was that mud bank we were a talking on.**

**SECOND BOY.—To be sure it was. Don't you see, Mr. Policeman, it's a country bank,— and we were a-going to make a run upon it, by troving to jump through it!**
