title: French Royal Tontine Certificate, 10th Tontine, 1759 (Page 1 of 2)
description: This handwritten and printed tontine certificate is issued under the "10ème Tontine créée par Édit du mois de Décembre 1759" (10th Royal Tontine created by Edict of December 1759). The subscriber is "Jean Marie Joseph François Gueneau de Vauzette, Bourgeois de Paris," who has paid "Deux cent Livres" (200 livres) for a share, with the annuity contingent on the life of "Victoire Sophie de Noailles," described as sixty years old and widow of a member of the household of Alexandre de Bourbon, Comte de Toulouse. Annuities are to be paid twice yearly at the Hôtel des Marchands et Échevins (Merchants' and Aldermen's Hall) of Paris, as established by the Royal Edict and the regulations of the Ferme générale. Signed by Joseph Micault d'Harvelay, Conseiller du Roi and Garde du Trésor royal. Royal tontines were a major mechanism of French state borrowing throughout the 18th century, raising capital by pooling life annuities that increased as fellow subscribers died.

pages.description: Page 1 of 2 - Tontine, 1760. Part of the Pierre Brissot de Warville collection
Page 2 pf 2 - Tontine, 1760

creator: Joseph Micault d'Harvelay (Garde du Trésor royal)
notes: French Royal Tontine certificate, 10th Tontine (created by Royal Edict, December 1759). Subscriber: Jean Marie Joseph François Gueneau de Vauzette. Nominee: Victoire Sophie de Noailles. Registered at the Contrôle général des Finances, Paris, 22 September 1760.
transcription:
# French Royal Tontine Certificate - 10th Tontine, 1759

## Original (French):

N.° 02
8e CLASSE.
II. Subdivisions.

10me TONTINE criée par Edit du mois de Décembre 1759.

Je Joseph Micault d'Harvelay, Conseiller du Roi en ses Conseils, Garde de son Trésor royal, confesse avoir reçu comptant en cette ville de Paris, de

**Paul Marie Joseph François Gueneau de Paucelle** Bourgeois, à titre payié les Bacheliers ayant causé de la rente d'après l'Edit de Mad Marie Victoire Sophie De Noailles âgée de soixante trois ans aux laisser D'or d'annière de M.r Alexandre & Bourbon Comte de Coulange

**Douze cent Livres**
en louis d'or, d'argent & monnoie, pour le principal de [unclear] mille livres de rentes viagères, qui [unclear] seroient constituées au Profit des Marchands & Echevins de la ville de Paris, sur les trois millions de Rentes viagères dites Tontines, vendues & aliénées auxdits sieurs Prévôt des Marchands & Échevins, en conséquence de l'Edit du mois de Décembre 1759, reglées ou befoin à cet, lesdites rentes alliaguées tant sur la Ferme générale des Postes que sur les droits d'Aides & Gabelles, pour jouir par [unclear] Gueneau de Paucelle à raison d'une action dans la Tontaine

[The document continues with legal language regarding the division of the 8th Class of the 10th Tontine, with references to financial obligations and conditions.]

Quittance du Garde du Trésor royal. Exercice mil sept cent cinquante-neuf.

[Signature] Micault D'Harvelay 1760

M.r D'Hilly Notaire.

---

## English Translation:

**No. 02**
**8th CLASS**
**II. Subdivisions**

**10th Tontine issued by Edict of the month of December 1759.**

I, Joseph Micault d'Harvelay, Counselor to the King in his Councils, Guardian of his Royal Treasury, confess to have received in cash in this city of Paris, from

**Paul Marie Joseph François Gueneau de Paucelle**, Bourgeois, as payment for the annuities caused by the Edict of Madame Marie Victoire Sophie De Noailles, aged sixty-three years, paid in gold louis and currency, the sum of

**Twelve hundred Livres**
in gold louis, silver, and currency, for the principal of [unclear] thousand livres of life annuities, which would be constituted for the benefit of the Merchants and Aldermen of the city of Paris, on the three million livres of annuities called Tontines, sold and transferred to the aforementioned Messieurs Provost of the Merchants and Aldermen, in consequence of the Edict of the month of December 1759, registered or if necessary to date, the said annuities charged both on the General Farm of Posts and on the rights of Aides and Gabelles, to be enjoyed by [unclear] Gueneau de Paucelle by reason of one share in the Tontine...

[Continues with legal conditions and terms]

Receipt from the Guardian of the Royal Treasury. Year seventeen hundred fifty-nine.

[Signature] Micault D'Harvelay 1760

Mr. D'Hilly, Notary.

---

## Notable Markings:

- **Serial Number:** N.° 02
- **Class:** 8e CLASSE
- **Signatures:** "Micault D'Harvelay 1760" (official signature); "D'Hilly" (Notary signature)
- **Date:** December 1759 (issued); 1760 (signed)
- **Visible wear and aging marks** on the document
- **Official stamps and seal impressions** (partially visible)

title: United States Continental Lottery Subscriber List, Winchester, Virginia, 1777 (Page 1 of 3)
description: This handwritten manuscript is the first page of a United States Continental Lottery ticket subscriber list compiled in Winchester, Virginia, recording names, ticket quantities, and assigned lot numbers (prefixed "52m"). Subscribers include John Hanly (2 tickets), John Norvell (4), William C. Holliday (3), Albert Roberts (1), John Cox (3), Edward M. Quinn (3), John DuHeld (2), Benjamin Williams, Ann Brownson, Elizabeth McFarland, Col. Isaac Lake (10 tickets), and others—totaling at least 86 tickets carried over to the next page. The tickets were sold on behalf of Josiah Watson Esq. of Alexandria, Virginia, who distributed United States Lottery tickets to local agents in the Shenandoah Valley for sale to the public. This list is part of a small archive documenting how Continental Lottery ticket sales were organized through a decentralized network of agents during the Revolutionary War.

title: United States Continental Lottery Ticket Sales List, Robert Traill, Easton, 1778 (Page 1 of 2)
description: This handwritten manuscript is headed "United States Lottery, Class the Second, List of Tickets sold by Robert Traill of Easton." It records 25 lottery ticket numbers (prefixed "gom") and concludes: "25 Tickets, 20 Dollars each, 500 Dollars." Signed "Rob. Traill, Easton, Decmr. 27, 1778." The United States Continental Lottery was authorized by resolution of Congress on November 18, 1776 to help finance the Revolutionary War, and was organized in successive classes (First, Second, Third) with tickets priced at $20 each. Robert Traill operated as a local lottery agent in Easton, Pennsylvania, distributing and selling tickets on behalf of the lottery commissioners in Philadelphia. This list is evidence of how the Continental Lottery reached small-town markets throughout Pennsylvania through a network of named local agents.

title: United States Continental Lottery Ticket, Class the Third, No. 1A.m.655, 1776 (Page 1 of 2)
description: This printed lottery ticket reads: "United States Lottery No. 1A.m.655 – CLASS the THIRD. THIS TICKET entitles the Bearer to receive such PRIZE as may be drawn against its Number, according to a Resolution of CONGRESS, passed at Philadelphia, November 18, 1776." Signed by "Jo: Budden" with a class indicator "S." at lower left. The Continental Congress authorized this lottery—widely regarded as the first national lottery in American history—on November 18, 1776, to help finance the Revolutionary War. The lottery was organized in classes with tickets sold at $20 each, and administered by commissioners including James Searle, John Bayard, and Daniel Roberdeau of Philadelphia. Jo: Budden served as a lottery manager responsible for signing the tickets. A later collector annotation notes this ticket as "Very rare," reflecting the extreme scarcity of surviving Continental Lottery tickets.

title: Anglo-American Tontine Plan, 1789: Prospectus (Page 1 of 3)
description: This printed prospectus describes "A PLAN OF A TONTINE to consist of 1000 Shares of £.100 each, to be divided into Seven Classes, as follow; the Dividend of each Class to increase by Survivorship." Seven age classes range from lives under 10 (170 shares at £6 2s per £100) to lives above 60 (100 shares at £8 3s per £100), totaling capital of £6,679. Subscribers are to pay £50 per £100 subscribed by November 1789 to Messrs. Lockhart, Bankers, Pall-Mall, with the remaining 50% due January 1790 when each subscriber must give the name, age, and description of their nominee. To secure the annuities, the prospectus pledges $600,000 in United States government funds (equal to £135,000 sterling at 6% per annum) vested in the names of trustees Francis Baring, Edmund Boehm, and Thomas Henchman of London—plus £50,000 in Three per Cent Consolidated Annuities of Great Britain. This Anglo-American tontine, backed directly by US federal bonds, exemplifies the transatlantic financial connections between Britain and the newly independent United States formed immediately after the Revolutionary War.

transcription:
# Transcription

A PLAN OF A TONTINE to consist of 1000 Shares of £100 each, to be divided into SEVEN CLASSES, as follow; the Dividend of each Class to increase by Survivorship.

| | | £. s. | | £. |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| First | Class, on Lives under 10 Years of Age | 170 Shares | at 6 2 | for each 100 | 1037 |
| Second | Class, on Lives above 10 and under 20 Years | 170 Shares | at 6 4 | for each 100 | 1054 |
| Third | Class, on Lives above 20 and under 30 | 170 Shares | at 6 6 | for each 100 | 1071 |
| Fourth | Class, on Lives above 30 and under 40 | 140 Shares | at 6 10 | for each 100 | 910 |
| Fifth | Class, on Lives above 40 and under 50 | 150 Shares | at 7 0 | for each 100 | 910 |
| Sixth | Class, on Lives above 50 and under 60 | 120 Shares | at 7 7 | for each 100 | 882 |
| Seventh | Class, on Lives above 60 Years | 100 Shares | at 8 3 | for each 100 | 815 |
| | | 1000 Shares | | | £.6679 |

Every Subscriber to mention the Class in which he will subscribe, and to pay £.50 on every £.100 subscribed, into the Hands of the Treafers, Messrs. LOCKHART, Bankers, in Pall-Mall, on or before the First of November 1789; and the remaining 50 per Cent. to be paid on or before the First Day of January 1790, when the Name, Age, and Description of every Nominee shall be given, and the Deed of Trust executed.

The Annuities to be paid half yearly, at Messrs. LOCKHART's, the Treafers, to commence from the Time of Subscribing; and the First half-yearly Payment to be made on the Second Wednesday in February 1790, and the Second half-yearly Payment on the Second Wednesday in August following; and on the same Days in each ensuing Year.

To secure the regular Payment of the Annuities, Six HUNDRED THOUSAND DOLLARS, in the Funds of the United States of America, which, at the Par of Exchange, are equal to ONE HUNDRED AND THIRTY-FIVE THOUSAND POUNDS STERLING, bearing an Interest of Six per Cent. per Annum, will be vested in the Names of FRANCIS BARING, EDMUND BOEHM, and THOMAS HENCHMAN, Esquires, of London, in Trust, to receive the Interest on the fame, and apply it in Payment of the Annuities as aforesaid.

The aforesaid Funds to be legally vested in the said Trustees, and by them declared fo to be; and the Deed securing the Application of the Interest to the Payment of the Annuities, to be enrolled in one of his Majesty's Courts of Record at Westminster.

The Proprietors of the 600,000 Dollars will alfo place FIFTY THOUSAND POUNDS Capital Stock in the Three per Cent. Consolidated Annuities of Great-Britain, in the Names of the said Trustees,

---

**Notable markings:** None visible in this page (signatures, stamps, or seals not present on this portion of the document).

---

# Transcription

**Left Page (Printed Text):**

Trustees, in Discharge of the First Fourteen half-yearly Payments; and so much of the said £50,000 "Three per Cent." shall be held by them, half-yearly, and paid to the Treasurers, as will pay the Sum of £3339 10s. the Sum that will be then due to the Annuitants.

In case all or either of the several Classes should not be completed by the First Day of *January* 1790, each Subscription shall be returned, with Interest after the Rate of Four *per Cent. per annum,* from the Time of Payment paid, unless such Class or Classes can be modified to the Satisfaction of the Parties concerned.

In case any Dividend shall remain unclaimed for the Space of Three Years, Notice shall be three Times inserted in the *London Gazette,* that such Dividend remains unclaimed; and unless the Parties entitled to receive the said Dividend shall claim the same within Three Months after such Notice, the said Dividend shall become forfeited, together with all further Claim to, or Interest in, the said Annuity; and the same shall be divided among, and continue to, the Survivors belonging to the Class wherein such Life was, after deducting the Expenses attending such Notice.

The 600,000 Dollars are on the same Security as the Loans made by the United States in *Holland,* which bear an Interest of Five *per Cent.* only; and the Capital Stock of those Loans now falls, on the Exchange at *Amsterdam,* at 94 *per Cent.*

If the Capital Stock of the United States should be paid off, the Sum being placed at Interest at Five *per Cent.* will produce nearly the annual Sum of £7000 Sterling.

Subscribers not completing their Second Payments on or before the First Day of *January* 1790, will forfeit their first Subscription.

title: Paine, The Decline and Fall of the English System of Finance – Cover (Page 1 of 23)
description: This image shows the front cover wrapper of Thomas Paine's pamphlet "The Decline and Fall of the English System of Finance," printed in Philadelphia in 1796. The worn, water-stained paper cover bears the title in plain typography: "THE DECLINE AND FALL OF THE ENGLISH SYSTEM OF FINANCE." Library call numbers "W0.30731" and "3408" are written at the top, indicating a Yale University holding. Paine wrote this work in France in 1796 to argue that the English system of funded national debt and paper bank notes was on a mathematically calculable path to collapse—a prediction partially vindicated the following year when the Bank of England suspended cash payments in 1797.

# Transcription

THE

DECLINE AND FALL

OF THE

ENGLISH SYSTEM OF FINANCE.

By THOMAS PAINE,

AUTHOR OF COMMON SENSE, RIGHTS OF MAN,
AGE OF REASON, &C.

PHILADELPHIA:

PRINTED BY JOHN PAGE, No. 67 MULBERRY STREET
FOR BENJ. FRANKLIN BACHE, No. 112 HIGH STREET.

1796.

"On the verge, nay even in the gulph of bankruptcy."
*Debates in Parliament.*

---

NOTHING, they say, is more certain than death, and nothing more uncertain than the time of dying; yet we can always fix a period beyond which man cannot live, and within some moment of which he will die. We are enabled to do this, not by any spirit of prophecy, or foresight into the event, but by observation of what has happened in all cases of human or animal existence. If then any other subject, such for instance as a system of finance, exhibits in

A

---

**Notable markings:** 

The left page shows age-related staining and discoloration typical of 18th-century paper. No visible stamps, seals, or signatures are present on this opening.

---

# Transcription

**Left Page (Page 2):**

its progress a series of symptoms indicating decay, its final dissolution is certain, and the period of it can be calculated from the symptoms it exhibits.

Those who have hitherto written on the English system of finance (the funding system) have been uniformly impressed with the idea of its downfall happening *some time or other.* They took, however, no data for that opinion, but expressed it predicatively, or merely as opinion, from a conviction that the perpetual duration of such a system was a natural impossibility. It is in this manner that Doctor Price has spoken of it; and Smith in his Wealth of Nations has spoken in the same manner; that is, merely as opinion without data. "The progress, says Smith, of the enormous debts, which at present oppresses and will in the long run *may probably ruin,* all the great nations of Europe, (though it might make some suppositions carried with it no conviction.

It is not my intention to predict any thing but I will throw from data already known, from hypotheses and facts, which the English funding system has already exhibited to, that it will not continue to the end of Mr. Pitt's life, supposing him to live the the age of a man. How much sooner it may fall, I leave to others to predict.

Let financiers diversify systems of credit as they will, it is nevertheless true, that every token of credit is a system of paper money. Two experiments have already been had upon paper money's the only one in both these cafes the America the other in France. In both cases the whole capital was emitted, and that whole capital, which in America was called continental money, and in France assignats, appeared in circulation; the consequence of which was, that the quantity became

**Right Page (Page 3):**

enormous, and to disproportioned to the quantity of population, and to the quantity of objects upon which it could be employed, that the market, if I may so express it, was glutted with it, and the value of it fell. Between *five and fix* years determined the fate of those experiments. The fame fate would have happened to gold and silver; could gold and silver could only have been iffued in the fame abundant manner as paper had been, and confined within the country as paper always is; by having no depreciation out of it; or, to speak on a larger scale, the fame thing would happen in the world, could the world be glutted with gold and silver, as America and France have been with paper.

The English system differs from that of America and France in this one particular, that its capital is kept out of light; that is, it does not appear in circulation. Were the whole capital of the national debt, which at the time I write this is about four hundred million pounds sterling, to be emitted in assignats or bills, and that whole quantity put into circulation, as was done in America and France, those English assignats, or bills, would sink in value as those of America and France have done with said bills in a greater degree, because the quantity of their would be more disproportioned to the quantity of population in England, than was the cafe in America or the other two countries. A nominal pound sterling in such bills would not be worth one penny.

But though the English system, by thus keeping the capital out of light, is preferred from [an] early destruction, as in the cafe of America and France, it nevertheless approaches the fame fate, and will arrive at it with the fame certainty, though by a flower progress. The difference is altogether in the degree of speed by which the two systems approach their

**Notable markings:** 

Page numbers visible at top: (2) and (3). Library stamp visible on right page. Binding margin visible on left side of left page.

---

# Transcription

**Left Page (4)**

fate, which, to speak in round numbers, is as twenty is to one; that is, the English system, at the time of giving the capital instead of idling it, contained within itself a capacity of enduring twenty times longer than the systems adopted by America and France; and at the end of that time it would arrive at the time common grave, the Potter's field, of paper-money.

The datum I take for this proportion of twenty to one, is the difference between a capital and the interest at five per cent. Twenty times the interest at five per cent. is equal to the capital. The accumulation of paper money in England is in proportion to the accumulation of the interest upon every new loan, and therefore the prospect to dissolution is twenty times closer than if the capital were to be emitted and put into circulation immediately. Every twenty years in the English system is equal to one year in the French and American systems.

Having thus stated the duration of the two systems, that of funding upon interest, and that of emitting the whole capital without funding to be as twenty to one, I come to examine the symptoms of decay approaching to dissolution, that the English system has already exhibited, and to compare them with similar symptoms in the French and American systems.

The English funding system began one hundred years ago; in which time there has been six wars including the war that ended in 1697.

1. The war that ended, as I have just said, in 1697.
2. The war that began in 1702.
3. The war that began in 1739.
4. The war that began in 1756.
5. The American war that began in 1775.
6. The present war that began in 1793.

Perhaps it may be asked, that as governments or ministers proceeded by no ratio in making loans or incurring debts, as nobody intended any ratio, or thought of any, how does it happen that there is one? I answer that the ratio is founded in necessity, and I now go to explain what that necessity is.

It will always happen that the price of labour or of the produce of labour, be that produce what it may, will be in proportion to the quantity of mo­ney in a country, admitting things to take their na­tural course. It is said in England that the value of paper keeps equal with the value of gold and silver. But the case is not rightly stated; for the fact is, that the paper has *pulled down* the value of gold and silver to a level with itself. However disposed governments may be to wring money by taxes from the people, there is a limit to the practice established in the nature of things. That limit is the proportion between the quantity of money in a nation, be that quantity what it may, and the greatest quantity of taxes that can be raised upon it. People have other rules for money besides paying taxes; and it is only a proportional part of that money they can spare for taxes, as it is only a proportional part they can spare for house-rent, for clothings, or for any other particular use. proportions find out and establish themselves; and that with such exactness that if any one part exceeds its proportion, all the other parts feel it.

Before the invention of paper money (bank notes) there was no other money in the nation than gold and silver, and the greatest quantity of money that ever was raised in taxes, during that period, never exceeded a fourth part of the quantity of money in the nation. It was high taxing when it came to this point. The taxes in the time of William the third, never reached to four millions before the invention of paper; and the quantity of money in the nation at that time was estimated to be about fifteen millions. The same proportions established themselves in France. There was no paper-money in France before the present revolution, and the taxes were collected in gold and silver money. The highest quantity of taxes never exceeded twenty-five millions sterling; and the quantity of gold and silver money in the nation, at the same time, as stated by Mr. Necker from returns of coinage at the mints, in his treatise on the administration of the finances, was about ninety millions sterling. # SPEECH OF THOMAS PAINE.

As delivered in the Convention, July 7, 1795.
Wherein he alludes to the preceding Work.

ON the motion of Lanthenas, "That permission be granted to Thomas Paine, to deliver his sentiments on the Declaration of Rights and the Constitution, Thomas Paine attended; an opposition being made to the motion, one of the Secretaries, who stood by Mr. Paine, read his speech, of which the following is a literal translation:

CITIZENS,

The effects of a malignant fever, with which I was afflicted during a rigorous confinement in the Luxembourg, have thus long prevented me from attending at my post in the bosom of the Convention, and the magnitude of the subject under discussion, and no other consideration on earth, could induce me now to repair to my station.

A recurrence to the vicissitudes I have experienced, and the critical situations in which I have been placed in consequence of the French Revolution, will throw upon what I now propose to submit to the Convention, the most unequivocal proofs of my integrity, and the rectitude of those principles which have uniformly influenced my conduct.

In England I was proscribed for having vindicated the French Revolution, and I have suffered a rigorous imprisonment in France for having pursued a similar moit

C

mode of conduct. During the reign of terrorism, I was a close prisoner for eight-long months, and remained so above three months after the era of the 10th Thermidor. I ought, however, to state, that I was not persecuted by the people either of England or France. The proceedings in both countries were the effects of the despotism existing in their respective governments. But, even if my perfection had originated in the people at large, my principles and conduct would still have remained the same. Principles which are influenced and judged to the control of tyranny have not their foundation in the heart.

A few days ago I transmitted to you, by the ordinary mode of distribution, a short Treatise, entitled "Dissertation on the First Principles of Government." This little work I did intend to have dedicated to the people of Holland, who, about the time I began to write it, were determined to accomplish a Revolution in their Government,—rather than to the people of France, who had long before effected that glorious object. But there are, in the Constitution which is about to be ratified by the Convention, certain articles, and in the report which preceded it, certain points, to repugnant to reason, and incompatible with the true principles of liberty, as to render this Treatise, drawn up for another purpose, applicable to the present occasion, and under this impression I presumed to submit it to your consideration.

If there be faults in the Constitution, it were better to expose them now, than to abide the event of their mischievous tendency; for certain it is, that the plan of the Constitution which has been presented to you is not confilient with the grand object of the Revolution; nor congenial to the sentiments of the individuals who accomplished it.

To deprive half the people in a nation of their rights as citizens, is as easy matter in theory or on paper; but it is a moist dangerous experiment, and rarely practicable in the execution.

**Left Page (36)**

But the article of the Constitution to which I have just advertised proposes as the object of the tax, the public good, or in other words, the good of all, but a partial good, or the good only of a few; and the Constitution provides solely for the rights of this few, to the exclusion of the many.

The second article of the Declaration of Rights says:—

"The Rights of Man in society are Liberty, Equality, Security of his person and property."

But the article alluded to in the Constitution has a direct tendency to establish the converse of this position, inasmuch as the persons excluded by this provision can neither be laid to possess liberty, nor security against oppression. They are condemned justly to the caprice and tyranny of the rest.

The third article of the Declaration of Rights says:—

"Liberty consists in such acts of volition, as are not injurious to others."

But the article of the Constitution, on which I have observed, breaks down this barrier. In enabling the liberty of one part of society to destroy the freedom of the other.

Having thus pointed out the inconsistency of this article to the Declaration of Rights, I shall proceed to comment on that part of the same article which makes a direct contribution a necessary qualification to the right of citizenship.

A modern document on the object of public revenue has divided the taxes or contributions into two duties, the direct and the indirect, without being able to define precisely the distinction, or difference between them, because the effect of both is the same.

Those are designated indirect taxes which fall upon the consumers of certain articles, on which the tax is imposed, because the tax being included in the price, the consumer pays it without taking notice of it.

The same observation is applicable to the territorial tax. The land proprietors, in order to reimburse them-

**Right Page (37)**

selves, will rack-rent their tenants: the farmer, of course, will transfer the obligation to the miller, by enhancing the price of grain; the miller to the baker, by increasing the price of flour; and the baker to the consumer, by raising the price of bread. The territorial tax, therefore, though called *direct*, is in its consequence *indirect*.

To this tax the land proprietor contributes only in proportion to the quantity of bread and other provisions that are consumed in his own family. The defecit is furnished by the great mass of the community, which comprehends every individual of the nation.

From the logical distinction between the direct and indirect taxation, some emolument may result. I allow, to auditors of public accounts, &c. but to the people a large capital in their respective pursuits, can ever, according to this system, attain the pre-eminence of a citizen. On the other hand, any upstart, who has by speculation or management, got possession of a few acres of land, or a miserable tenement, may exultingly exercise the function of a citizen, although perhaps he neither possesses a hundredth part of the worth of property of a simple mechanic, nor contributes in any proportion to the exigencies of the state.

I shall now proceed to the second article of the fame title, with which I shall conclude my remarks.

The second article says, "Every French soldier, who shall have served one or more campaigns in the cause of liberty, is deemed a citizen of the Republic, without any respect or reference to other qualifications."

It would seem, that in this Article, the Committee were desirous of extricating themselves from a dilemma into which they had been plunged by the preceding article. When men depart from an established principle, they are compelled to resort to trick and subterfuge, always shifting their means to preserve the unity of their objects; and as it rarely happens that the first expedient makes amends for the profusion of principle, they must call in aid a second of a more flagrant nature to supply the deficiency of the former.

In this manner legislators go on accumulating error upon error, and article upon article, until the mast becomes to bulky and incongruous, and their embarrassment to desperate, that they are compelled, as their last expedient, to resort to the very principle they had adopted.—The Committee were precisely in this predicament, when they framed this article; and to now I confess, their conduct appears specious rather than efficacious.

It was not for himself alone, but for his family, that the French citizen, at the dawn of the revolution (for then indeed every man was considered a citizen) marched soldier-like to the frontiers, and repelled a foreign invasion. title: Jacob Henriques: Proposed Lottery Scheme, London, April 1753
description: This printed broadside by Jacob Henriques of London, dated April 1753, proposes a new lottery scheme of 100,000 tickets at four pounds each, to be drawn by quadruple numbers so that 25,000 numbers will determine the whole lottery. The prize scheme offers: 4 prizes of £10,000, 8 of £5,000, 40 of £1,000, 130 of £500, 340 of £100, 800 of £50, 2,000 of £20, and 12,000 of £10, totaling 15,272 prizes of £394,000 with 84,728 blanks (a ratio of only five and a half blanks per prize). An additional £6,000 is distributed to the first and last drawn tickets. Twelve per cent is to be deducted from prizes for the intended purpose and paid either in money or in annuities at 3 per cent. Henriques argues that "My Proposal for lessening the national Debt is real Good, and my Intention for it is still better; and if the Parliament and Government are pleased for the Sake of their Community to encourage me with their prudent public Approbation, I will find out other great Matters for the Glory, Honour, and Welfare of his excellent Majesty." Beinecke Library call number annotations "65.802" and "cw" appear at top right.
creator: Jacob Henriques
notes: Jacob Henriques, proposed lottery scheme broadside, London, April 1753. 100,000 tickets at £4 each.
transcription:
# Transcription

THO' I have already taken the Trouble and Pains to fettle the following Lottery Scheme of four Pound each Ticket, that they may have a Choice either for the prefent or the future yet I conceive the fame will be a Detriment to many People ; whereas my publifhed Guinea Lottery Scheme delivered laft Week, can hurt none, but will pleafe every body ; and though there are nine Blanks to a Prize, thofe who will take four Tickets will make it hard only to one and half Blanks a Prize ; befides, that to have one Ticket of four Pound, or four at one Guinea each, is four Chances to one ; and with four Guineas they have the Chance to 4000l. and be a prudent Pattern to fhew the Probability of my publifhed great Propofal for this Nations Welfare and Happinefs, which I have much at heart, &c.

SCHEME

100,000 Tickets (either more or lefs in equal Proportion) at four Pound each Ticket to depofit one Pound, and one Pound every Month afterwards, is 400,000l.

Thofe 100,000 Tickets to be drawn by quadruple Numbers, with quadruple Prizes, fo that 25000 Numbers will determine the whole Lottery, and fave Time, Trouble and Expence.

4 Prizes of     10000 each, is     40000
8               5000              40000
40              1000              40000
80              500               40000
340             100               34000
800             50                40000
2000            20                40000
12000           10                120000

15272 Prizes                amounts to £ 394000
84728 Blanks (is only five and half Blanks to a Prize)

100000 Tickets

For the firft Drawn at 500l. 2000
For the laft Drawn at 1000l. 4000

Amounts to £ 400000

Note, From the Prizes are to be deducted 12 per Cent. for the intended Purpofe, and to be paid either in Money, or in Annuities of 3 per Cent.

My Propofal for leffening the national Debt is real Good, and my Intention for it is ftill better, and if the Parliament and Government are pleafed for the Sake of their Community to encourage me with their prudent public Approbation, I will find out other great Matters for the Glory, Honour, and Welfare of his excellent Majefty and this Kingdom, and which is my chief Intention, moft earneft Defire, and humbly pray for it, &c.

London, April 1753.                JACOB HENRIQUES.

---

## Notable markings:

- Call number annotations in top right corner: "65.802" and "cw"
- Printed broadside format
- Signature: "JACOB HENRIQUES" at bottom right

title: Ancient Papyrus: Greek or Coptic Cursive Financial or Legal Document (Ptolemaic–Byzantine Egypt, ca. 1st–7th century CE)
description: This fragment of an ancient papyrus bears cursive script in Greek or Coptic, consistent with documents produced in Egypt during the Ptolemaic, Roman, or Byzantine period (ca. 3rd century BCE to 7th century CE). The text appears to be a financial or administrative document—possibly a tax receipt, grain account, loan agreement, or land lease—of the type commonly produced in pharaonic and Hellenistic administrative practice. Papyri of this kind constitute the oldest surviving form of financial documentation in the Western world and are among the earliest physical evidence of organized credit, taxation, and contractual obligation. This is likely the oldest item in the collection.
creator: Unknown (Egyptian administrative scribal office)
notes: Ancient papyrus fragment with cursive Greek or Coptic script. Likely a financial or administrative document (tax receipt, grain account, or land lease). Ptolemaic, Roman, or Byzantine Egypt, ca. 1st–7th century CE. Oldest item in the collection.

title: New York Central Rail Road Company: $1,000 Bond at 6% (No. 1890, August 1858) [Cancelled]
description: This engraved bearer bond of $1,000 at 6% annual interest was issued by the New York Central Rail Road Company. No. 1890. Dated August 1858. Stamped CANCELLED. The New York Central Rail Road was created in 1853 by the merger of several smaller railroads connecting Albany to Buffalo and was one of the flagship lines of the Cornelius Vanderbilt railroad empire. At 6% interest, this bond was issued during a period when American railroads were paying relatively high rates to attract capital. The CANCELLED stamp indicates this bond was redeemed or retired before the company reorganized into the New York Central and Hudson River Railroad in 1869.
creator: New York Central Rail Road Company
notes: New York Central Rail Road Company. $1,000 bond, 6%. No. 1890. August 1858. CANCELLED.
transcription:
# Transcription of New York Central Rail Road Company Bond

**UNITED STATES OF AMERICA.**

**No. 1890** | **STATE OF** | **NEW-YORK.**

**$1000.** | **$1000.**

---

## Main Text:

**The New-York Central Rail Road Company.**

*Hereby certify, that the person to whom this Certificate is issued, being a Stockholder in one or more of the several Railroad Companies authorized to be consolidated into one great Organization, under the Act forming the main road, is entitled for the consideration herein stipulated, to the sum of* **ONE THOUSAND DOLLARS**, *to be paid out of the revenues, arising from the said Road, and the maintaining their Head Office at the city of Thirty years in the... [text continues with bond terms], with interest from that day at the rate of **SIX per cent per annum**, payable semi-annually on the last day of May, and the first day of November in each year, in the manner of the mystive Interest Warrants hereby apportioned. Upon equal and similar being payable in the City of New York. This Certificate is only transferable on the books of the company upon surrender of this Certificate, and in written assignment, under the Act forming the consolidation of certain Rail Road Companies pursuant to the resolution, and in accordance with such proviso. The whole thereof to the...*

*In testimony whereof, this Certificate, in pursuance of the order of the Board of Directors of the said New-York Central Rail Road Company, and in behalf of the officers even signed by the President and Treasurer of the said Company, the first day of August, in the year eighteen hundred and fifty-eight.*

title: Spanish Colonial Government: Libramento (Payment Order) No. 444 – Lima, Peru, October 1816 [Empréstito Patriótico]
description: This handwritten document is a libramento (government payment order/draft) No. 444 issued by the Spanish colonial treasury in Lima, Peru, in October 1816. Related to the "Empréstito Patriótico" (Patriotic Loan), an emergency forced loan imposed by the colonial government on the population of Lima to finance the royalist war effort during the South American Wars of Independence. Son 500 pesos. The document specifies a remarkably high annual interest rate of 25%. An additional notation from 1817 attests to further processing or partial payment. At the time of issue, the Vice-royalty of Peru was one of the last remaining strongholds of Spanish colonial rule in South America; independence would come only in 1821. This document is a rare surviving financial instrument from the final phase of Spanish colonial governance in Peru.
creator: Spanish Colonial Treasury, Lima (Tesorería de Lima)
notes: Spanish colonial libramento No. 444. Lima, Peru, October 1816. Empréstito Patriótico (forced loan). 500 pesos at 25% annual interest. Additional notation from 1817.
transcription:
# Transcription

**Original (Spanish):**

Numero. 6441.—

En virtud de este Libramiento, los Ministros de Real Hacienda de las Cajas de [handwritten: Lima] pagarán á un Año de la fecha de su aceptacion á [handwritten: Doña María Basilia] ó al tendedor que lo presente con legítimo endoso, ó recibirán en pago de qualquier crédito activo de la Real Hacienda, sin exceptuar alguno por privilegiado que sea, la Cantidad de Quinientos pesos fuertes en plata doble de cordonazo, con que ha contribuido al empréstito patriótico, ó más veinte y cinco pesos de la misma moneda por razón del interés de cinco por ciento prometido, señalándose á mayor abundamiento por hipoteca especial de este pago los fondos y rendimientos de la Caja general de censos, que queda reunida al orario para este único objeto.

Lima [handwritten: Diez y Ocho de mil ochocientos dieciséis.]

[Signatures and handwritten notations]

Don 500 p

[Lower handwritten notation regarding payment in the Caja del R.l Tribunal de Cuentas, Lima, October 1816]

[Further notation dated 1817]

---

**English translation:**

Number. 6441.—

By virtue of this Payment Order, the Ministers of the Royal Treasury of the Treasury Offices of [handwritten: Lima] shall pay in one year from the date of its acceptance to [handwritten: Doña María Basilia] or to the bearer who presents it with legitimate endorsement, or shall receive in payment of any active credit of the Royal Treasury, without exempting any that may be privileged, the Sum of Five Hundred strong pesos in double cordobazo silver, with which [she] has contributed to the Patriotic Loan, or rather twenty-five pesos of the same currency by reason of the interest of five percent promised, designating for greater assurance as special mortgage of this payment the funds and revenues of the General Censos Fund, which is hereby consolidated with the [unclear] for this sole purpose.

Lima [handwritten: Eighteenth of eighteen hundred sixteen.]

Don 500 p

---

**Notable markings:**

- **Serial Number:** 6441
- **Royal Seal/Emblem:** Ornamental royal crest at top with crown and heraldic design
- **Decorative Border:** Elaborate engraved frame with flourishes on all sides
- **Amount Notation:** "Don 500 p" (500 pesos) in oval cartouche at bottom center
- **Multiple Signatures:** Several handwritten signatures and rubrics present
- **Handwritten Amendments:** Various dates and amounts added in manuscript, including a 1817 notation indicating subsequent processing
- **Authentication Marks:** Wax seals or impressions (indicated by circular marks at bottom)

title: Imperial Russian Government: Internal 5% Prize Loan (Билет Внутреннего 5% с Выигрышами Займа), 100 Rubles (Series 15871, No. 45, 1864)
description: This bilingual (Russian/German) printed bearer lottery bond of 100 rubles at 5% interest with prize draws (выигрыши) is from the Internal 5% Prize Loan of 1864. Russian title: Билетъ Внутреннего 5% с Выигрышами Займа. German title: "Obligation der Russischen 5% Inneren Anleihe mit Prämien-Verlösungen von 1864." Series 15871, No. 45. 1864. Prize loans (займы с выигрышами) combined regular interest payments with periodic lottery drawings offering large cash prizes (выигрыши), making them more attractive to small and medium investors than plain bonds. They were widely used by European governments in the nineteenth century. The bilingual German text indicates this domestic loan was also actively traded in German financial markets.
creator: Imperial Russian Government
notes: Imperial Russian Government Internal 5% Prize Loan 1864. 100 rubles. Series 15871, No. 45. Bilingual Russian/German. Lottery prize-bond format.
transcription:
# Russian Internal 5% Prize Loan Bond - 1864

## Original Text:

**Header (decorative border):**
ВЛАСТЬ СВЯТЕЙШАГО ПРАВИТЕЛЬСТВУЮЩАГО СѴНАТА ВЫИГРЫШНАГО ЗАЙМА

**Upper medallions:**
Серия 15871 | № СОРОК ПЯТЫЙ 45ⁿ

**Main title:**
БИЛЕТЪ
ВНУТРЕННИЯГО 5% СЪ ВЫИГРЫШАМИ ЗАЙМА,
разрѣшенное Высочайшимъ Указомъ отъ 19 Ноября 1864 г.

НА НАПИТАЛЪ ВЪ СТО РУБЛЕЙ.
ГОДОВОЙ ДОХОД ПЯТЬ РУБЛЕЙ.

**German parallel text:**
OBLIGATION DER RUSSISCHEN 5% INNEREN ANLEIHE
MIT PRÄMIEN-VERLÖSUNGEN VON 1864.
Šin Hundert Rubel.

**Body text (Russian):**
Издложение изъ Высочайшаго Указа, данного на имя Министра Финансовъ, 13 Ноября 1864 г.

§ 1. Займъ выпускается по Государственному долгу под названием Внутреннего пятипроцентнаго займа съ выигрышами, и соответственно смыслу сумма снимается по Государственному Казначейству Государственнаго Банка.

§ 2. Возложить на Государственное Казначейство отнести Государственному Банку сумму потребующую на платежи процентов, капитала и выигрышей по билетам сего займа.

**Body text (German):**
Ausszug aus dem Allerhoechsten Ukas an den Finanz-Minister von N.N. November 1864.

§ 1. Diese Anleihe ist unter der Benennung: innere 5% Anleihe mit Prämienziehungen aus das Reichsstaatlich Darleehen, und die derselben untergelegene Summe von der Schuld des Reichsstaates in Schuld-schreiben auszuschreiben.

§ 2. Die Habschaft hat beauftragt die Staatsbank die Summen zu verfolgen, welche in den jaehrilehen Zahlungen an Zinsen, Capital und Gewinnung der Obligationen dieser Anleihe erforderlich sind.

**Signature section:**
Управляющий Государственнымъ Банкомъ [signature]
Министръ Финансовъ [signature]
Казначей [signature]
Инспекторъ [signature]

**Date and serial information:**
45 | 1587 | 1864

## English Translation:

**Header:**
Authority of the Most Holy Governing Senate [on] the Prize Loan

**Main Title:**
BOND
OF THE INTERNAL 5% PRIZE LOAN,
Authorized by Imperial Decree of November 19, 1864

FOR A CAPITAL OF ONE HUNDRED RUBLES.
ANNUAL INCOME FIVE RUBLES.

**Translated body text:**
Extract from the Imperial Decree issued to the Minister of Finance on November 13, 1864

§ 1. The loan is issued for state debt under the name of the Internal Five-Percent Loan with Prize Drawings, and in accordance with the sense thereof, the sum is withdrawn from the State Treasury of the State Bank.

§ 2. Direct the State Treasury to remit to the State Bank the sum required for the payment of interest, capital, and prize winnings on bonds of this loan.

- **Decorative border** with repeating geometric patterns
- **Denomination "100"** repeated in corners
- **Bilingual format:** Russian (Cyrillic) and German text throughout
- **Bearer bond:** Printed as transferable financial instrument

title: Thomas C. Jenkins, Wholesale Dealer in Flour & Groceries: Invoice to Offutt & Son (Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, December 19, 1902)
description: This printed commercial invoice is from Thomas C. Jenkins, described as "The Largest Flour and Grocery House in the World," located at Nos. 508–522 Penn Avenue and Nos. 509–523 Liberty Street, Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, at the Foot of Fifth Avenue near the Pittsburgh Market. The invoice is dated December 19, 1902, and addressed to Offutt & Son. Items listed include tobacco products: "J.S. Toba[cco] 63" (36 units at $2.68) and "Island Navy [tobacco] 56" (36 units at $2.016). Note: "No Goods Sold Families. No Retail Store or any Connection with any Retail House in any Shape or Form. All Electric Line Cars run to or pass near the New Building." SHIPPED DIRECT stamp. Jenkins' establishment handled flour, groceries, teas, roast coffees, and tobaccos on a wholesale basis, and was a major commercial presence in late Gilded Age Pittsburgh.

title: Holladay Overland Mail and Express Company: Blank Check, Virginia City, Montana Territory (No. 224S, ca. 1866)
description: A blank (unused) check No. 224S from the Holladay Overland Mail and Express Company, drawn at Virginia City, Montana Territory. Payable to order in Dollars, signed by John Russell & Co., William, N.Y., acting as Treasurer and Agent of the Holladay Overland Mail and Express Company. The check features an engraved vignette of a frontier scene and a stagecoach. Ben Holladay's Overland Mail and Express Company was the dominant stagecoach and mail carrier in the American West in the 1860s, holding government mail contracts across transcontinental routes before Holladay sold the enterprise to Wells, Fargo & Co. in 1866. Unused financial paper from frontier-era western transportation finance.
creator: Holladay Overland Mail and Express Company
notes: Blank check No. 224S. Holladay Overland Mail and Express Company. Virginia City, Montana Territory. Signed by John Russell & Co., William, N.Y., Treasurer. Ben Holladay's stagecoach enterprise; sold to Wells Fargo 1866.

title: Willem, Prince of Orange-Nassau: Archaic Dutch Princely Bond / Obligation (Netherlands, 17th Century)
description: A printed archaic Dutch-language bond or financial obligation beginning "Y VVILHELM: BY DER GRATIE GODES, Prince van [Vernorde-der-dijck]..." referencing the Prince of Orange-Nassau and financial obligations associated with his treasury (Tresorier General and Rentmeester). The document references the name "FREDERICUS HENRICX, Prince van Oraenien" (Frederick Henry, Prince of Orange, 1584–1647) and appears to concern a princely debt obligation, potentially related to the fiscal administration of the Orange-Nassau domains. The archaic spelling and typography suggest a mid-17th century document. This is a rare and historically important early modern sovereign financial instrument from the Dutch Golden Age.
creator: Prince of Orange-Nassau (Willem / Frederick Henry)
notes: Archaic Dutch princely bond/obligation. "Y VVILHELM BY DER GRATIE GODES, Prince van [?]..." References FREDERICUS HENRICX (Frederick Henry), Prince of Orange. Tresorier General and Rentmeester noted. Mid-17th century Dutch Golden Age fiscal document.
transcription:
# Transcription

**Original (Archaic Dutch):**

Y VVILHELM; BY DER GRATIE GODES, Printze van Oraingnen, &c. Stadtholder, mitgaders die Raeden uyt den naem van de Heeren Staten des Vorltendoms Gelre ende Graeffschapszutphen, Doen condt in crachtc deses, Alfoo 't den wel gedachten Heeren Staeten besieft heelt te revolueeren op den Landstach in April. 1644. binnen de Stadt Zutphen gehouden, bij dadius ernde wiltoude notice Horde Representaten ende Hooghgebooreri Vorst ende Heere FREDERIJCK HENRIJCK, Printze van Oraingnen, &c. Hooghilingdeste memoiris, in leven mede Stadtholder, Heer Vader van ons Stadtholder, gedaert den 5. vande voornoemste Maert Aprils, datmen tot meesten wellantende reddingihe van tien voorze: Vorstendtom ende Graelschap bij jegenwooordige gelegenheyt, doeh lichten ende op Intereele negotieteren is decstecke sommen van penningen, om bekesede geène plooyel te woorden, soo begaunce vau der Landdschap noodirijche [schulden, die laoten, nacnder verhaclt by de voovle: uytspraccke, ende tic voornm le Resolutie daer op gevolght vande wel gemelde Heeren Staten. Soo beken wij in krachte defs dat [unclear] op huyden dato onderschieven in goeden gauckbaren gelde [unclear] Summe van [unclear] sijnde uyt dier voorn [unclear] twintig luyfers Stuck. Ende willemde mits dien de voorn: [unclear] erven, ofte Actie van hebbende tot

[*Second paragraph continues with similar archaic Dutch text regarding financial obligations, interest rates, and conditions of repayment*]

Willende dat delen onsen brief, Vidimus ofte Copie authentijcq, mitgaders alle gnatie op eenigh Comptoir des voornoemden Vorltendoms ende Graeffschap van ons, voor deselve reyle met quítantie, ende dat na quitantie allenlijck, soo dícslls als 't van noodis is [unclear] gedienden sal, welcke beleenigden, Lieuetennlter generale [unclear] Vorltendom ende Graeffschaps, ofte dien fulcx anders te iallen geleyst sal mogén weelen, in uytgaue sonder [swarigheyt] al worden geleelen; Alles sonder argh of lijst; In oorkundé hebben wij 't groote Segel gedachtes Vorltendoms en de Graeffschaps onder jegenwoardt ze verschryvinse laere hangen, ende deelve door des Hoves Guiffiet doen beteckenacn. Gegeven Arnhem den [date] [unclear]

title: Wy Gustavus, Koning van Sweeden: Royal Loan Bond, 400,000 Rijksdaalders Hollandsch Courant (Amsterdam, February 9, 1782)
description: A printed royal Swedish government bond beginning "Wy GUSTAVUS, door Gods Genade Koning van Sweeden, der Gothen en Wenden &c. &c. &c., Erfheer van Noorwegen, Hertog van Schleswyck, Hollstein, Stornarn en Ditmarschen, Grave van Oldenburg, Delmenhorst &c. &c." Issued in Amsterdam on February 9, 1782. The bond is for Vierhonderd Duizend Rijksdaalders Hollandsch Courant Geld (400,000 Rijksdaalers Dutch currency), raised through the Amsterdam money market via Johannes Verge [?] as agent and translator. The document sets out the terms of the Swedish royal loan, interest payments, and the security provided by the Swedish Crown. King Gustav III of Sweden (reigned 1771–1792) frequently borrowed in the Amsterdam capital market to finance his ambitious domestic and foreign policies, including the Russo-Swedish War of 1788–1790.
creator: King Gustav III of Sweden
notes: Swedish royal loan bond, King Gustav III. 400,000 Rijksdaalders Hollandsch Courant. Amsterdam, February 9, 1782. Issued through Amsterdam money market. Johannes Verge, agent/translator. Gustav III borrowed extensively in Amsterdam to finance Swedish policy.
transcription:
# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Wy GUSTAVUS, door Gods Genade Koning van Zweeden, der Gothen en Wenden &c. &c. &c., Erfheer van Noorwegen, Hertog van Schleswyck, Hollstein, Stornarn en Ditmarschen, Grave van Oldenburg, Delmenhorst &c. &c.

Doen te weeten: Dat wy, goedsgevonden liebbende ten welzyn van onze Staten een Leening te doen van Twee Millioennen Gouldens, of achter maal honderd Duizend Ryksdalders Hollandsch Courant Geld, geeevende tot zeekerhood alle de Effecten en Revenuen van de Kroon, welke niet reeds zyn verpyptheked en Speciaal en Speciaal Hypotheken van onze Douanen en Tolien, aan de Heeren Hope en Compagnie en Fizzaux, Grand en Compagnie, te Amsterdam, laet gegeven hebben en dezelve uit krachte van deze tegenwoordige, Volkomen gegeven om voor ons en ons Konigryk dezelve Leening te protesteren.

En vermits wy van bovengemeelde Heeren tot ons volkomen genoegen en ten behoeve van ons Koninge, onvermijdelijk hebben eene Somma van Acht maal Honderd Duizend Ryksdalders Hollandsch Courant geld, om aan one te worden geleend, tegen Een Interest van vier op een half ten Honderd Jaarlykx, en voor den tyd gelijk bepaald in de vier Obligatien van outs Staats Comptoir, mitsgader groot Tweemal Honderd Duizend Ryksdalders Hollandsch Courant geld, aestalpsed by de voorzeiide Heeren als Speciale Hypotheek van diezse Leening, welke Somma van Achtmaal Honderd Duizend Ryksdalders Hollandsch Courant geld, aan ons ons Konigryk deduijdig is geleourneerd geworden.

Zoo beschlieten wy ons, voor ons en onze Erfgenaamen en Opvolgers in het Koningryk Zweeden, om niet alleen aan opgemeide Heeren te Amsterdam, allie zes Maanden te doen betaalen de Interesten van Honderdachtig Speciaal en Speciaal Hypotheken van onze Achtmaal Honderd Duizend Ryksdalders Hollandsch Courant geld, tegen vier en een half ten Honderd Jaarlykx, maar ook het geheele Capitaal op de vervaldy der deselve binnen Twaalf Jaren zyn en bekomen en der Eersten December aanstaande, te doen reimbourseeren, ten zy niet gezamentlyk overeesteentiming mogte worden goedsgevonden, den Termyn van het Zwelf Reimbourseement te prolongeeren, in welke laast geval zulks zes Maanden na vorren zal worden geclareerd; ons echter als Faculteits voorschuldende op deze Leening naar verloop van de Eerste Jil-jaarlyksch geslachts de doen reimbourseeren.

Tellens peranderen wy mits deze aan opgemeide Heeren, om de voorgemeide Hooftsomma in Oost-indien met inbcoe Commercie te verdeelen, en dezelve onder hunne Handelsbreng aan wien zy zullen goedsviniden te vergoedereeu, mits deselve Obligation door eene bepecijd Openbaar Notaris geheel geclareerd is, zondige dezelve in zolanig geval van dezelve kraft en van arde zon dese tegenwoorrige Hoofd Obligatie, zodatnde, dat aan de Eigenars of Honders van dezelve Obligatien van zes les Maanden en de Comptounie wij bovengemeilde Heeren zal worden betaald de Interest tegen vier en een half en Honderd Jaarlykx, en by expiratie van der vervaldy der Leening, het Capital zelve, ten waare der nog verguns zal Literaire Prolongatie moghe zyn overeeustcoming. Alles zonder eenige korting.

De zekerheid van het Reimboursfement dezer Geldleening, zo wel ten opzichte van het Capitaal als de Interest, verhinden en verlyphothequeeren wy alle de Effecten en Revenuen van ons Kroon, vry en ontlangen van alle verhypothekeerde, van wat naam en Hoofdligheid deselve ook mogen zyn en Speciale Inkonsten van onze Douanen en Tolien, en dart ook de Effecten van Honders van deselve Obligatien van ons Staats Comptoir, heden groot Tweemal Honderd Duizend Ryksdalders, Hollandsch Courant geld, Authoriteride die meestgeslede Heeren, hunne Eigenamen en Opvolgers in hun Interest op Plaats en Termyn voorschreeven, als dan direct de iemaidzul besluiting van het Capitaal der Interest op Plaats en Termyn vastgestesl en vergoedgeslesd tot de Cozurende van het gunt hen verzehijdinge zal zyn.

Na dat het Capitaal en de Interest van ons tegenwoorige Hooftsobligation door een goedheel zullen zyn Geremboursseert, zo zal deselve Obligation van ons Staats Comptoir voor dezid Leening Speciaal en Speciaal verpythothequeert, aan ons en onze Kroon, vry en ontlangen van alle verbintenisse nog gemijdt, moeten word te rug gegeven, en alle geente meestgeladhe Heeren zullen nodig vinàen by deze der Leening Negotiatie, het beste in voordsche vond Koningryk, overeeenkomltig onze hier voren uitgeveelde Intentie, te reglulieren.

Ten Ofkondes heaervan, hebben wij dezen aan opgemeide Heeren Authorities der Obligation gemaijljk heeten geteckend, en oorkonde gesteeld, zegul dan doen Appoloren.

Gedain op het Konigkl Griphold, dezen Twintighden Oktober des Jaars Een Duizend Zeven Honderd twee en Tachtig.

[Signature mark] GUSTAF. (L.S.)

(Lager was gctekend)
I. LILL. IENCRANTS.

Accordeerd met het Fransch Origineel geteekend No. 2155, de varietur.

Amsterdam den 9 February 1785.

JOHANNES VERGEEL Lic. Zk.
Beleidge Translateur.

---

## English Translation:

We GUSTAVUS, by the Grace of God King of Sweden, of the Goths and Wends, &c. &c. &c., Hereditary Lord of Norway, Duke of Schleswig, Holstein, Stormarn and Dithmarschen, Count of Oldenburg, Delmenhorst, &c. &c.

Know Ye: That we, having deemed it good for the welfare of our States to contract a Loan of Two Million Guilders, or in other terms Four Hundred Thousand Rijksdaalders in Dutch Currency, granting as security all the Effects and Revenues of the Crown, which are not already pledged and specially hypothecated, and specifically the Hypothecation of our Customs and Tolls, to the Messrs. Hope and Company and Fizzaux, Grand and Company, in Amsterdam, have given and do hereby give in the force of this present [instrument], fully given for us and our Kingdom to guarantee the same Loan.

And whereas we have received from the aforesaid Messrs. to our full satisfaction and for the benefit of our Kingdom, unavoidably a Sum of Eight Hundred Thousand Rijk

title: Potosian Land Grant: Reverse Side / Back of 400 Acre Certificate (London, ca. 1825)
description: The reverse side (back) of a Potosian Land Grant certificate for 400 Acres. The reverse shows the ornate printed header of the certificate featuring the POTOSIAN LAND GRANT title, a central coat of arms or heraldic vignette (400 Acres / 400 Acres), and handwritten endorsements or signatures at the bottom (including initials "RW," "AS," and a further signature "W.R. Ramos" or similar). The Potosian Land Grant certificates were issued by a British company in London ca. 1825 as part of speculative investment schemes in the Bolivian Potosí region following South American independence. This reverse side complements the front face of the certificate (see goetzmann0506), together comprising the complete land grant document.
creator: Potosian Land Grant Company (London)
notes: Potosian Land Grant, reverse/back side of 400-acre certificate. Shows printed header with coat of arms and handwritten endorsements. Complements front face (goetzmann0506). London, ca. 1825. British speculative investment in Bolivian Potosí region.

title: French Princes in Exile (Louis Stanislas Xavier & Charles Philippe): Obligation No. 34, f. 1,000 Dutch Guilders (Amsterdam, September 28, 1793)
description: A remarkable historical bond document issued under authority of the French Princes in exile, bearing the authorization of LOUIS STANISLAS XAVIER (the future King Louis XVIII) and CHARLES PHILIPPE (the Comte d'Artois, future King Charles X), countersigned by Le Maréchal, Duc de Broglie (from Hamm en Westphalie, signed on the twenty-fifth day of September 1793). No. 34. The Amsterdam house of J. Bourcourd and Wedowe F. Croese & Comp. certifies receipt, on behalf of Their Royal Highnesses the Princes of France, from an unnamed party, of the sum of EEN DUIZEND GULDENS Hollandsch Courant (f. 1,000 Dutch guilders) in exchange for a share in the stated Obligation, with corresponding coupons attached. Amsterdam, September 28, 1793. Notarized and authenticated. This bond was issued by the French royal family members during their exile following the execution of Louis XVI, as a means of raising funds in the Dutch capital market for the Royalist cause.
creator: Louis Stanislas Xavier (Louis XVIII); Charles Philippe (Comte d'Artois); Maréchal Duc de Broglie
notes: French émigré princes' bond No. 34, f. 1,000 Dutch Guilders. Authorized by Louis Stanislas Xavier (future Louis XVIII) and Charles Philippe (Comte d'Artois). Duc de Broglie, Hamm en Westphalie, September 25, 1793. Amsterdam, J. Bourcourd & Wedowe F. Croese & Comp., September 28, 1793. French Royalist exile finance.
transcription:
# Transcription

## Original Text (French and Dutch):

DONNÉ À HAMM EN WESTPHALIE (sous nos sceaux ordinaires & tius le contrefacing du Maréchal Duc de Broglie, Ministre affEtat, le vingt cinquième jour de Septembre l'an de Grace Mille fait cent quatrevingt treize, & du Regne du Roi le premier.

LOUIS STANISLAS XAVIER,
CHARLES PHILIPPE.

(L. S.)

LE MARECHAL, DUC DE BROGLIE.

---

N° 34

Wy ondergestechtevene I. BOURCOURD en Weduwe E: CROEESE & COMP., als daar toe, gelyck in vorenstaande Obligatie breeder staat gemeld, geauthhoriseert zynde, bekennen voor rekening van hunne Koninglycke Hoogheden de Prineen van Vrankryk, ontfangen te hebben uit handen van

de Somma van EEN DUIZEND GULDENS Hollands Courant voor Aandeel in dezelve Obligatie, en zyn de Coupons hiertoe behorende geteekend

*Actum Amsterdam, den 28. September; 1793.*

ƒ 1000:--:

Deze recontrafiaerd en ten Prohocolle aangetekend, belopende de door my getekende Aandeelen niet boven het getal van Twaaif Duyzend Nummers. En zyn de *Lettres Patentes* dato 7 September 1793, benevens de-Originele Obligatie van dato 25 September 1793 gedeponeerd en/ berustenде onder my.

Par ordre de leur Altesses Royales.

Garde du Tréfor Royal.

---

## English Translation:

GIVEN AT HAMM IN WESTPHALIA (under our ordinary seals & under the countersigning of the Marshal Duke of Broglie, Minister of State, on the twenty-fifth day of September in the year of Grace One Thousand Seven Hundred Ninety-three, & of the Reign of the King the first.

LOUIS STANISLAS XAVIER,
CHARLES PHILIPPE.

(L. S.)

THE MARSHAL, DUKE OF BROGLIE.

---

N° 34

We undersigned I. BOURCOURD and Widow E: CROESE & COMP., as thereto authorized, as appears stated more fully in the foregoing Obligation, hereby acknowledge receipt for the account of Their Royal Highnesses the Princes of France, from the hands of

the Sum of ONE THOUSAND GUILDERS Dutch Currency for a share in the said Obligation, and the Coupons belonging hereto are signed

*Done in Amsterdam, the 28th of September; 1793.*

ƒ 1000:--:

This is re-authenticated and registered in the Protocol, the number of Shares signed by me not exceeding the total of Twelve Thousand Numbers. And the *Letters Patent* dated 7 September 1793, together with the Original Obligation dated 25 September 1793 are deposited and rest in my custody.

By order of Their Royal Highnesses.

Keeper of the Royal Treasury.

---

## Notable Markings:

- Document number: **N° 34**
- Denomination: **ƒ 1000:--:** (1,000 Dutch Guilders)
- Date: **Amsterdam, 28 September 1793**
- Multiple handwritten signatures and notarial marks
- Circular library stamp (lower left corner)
- Handwritten annotations and endorsements throughout the document
- Official seal impression notation (L. S.) indicating "Locus Sigilli" (place of seal)

title: Ricardo & De Lara (Amsterdam): Forward Contract for British Consolidated Stock, £[?] (Amsterdam, May 13, 1805)
description: A handwritten forward sale contract (Dutch-language) arranged through Ricardo en De Lara, Warpenfort over het Wardhuis, No. 70, Amsterdam. The undersigned commits to sell a quantity of Pounds Sterling of the Consolidated Stock of Great Britain (British Consols / Geconsolideerde Schuld van [?] Ann [?]) at a set price, to be delivered at the Recontre (settlement date) in London at the agreed price. All deliveries and conditions are governed by the December 1803 Reglement referenced. Amsterdam, May 13, 1805. Signed by the counterparty. The Ricardo firm in Amsterdam (related to the famous Ricardo family including economist David Ricardo) was a leading broker in Anglo-Dutch financial transactions, facilitating the forward trading of British government bonds in Amsterdam — a major center for international consol dealing.
creator: Ricardo & De Lara (Amsterdam)
notes: Amsterdam forward contract for British Consolidated Stock (Consols). Ricardo en De Lara, No. 70 Amsterdam. Governed by December 1803 Reglement. Amsterdam, May 13, 1805. Ricardo family Amsterdam firm; Anglo-Dutch financial intermediation.
transcription:
# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Deze CONTRACTEN zyn te bekomen by RICARDO en DE LARA op J'et Wesperveld over het Wardhuis, N°. 70. Te Amsterdam.

Ondergetekende bekenne Verkogt te hebben aan [handwritten: A. L. Duviaux Dieu-Duzundt] — Ponden Sterlings Capitaal Actien van de Geconsolideerde [?] Ianns — van Engelandt, ten Pryze van [handwritten: Diegenen zynde] en [?] [unclear] — 50½ zynde Restanten van, de Undeling, uitgegeven in de Maand Januarij laats[til]. welken — 2000 — algemene, mits darentegen de Rekening te geven, op de Rescontre van [?] aankomende te laten Leveren, binnen Londen tot den gemelden Prys. En, zo middelerwyl eenige Uitzdelingen gegeven worden, zullen dezelven zyn ten profyte van den Koper, en tot afkorting van den bovengemelden prys, en in tegendeelal alle Bylagen en Inroepingen ten zynen laste, naar den gemeenen styl; alles op zodanige voorwaarden, als by het Reglement in de Maana December 1803 gearresteerd, fraan vermeld, waarnaar [?] Ondergetekende aanneeme zig te geaargagen, en voorts te goeder trouwwe.

Gedaan in Amsterdam den [handwritten: 13 Mei] A°. 1805

[Signature - illegible]

---

**English translation:**

These CONTRACTS can be obtained from RICARDO and DE LARA at J'et Wesperveld over het Wardhuis, No. 70, Amsterdam.

The undersigned acknowledges having sold to [handwritten: A. L. Duviaux Dieu-Duzundt] — Pounds Sterling Capital Stock of the Consolidated [?] Anns — of England, at the Price of [handwritten: various terms] — 50½ being Remainders of the Division, issued in the Month of January last[illeg.], which — 2000 — in general, on condition that the account be rendered, at the Rescontre of [?] to be delivered, within London at the aforesaid Price. And if, in the meantime, any distributions are issued, they shall be for the benefit of the Buyer, and in reduction of the abovementioned price, and conversely all Attachments and Claims shall be at his charge, according to common practice; all on such conditions as are fixed in the Regulation of December 1803, as mentioned, whereafter the Undersigned undertakes to conduct himself, and furthermore in good faith.

Done in Amsterdam on [handwritten: 13 May] A.D. 1805

[Signature]

---

**Notable markings:**
- Handwritten insertions throughout, particularly names and numerical amounts
- Signature at bottom (largely illegible)
- Date: May 13, 1805
- Reference to Ricardo en De Lara as brokers
- Address: Wesperveld over het Wardhuis, No. 70, Amsterdam

title: Conditien van Negotiatie, Daniel Changuion, Essequibo & Demerara, Article 1, f.400,000 at 6%, 1816
description: Printed conditions text (Article 1) of the negotiatie organized under the directorship of Daniel Changuion for planters in Rio Essequibo and Rio Demerara, to fund improvements to their plantations for a total of f.400,000 at 6% interest over 10 years. This page shows the body article text of the conditions, complementing the title page (see related document). Dated 1816.
creator: Daniel Changuion
notes: Plantation negotiatie for Essequibo and Demerara (now Guyana) planters; f.400,000 at 6% for 10 years; Article 1 of conditions text; see related title page
transcription:
# Transcription

**Original:**

Conditien van Negotiatie, tot
cen Fonds waar uit, onder Directie van
DANIEL CHANGUION, aan cenige
Planters in Rio Essequebo en Rio Demmerary, tot voorzetting en verbeete-
ring hunner Plantagien een Somma van
ƒ 400000:- voor 10 Jaaren zal werden
gefourneert, teegens den Intreft van 6
pC⁰. 's Jaars.

**ART. 1.**

De Planters in opgemelde Colonien, welke geneegen zyn eenige Gelden ter verbeete-ringe en voorzetting hunner Plantagien op te neemen, en daar toe door den Directeur zyn geaccepteerd, zullen gehouden zyn ten hunnen kosten, gemelde Plantagien door be-diedige Prisfeurs doorluyrig te laaten Inventariseeren, 200 te Grondstoe beboord, met uitsluitinge van Meubelen en andere Zaken, onnodig tot het be-werken van een Plantagie, en den volien en onbelesten Eygendom van dien Inventaris door hun beboeld, door gemelde Taxateurs doen in waarde brengten.

**2.**

Den Inventaris al hier voren bepaald, opgemaakt en geprijceert weesnde, zal aan Daniel Changuion, moeten werden gezonden, beneevens de behooflyke Hypotheeck of Verbandbrier, waar uit zal moeten blijken dat deselve Hypotheeck, ter Secretary van de Colonie behoorfyk gereustreerd is, en dat geen andere Speciale of Generale hypotheeck op deselve Effecten zyn leggenda, als miede dat geen verbauden... [illegible section]... in deeds.

**3.**

Aan de Planters welke den Inventaris en Verbandbriof als boven zullen overmaken, zal worden gelooten de helft of uyterlyck vyf agtde der waarde waar op derzelvér Plantagien, met hun toebehooren, zyn geteixed, teegens den Intreft van Ses pCento in 't jaar, die zy zig verbinden jaarlyks boven alle andere Lasten te voldoen, op Paene in de Verbandbrier vermeld.

**4.**

De Geld-opneemers, welke Jaarlyks met Valaable bewyzen kunnen aantconen, dat hunne bezittingen daar door zyn verbeeterdt, zullen vryheid hebben, op een nieuwe Prisfatie als boven genaeld te doen, van het meerder bedragen der waarde, nog op te neemen de helft van de verbeeteringe, mits dezelve aan voorn: Daniel Changuion mede Veryhpothequeerde.

**5.**

De Respective Planters Geld-opneemers, verbinden zig alle hunne zendende Producten te addresseeren aan, nodige Provitien te ontbieden van, en brieven te trecken op Daniel Changuion, als miede by afzending hunner Producten, aan hem in te gee boorflyke opgaave ter besorging der noodige afflurantie te geeven, met overzichte dat in gevoalle zy daar aan niet mogten voldoen of ook hunne Producten in de respective Colonien Verkoopen, of op andere wyze te divergere, en in ghebroken blijven jaarlyks hunne Intreft te voldoen, het by hun genoog Capital met de Intraffen direckteyк zal konnen worden opgeeyschr, lyk ten kolten van zodainge Planters, by Executie verhault, aan hunne Personen, Plantagien niet derselver toebebooren, en verdere zoo roerends als onroerende Goederen, zig hier toe voor de Hove van Justitie in de Colonie, of waar het anders zonde mogen verreycscht werden, by vrywillige Condemnatie verbindendc.

**6.**

De Geldgeeverz behoulen aan zig de vryheid, zodanige en zo veele Persoenen in de Rivieren van Essequebo en Demerary aan te fallen en te qualificeeren, als zy zul-

[Page continues with "A" at bottom]

---

# English translation:

**Conditions of Negotiation for a Fund under the Direction of DANIEL CHANGUION, for certain Planters in Rio Essequebo and Rio Demerara, for the continuation and improvement of their Plantations, a Sum of ƒ400,000 for 10 Years shall be furnished, at an Interest of 6% per annum.**

**ARTICLE 1.**

The Planters in the aforementioned Colonies, who are inclined to take up certain Monies for the improvement and continuation of their Plantations, and who are accepted for this purpose by the Director, shall be bound to cause said Plantations to be inventoried at their own cost by competent Appraisers, namely with respect to the Land, buildings, with the exclusion of Furniture and other Articles unnecessary to the working of a Plantation, and the full and unencumbered Ownership of such Inventory shall be brought to value by the aforementioned Assessors.

**2.**

The Inventory as determined above, having been drawn up and assessed, shall be sent to Daniel Changuion, together with the necessary Deed of Hypothecation, from which it must appear that such Hypothecation has been properly registered at the Secretary of the Colony, and that no other Special or General hypothecation on such Effects is in place [illegible section].

**3.**

To the Planters who shall submit the Inventory and Deed of Hypothecation as above, shall be loaned half, or at most five-eighths of the value upon which their Plantations, with their appurtenances, are assessed, at an Interest of Six percent per annum, which they bind themselves to pay yearly in addition to all other Charges, on penalty as mentioned in the Deed of Hypothecation.

**4.**

The Money-borrowers, who can annually prove by means of Valuable Certificates that their possessions have thereby been improved, shall have the liberty to have a new Appraisal made as above of the additional value, and to take up half of the improvement, provided the same be hypothecated to: Daniel Changuion.

**5.**

The Respective Planters Money-borrowers bind themselves to address all their produce to, offer necessary Provisions from, and draw Bills on Daniel Changuion; and at the time of sending their Produce, to furnish him with a proper account for the arrangement of the necessary supply, with the proviso that if they should fail therein, or should sell their Produce in the respective Colonies in any other manner, and should fall behind in paying their annual Interest, the Capital with the Interest may be immediately collected from their Persons, Plantations with all appurtenances, and all other moveable and immoveable Goods, before the Court of Justice in the Colony, or wherever it may be required, by voluntary Condemnation.

**6.**

The Money-lenders reserve to themselves the liberty to appoint such and as many Persons in the Rivers of Essequebo and Demerara and to qualify them as they...

---

- Handwritten notation in upper right corner: "1816" and "Bat: 10 Gr" with additional marks
- Archival stamp/notation visible in top right: "en mitzegcom" and "10 Conpositis"
- Document dated 1816
- Printed by standard typography of the period
- Page marked "A" at bottom (indicating continuation)

title: Gemeente Warnsveld, Bewijs van Aandeel in Municipal Loan f.12,000, f.250 at 4.5%, 1883
description: Dutch municipal bond share certificate (Bewijs van Aandeel) in a f.12,000 municipal loan of the Gemeente (Municipality) of Warnsveld (Gelderland province), bearing interest of 4.5% per annum. Face value f.250. The loan was authorized by resolution of the Municipal Council on September 16 and October 21, 1882, approved by the States of Gelderland on October 25, 1882, for a total loan amount of f.14,000. Redeemable by lottery over twelve years. Signed by the Burgemeester and Wethouder of Warnsveld. Dated April 30, 1883.
creator: Gemeente Warnsveld
notes: Municipal loan; f.12,000 total; f.250 denomination; 4.5% interest; lottery redemption; Warnsveld, Gelderland
transcription:
# ORIGINAL TEXT:

BEWIJS van AANDEEL

IN DE

GELDLEENING

groot f 12,000,

ten laste der Gemeente WARNSVELD,

vastgesteld bij besluiten van den Gemeenteraad van den 16 September 1882 en van den 21 October d.a.a., goedgekeurd door Heeren Afgeputeerde Staten van Gelderland bij besluit van den 25 October 1882, nr. 29 [or 39] voor het bedrag van hoogstens f 14,000.

BURGEMEESTER en WETHOUDERS der gemeente WARNSVELD verklaren hierbij aan toonder schuldig te zijn de somma van **Twee honderd vijftig Gulden, f 250,—** als aandeel in de bovengenoemde geldleening en zulks tegen eene rente van vier en een half ten honderd in het jaar, te voldoen op en tegen inlevering van bijgevoegde coupons.

De eerste twaalf jaren zal telken jare een aandeel worden uitgoloot, de daarop volgende tien jaren twee aandeelen en de overige jaren drie aandeelen, bij loting te voren aan te wijzen.

De uitgelote aandeelen zullen op den dertigsten April na de uitloting ten kantore van den gemeente-ontvanger worden uitbetaald, tegen intrekking van dit bewijs en de onverschenen interest-coupons.

Zullende de rente der uitgelote aandeelen niet langer loopen dan tot den Dertigsten April van het jaar waarin de uitloting heeft plaats gehad.

Gegeven te WARNSVELD den 30 April 1883.

Burgemeester en Wethouders voornoemd,

[Signatures]

Burgemeester.
Wethouder.

---

# ENGLISH TRANSLATION:

CERTIFICATE OF SHARE

IN THE

MUNICIPAL LOAN

amounting to f 12,000,

charged to the Municipality of WARNSVELD,

established by resolutions of the Municipal Council of September 16, 1882 and October 21 of the same year, approved by the Appointed Representatives of the States of Gelderland by resolution of October 25, 1882, no. 29 [or 39] for the amount of at most f 14,000.

BURGEMEESTER [Mayor] and WETHOUDERS [Aldermen] of the municipality of WARNSVELD hereby declare themselves indebted to the bearer in the amount of **Two hundred fifty Guilders, f 250,—** as a share in the aforementioned municipal loan and at an interest rate of four and one half percent per year, payable upon and against submission of the attached coupons.

During the first twelve years, one share shall be drawn by lot each year; during the following ten years, two shares; and during the remaining years, three shares, to be determined beforehand by lottery.

The drawn shares shall be paid at the office of the municipal receiver on the 30th of April following the drawing, against surrender of this certificate and the unsubmitted interest coupons.

The interest on the drawn shares shall cease to accrue as of the 30th of April of the year in which the drawing takes place.

Given at WARNSVELD on April 30, 1883.

Burgemeester and Wethouders aforementioned,

---

# NOTABLE MARKINGS:

- Two handwritten signatures at bottom (Burgemeester and Wethouder)
- Stamps/seals visible (partially illegible circular stamps on document)
- Handwritten annotations/marks in margins
