title: Belgian Congo Company Share – Extraits des Statuts (Statutes Extract)
description: Extracts of the statutes (Extraits des Statuts) of an anonymous Belgian Congo company, printed in French. The company has its registered office in the Congo Belge with branches in Brussels, France, and the international union. Capital represented by 1,500 shares of 50 francs each. Louis Lambotin named as the company's first representative in the Congo. Page 1 of 2.
creator: Unknown Belgian Congo company
notes: Belgian/French company document, Extrait des Statuts (corporate statutes extract) in French; references to Louis Lambert, general assembly in Brussels, dividends and board of directors; p.1/2
transcription:
# Transcription

**Original:**

EXTRAIT DES STATUTS

ARTICLE PREMIER. — Il est constitué, par les présentes, une société anonyme sous la dénomination de l'Ucèlamba.

ART. 2. — La Société a son siège social au Congo Belge indépendant, dans le bassin de l'Ukelamba, et son siège administratif en Belgique, à Bruxelles ou dans l'agglomération bruxelloise.

Elle pourra avoir des sièges administratifs en tous pays où elle exercera son industrie ou son commerce.

ART. 3. — La Société est fondée pour une durée de trente ans, mais elle peut proroger ses engagements et essuyer des concessions pour un terme excédant cette durée.

Le délai pourra être prolongé en réalité comme il sera dit à l'article 31 des présents statuts.

ART. 5. — Le capital social est fixé à 750,000 francs, représenté par 5,000 actions de capital, sans désignation de valeur.

Chaque action confère à la Société de l'Ukelamba un droit d'enquête, pour elle, n'hg de 500 hectares de terres, dont la propriété a été demandée par elle et dont les comparties déclarent approbatrice. A défaillance dans le bassin de l'Ukelamba.

D'y constitution de cet apport, il lui est attribué les deux dixièmes des actions de dividende.

1° 200 actions de dividende restantes sont attribuées aux souscripteurs, après, des 5,000 actions de capital, proportionnellement à leur souscription.

Toutes les actions de dividende sont inaliénables pendant les trois premières années après la date des présentes, à moins d'une autorisation expresse du Conseil d'administration.

Le nombre des actions de dividende ne pourra jamais être augmenté, pas même par voie de modification aux statuts.

ART. 30. — Les assemblées générales se réunissent à Bruxelles ou dans l'une des faubourgs, au lieu désigné par le Conseil d'administration.

L'assemblée générale annuelle se tient le dernier samedi du juin de chaque année, à 3 heures de relevée.

ART. 31. — Au 31 décembre de chaque année, et pour la première fois le 31 décembre 1889, les livres de la Société sont arrêtés et le Conseil d'administration dresse le bilan.

ART. 4. — L'excédent favorable du bilan, déduction faite de tous les frais généraux, condition le bénéfice de la Société ; ce bénéfice sera réparti comme suit :

1. 5 p. c. pour la réserve légale, jusqu'à ce qu'elle ait atteint la dixième partie du capital social.

Le Conseil d'administration pourra augmenter cette réserve et, en outre, créer une réserve spéciale ou un fonds de prévision ;

2. Un premier dividende de 5 p. c. sur la partie appelée des actions de capital, dans le cas où les bénéfices nets d'une année seraient pas suffisants pour payer ce dividende aux actions de capital ; somme qui manquera sera prélevée, sans intérêt de retard, sur les bénéfices des années subséquentes, après dotation de la réserve légale.

Le solde se répartit comme suit :

2 p. c. à chaque commissaire un tiers du tantième revenant à chaque administrateur.

Le minimum à attribuer à chaque administrateur et à chaque commissaire est réservé à la délation de l'assemblée générale, qui en dispose immédiatement après la constitution de la Société.

Le surplus est distribué par motifs aux actions de capital et par motifs aux actions de dividende.

---

## English Translation:

EXTRACT OF THE STATUTES

ARTICLE FIRST. — There is hereby constituted, by these presents, an anonymous company under the denomination of l'Ucèlamba.

ART. 2. — The Company has its registered office in the Belgian Congo Independent, in the basin of the Ukelamba, and its administrative office in Belgium, in Brussels or in the Brussels agglomeration.

It may have administrative offices in all countries where it carries on its industry or commerce.

ART. 3. — The Company is founded for a term of thirty years, but it may extend its commitments and sustain concessions for a term exceeding this period.

The period may be prolonged in reality as shall be stated in Article 31 of the present statutes.

ART. 5. — The capital stock is fixed at 750,000 francs, represented by 5,000 shares of capital stock, without designation of value.

Each share grants to the Company of the Ukelamba a right of inquiry, for it, of 500 hectares of land, whose property has been requested by it and whose neighbors declare approval. Upon default in the Ukelamba basin.

Upon constitution of this contribution, there are attributed to it two tenths of the dividend shares.

1° 200 remaining dividend shares are attributed to the subscribers, after, of the 5,000 capital shares, proportionally to their subscription.

All dividend shares are inalienable during the first three years after the date of these presents, unless by express authorization of the Board of Administration.

The number of dividend shares may never be increased, not even by way of amendment to the statutes.

ART. 30. — General assemblies meet in Brussels or in one of the suburbs, at the place designated by the Board of Administration.

The annual general assembly is held on the last Saturday of June of each year, at 3 o'clock in the afternoon.

ART. 31. — On December 31 of each year, and for the first time on December 31, 1889, the books of the Company are closed and the Board of Administration prepares the balance sheet.

ART. 4. — The favorable balance of the balance sheet, after deduction of all general expenses, constitutes the profit of the Company; this profit shall be distributed as follows:

1. 5 percent for the legal reserve, until it has reached one tenth of the capital stock.

The Board of Administration may increase this reserve and, moreover, create a special reserve or a contingency fund;

2. A first dividend of 5 percent on the called portion of the capital shares, in the case where the net profits of a year would not be sufficient to pay this dividend on the capital shares; the amount that is lacking shall be taken, without interest on arrears, from the profits of subsequent years, after allocation to the legal reserve.

The balance is distributed as follows:

2 percent to each commissioner one third of the portion accruing to each administrator.

The minimum to be allocated to each administrator and to each commissioner is reserved for the disposal of the general assembly, which disposes of it immediately after the constitution of the Company.

The surplus is distributed one half to the capital shares and one half to the dividend shares.

---

## Notable Markings:

Decorative ornamental borders frame the text with scrollwork designs at all four corners and along the edges. A decorative flourish appears at the bottom center of the page.

# Transcription of 18th-Century Dutch Manuscript Bond

## Original (Dutch):

The document is heavily worn and contains dense handwritten text in early modern Dutch. Due to the age, condition, and cursive handwriting style, much of the text is difficult to read with complete certainty. The following represents the most legible portions:

**Main body (opening):**
[Large block of dense manuscript text - largely illegible due to deterioration, but appears to be standard legal/contractual language typical of Dutch obligations/bonds of the period]

**Bottom section - Endorsement signatures and dates (more legible):**
- Dated entries visible include references to years: 1722, 1726, 1728, 1729, 1732, 1740
- Multiple signature lines with associated dates
- Names and notations partially visible but difficult to read with certainty due to condition

**Marginal annotations:**
Various notations in margins throughout, including what appear to be reference marks and calculations

## English translation:

Due to the severely degraded condition of the manuscript and the density of the handwriting, a reliable complete translation is not feasible. However, the document structure suggests this is a formal **Dutch obligation/bond (obligatie)** with multiple endorsement transfers recorded chronologically at the bottom, indicating the document changed hands multiple times between approximately 1722-1740.

## Notable markings:

- **Signatures:** Multiple cursive signatures and initials throughout (illegible)
- **Dates:** 1722, 1726, 1728, 1729, 1732, 1740 (visible at bottom)
- **Wax seal impression:** Circular seal mark visible in center-bottom area
- **Damage:** Significant wear, staining, edge deterioration, and creasing throughout
- **Physical condition:** Thick paper/parchment, brown discoloration consistent with age

title: Da Ming Tong Xing Bao Chao (Great Ming Circulating Treasure Note) – 1 Guan
description: Front of a Ming Dynasty paper banknote (大明通行寶鈔, Da Ming Tong Xing Bao Chao) for 1 Guan (= 1,000 wen/cash), issued by the Ministry of Revenue (戶部) of the Great Ming dynasty. Bears decorative border with dragon motifs, central denomination character 壹貫, official red seals, and text stating the note circulates interchangeably with copper coins. First issued in 1375 under the Hongwu Emperor; this format was produced throughout the dynasty.

# Transcription: Da Ming Tong Xing Bao Chao (1 Guan Note)

## Original Text (Classical Chinese):

**Header (top, right to left):**
大明行钞一贯

**Central Denomination Panel:**
壹貫

**Lower Section (right to left, top to bottom):**
戶部奉
太祖高皇帝
制曰凡民間
買賣皆許用
寶鈔一貫準
銅錢一千文
除金銀外不
許別作踏樣
者被徒二年
仍沒其家産
[several characters unclear/illegible due to wear]
千[?]月[?]日

## English Translation:

**Header:**
Great Ming Circulating Treasure Note – One Guan

**Central Denomination:**
One Guan [worth 1,000 wen/cash]

**Lower Text (summarized):**
The Ministry of Revenue, following the mandate of the Great Ancestor Emperor [Hongwu Emperor], proclaims: All common people are permitted to use Treasure Notes in their buying and selling. One Guan note is equivalent to one thousand copper coins. Except for gold and silver, no other denominations or counterfeits are permitted. Those who violate this shall be punished with two years of forced labor and confiscation of their entire household property...
[remainder partially illegible]

title: United States Continental Lottery Ticket, Class the First, No. 16m769, 1776
description: This printed lottery ticket reads: "United States Lottery No. 16m769 – CLASS the FIRST. THIS TICKET entitles the Bearer to receive such PRIZE as may be drawn against its Number, according to a Resolution of CONGRESS, passed at Philadelphia, November 18, 1776." Signed in manuscript by "D. Jackson" (a lottery manager) and marked "I." at the lower left to indicate the first class. The Continental Congress authorized this lottery by resolution on November 18, 1776 to help finance the Revolutionary War, organizing it in successive classes with tickets priced at $20 each. The printed ornamental border, the engraved script heading, and the official congressional authorization text are all identical to the other surviving Continental Lottery ticket in this collection (goetzmann0186), confirming both were printed from the same plate and issued under the same authority. This ticket is among the few known surviving examples of the first class of the United States Continental Lottery.
creator: D. Jackson (lottery manager)
notes: United States (Continental) Lottery ticket, Class the First, No. 16m769. Authorized by Resolution of Congress, Philadelphia, November 18, 1776. Signed by D. Jackson.

title: South Sea Annuities Dividend Warrant, No. 624, to Conrade de Gols, 1730 (Page 1 of 2)
description: This printed and manuscript dividend warrant is headed "14th" and numbered "No. 624," addressed to "Mr. Conrade De Gols, Sir." It directs: "You may pay on the 13th Day of May, 1730 [to] Mr. John Bardin the Sum of Sixteen Pounds Eight Shillings for Half a Year's Annuity, at 4 per Cent. per Annum, on the Sum of £820 Interest or Share in the Joint-Stock of South-Sea-Annuities, Erected by Act of Parliament in the Ninth Year of the Reign of His late Majesty King George, made for dividing the whole Capital Stock of the South-Sea-Company into Two equal Parts or Moieties, and for converting one of the said Moieties into certain Annuities... which became due on the 25th Day of March, 1730, and take a Discharge on the Back hereof. South-Sea-House the 30th Day of April, 1730." Signed by Williams and E. Anderson. This warrant is evidence of the post-Bubble reorganization of the South Sea Company, in which the company's capital stock was divided into equity shares (South Sea Stock) and annuities (South Sea Annuities) to satisfy creditors.

title: Antwerp Exchange Obligation, Generale Keyserlyke Company, 1725 (Page 1 of 2)
description: This printed and handwritten Dutch-language obligation, issued at Antwerp at the Exchange ("t'Antwerpen bg A. Du Casté og de Borse 1725"), bears an engraved coat of arms at the top. The text ("Ck Onderteekene... beloove en obligeer my... een Contante premie") records a financial obligation by J.B. Franciscus, acting as voordeel-Man (profit-man or agent), to deliver and pay for shares in the Generale Keyserlyke Jut-die Compagnie (Imperial General... Company) at Antwerp. The contract specifies conditions for reimbursement of all legitimate costs, payment of dividends, and liability if the shares are not delivered on the appointed transport-day. The document was signed on a seventeenth of April in the year seventeen hundred and twenty-five, in Antwerp, before witnesses Jan van Lancker and Joan Parcolo Wittebog as guarantor. The Antwerp Exchange was one of the most important trading floors in the Spanish/Austrian Netherlands.

transcription:
# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

tot Antwerpen bij A. Di. Civili
bij de Bourse 1728

[Coat of arms with "SPIRO SPERO" motto]

I. B. Tongelins Sculpist Antw:

Ck Onderschreve —, —, —, belove en obligiere my, mits een Constante premie, die tot myn contentement hebbe ontfangen, aen [handwritten: Mijn Heer Grammond?] ofte ordre, op het verthood deser tuschen heden en den eersten transportdagh naer [handwritten: Anno 1730 vijfde maande] 1730 — te leveren en te transporteeren [handwritten: Jan 28 —] Actien, inde Generacle Keyserlyke Indise Compagnie althier, onder conditie, van aen my benefens het transport te rembourfseren alle her gene nu reels is, en mildschen noch op die Actien in die tustschen-teyt sal gefourniert worden, en boven dien noch te gelyek aen my te betaelen [handwritten: andere Summe van Vierhondert en Vijftig ponden] voor tweede Premie a [handwritten: Dateerend Voordagh per Cento Agto] op ieder Actie, fullende daer regens aen de gene die dese Actien sal aenvoerden, ende betaelen, ter selver tyde uyt-keeruen mijn bovenfchreve alle voor de Compagnie in die tutschen-teydt op die Actien ceniger wyfe sal divideren, rembourleren, ofte geven, al beleere duyck meer als zijn bovenfchreve te doene naer betaelichge, maer [handwritten: voor de tweede Male den 1730] voorseyt, dese Actien aen my niet opgevraeght fijnde, en op den eersten transpot-dagh naer de op-vraeging niet getransporteerde betaelt fijnde, waer toe den op-vraeger gehouden is, dan blyft dese Obligatie ten mynen laste Nulle, en van geender waerde meer, fijnde dan die Actien gelyck oock den contanten premie, die my voor nu voor al-too is opgeleverd, en onder-roepelyek toebeboort, met alles wat van de Compagnie sal ontfangen. Aldus gecontractaert ter interventie van [handwritten: Makelaar Wijkman?]

onder verbintenisse van myn Persoon en goederen in Antwerpen den [handwritten: Twelfde April —] zeventien hondert [handwritten: en twintig]

[Signature] Jean Van Lancker

voor Actien à 192 P. C. in optie tot [handwritten: Eerste October 1730] inden tustschen tyde 2 daghen voor transpot op te vraegen

[Handwritten annotations and signature: Carlo Brittele / Jean Crampel?]

---

## English translation:

In Antwerp by A. Di. Civili
at the Exchange 1728

[Coat of arms with "I BREATHE I HOPE" motto]

I. B. Tongelins Sculptor Antwerp:

I, the undersigned —, —, —, promise and obligate myself, with a constant premium, which I have received to my satisfaction, to [handwritten: My Lord Grammont?] or order, on the obligation hereof between now and the first delivery day after [handwritten: Anno 1730 fifth month] 1730 — to deliver and transport [handwritten: January 28 —] Shares in the General Imperial Indian Company here, under the condition that I will reimburse my beneficiary for the transport of all that is now due, and further still on those Shares in the interim period shall be furnished, and beyond that still to pay equally to me [handwritten: another Sum of Four Hundred and Fifty pounds] for a second Premium at [handwritten: Dated Postage of Per Cent Ajo] on each Share, with the understanding that for those delivering these Shares and paying, at the same time the above-mentioned all for the Company in the interim period on the Shares anything shall divide, reimburse, or give, all much less than his above-mentioned to do toward settlement, but [handwritten: for the second time the 1730] aforesaid, these Shares not having been requested of me, and on the first delivery day after request not being transported [and] paid for, whereby the requester is obligated, then this Obligation remains to my charge as Null and void of any value more, whereby those Shares like also the cash premium, which I for now for all time have received, and subordinately belong to me, with all that the Company shall deliver. Thus contracted with the intervention of [handwritten: Broker Wijkman?]

under obligation of my Person and goods in Antwerp the [handwritten: Twelfth April —] seventeen hundred [handwritten: and thirty]

[Signature] Jean Van Lancker

for Shares at 192 P.C. in option until [handwritten: First October 1730] in the interim period 2 days for transport to be requested

---

## Notable markings:

- **Coat of arms seal**: Central oval seal with imperial eagle, crown, and crossed insignia with "SPIRO SPERO" (I Breathe I Hope) motto
- **Artist signature**: "I. B. Tongelins Sculpist Antw:" (Sculptor in Antwerp)
- **Multiple handwritten annotations and corrections** throughout the document in cursive script
- **Signatures**: Jean Van Lancker (main signature), with additional names Carlo Brittele and Jean Crampel (?) at bottom
- **Serial/Reference numbers**: 192 P.C., dates of 1728, 1730, and 1730
- **Document condition**: Aged parchment with wear and foxing marks

---

# Transcription

**Original (Dutch/French):**

[Top of page - handwritten:]
"...decree soit contract aen o'ordre mois Joan d'ramp sonier guarsntie
Joan l'arcole Wittde..."

[Main body - printed text with manuscript annotations, largely illegible due to image quality and fading]

The document appears to be a formal legal/financial contract with multiple paragraphs of printed text in Dutch or French, with various handwritten annotations and markings throughout.

[Bottom sections contain additional text that is too faded or blurred to transcribe with confidence]

**Notable markings:**

- **Central seal/coat of arms:** An oval heraldic seal featuring an imperial or royal coat of arms (appears to be Dutch/Holy Roman Empire heraldry), centered in the upper portion of the page
- **Handwritten signatures/initials:** Multiple manuscript signatures and notations in the margins
- **Wax seal or stamp:** Visible in lower right corner area (appears faded)
- **Serial numbers or reference marks:** Present but illegible

**Note:** The majority of the printed body text is too faded in this scan to accurately transcribe. This appears to be page 2 of a 2-page obligation document related to the Generale Keyserlyke Company (General Imperial Company) for Antwerp Exchange, dated 1725-1730.

title: Alexander Hamilton Circular Letter on State Debt Certificates, Treasury Department, 1795
description: This manuscript letter, headed "Treasury Department Oct 13, 1795 (Circular)," is signed "A. Hamilton" and addressed to loan commissioners across the United States. Hamilton writes: "I find from a letter from one of the Commissioners of Loans, that it is conceived, certificates of the State Debts cannot be subscribed to the new Loans unless that they express that they are issued for services or supplies towards the prosecution of the late war." He advises that this overly narrow construction would improperly exclude legitimate state debt certificates, and that any certificate of a state that was issued for compensations and expenditures toward prosecution of the war and the defense of the United States should be accepted as eligible for subscription to the new federal loan, even if the certificate does not explicitly state this purpose. This circular represents Hamilton's active management of the first U.S. federal debt consolidation under the Funding Act of 1790, clarifying eligibility rules for converting state war debts into federal securities.
creator: Alexander Hamilton (Secretary of the Treasury)
notes: Alexander Hamilton circular letter, Treasury Department, October 13, 1795. On eligibility of state debt certificates for subscription to new federal loans under the Funding Act of 1790.
transcription:
# Transcription

**Treasury Department Oct 13 1795**

(Circular)

Sir,

I find from a letter from one of the Commissioners of Loans, that it is conceived, certificates of the State Debts cannot be subscribed to the new Loans unless that they express that they are issued for services or supplies towards the prosecution of the late war.—

By this construction all certificates containing a simple acknowledgement of debt to a certain amount and all Certificates for monies loaned to the States would be excluded which is not the intent and meaning of the laws. If a Certificate of any State shall be issued and it shall not appear by the tenor of it, if it cannot be ascertained from any public record act or document to have been issued for some purpose, other than common transactions and expenditures for forces or supplies towards the prosecution of the late war and the defence of the United States, or some part thereof during the said war, it is consistent with the directions of the law to receive it on loan.—

I am Sir

Your Obedient Servant

**A Hamilton**

---

**Notable markings:**

- Signature: "A Hamilton" (Alexander Hamilton)
- Document classification: "(Circular)" - indicating this is a circular letter distributed to multiple recipients
- Date: October 13, 1795
- Header: "Treasury Department"

title: Massachusetts Bills of Exchange Schedule, Thomas Hopkinson, Philadelphia, 1778 (Page 1 of 2)
description: This handwritten schedule, titled "Massachusetts," records a distribution of bills of exchange in multiple denominations for the State of Massachusetts, signed by Thomas Hopkinson, LL [Loan-office Loan?], Philadelphia, September 22, 1778. The columns list denomination (from $12 to $1,200), sets, serial number ranges, and amounts in dollars. Totals: 1,803 sets of bills amounting to $93,000. Specific entries: $12 denomination—305 sets (nos. 36-340)=$3,660; $18—305 sets=$5,490; $24—305 sets=$7,320; $30—305 sets=$9,150; $36—305 sets=$10,980; $60—100 sets=$6,000; $120—100 sets=$12,000; $300—48 sets=$14,400; $600—20 sets=$12,000; $1,200—10 sets=$12,000. This document records the allocation of Continental loan-office certificates or bills of exchange drawn on behalf of Massachusetts during the Revolutionary War, consistent with the Continental Congress's financing operations in 1778.

title: Rowley, Massachusetts: Selectmen's Fine Warrant to Constable Samuel Searl, 1782 (Page 1 of 3)
description: This handwritten document from the Selectmen of Rowley, Massachusetts, dated August 15, 1782, is addressed "To Constable Samuel Searl" and orders him to collect fines from the persons named on the accompanying list. The text reads: "the Within List Contains Each Person there in Named is afresed as their Proportion of the fine Levid on the Sixth Class in the Town of Rowley for not procuring a man to Serve in the Continental Army pr. year agreeable to a Resolve of the Generel Court. Each Persons Tax is in the line with their name and the Same is Committed to you to Colect." Signed by the Selectmen of Rowley: Thomas Mighill, Daniel Spofford, Joseph Poor, [?] Pickard, and Isaac Smith. This document illustrates the Massachusetts system of class-based conscription, by which towns were divided into classes of taxpayers, each class responsible for providing a soldier for a year or paying a fine in lieu of service.

title: State of Massachusetts Bay War Bond, £20, Seth Davenport, 1777 (Page 1 of 2)
description: This printed and handwritten bond certificate is issued by the State of Massachusetts Bay, dated February 26, 1777. It reads: "Borrowed and received of Seth Davenport, the Sum of Twenty Pounds, Lawful Money, for the Use and Service of the State of Massachusetts-Bay; and in behalf of said State I do hereby promise and oblige myself and Successors in the Office of Treasurer or Receiver-General, to repay the Possessor by the First Day of June 1780, the aforesaid Sum of [Twenty Pounds] Lawful Money, in Spanish mill'd Dollars at Six Shillings each, or in the several Species of coined Silver and Gold enumerated in an Act made and passed in the Twenty third Year of his late Majesty King George the Second... with Interest to be paid annually at Six per Cent." Signed by D. Jeffries and the Committee (J. Summer). This is a direct emission of state war debt during the American Revolutionary War, promising repayment by June 1780 at 6% annual interest, before the federal consolidation of state debts under Hamilton's Funding Act of 1790.

transcription:
# Transcription: State of Massachusetts Bay War Bond, 1777

**State of the Massachusetts-Bay**

Sept 26, 1777

**Borrowed and received of** Seth Davenport **the Sum of** Twenty Pounds **Lawful Money, for the Use and Service of the STATE of** Massachusetts-Bay; **and in behalf of said State I do hereby promise and oblige myself and Successors in the Office of Treasurer or Receiver-General, to re-pay the Boffeller by the First Day of** June 1780, **the aforesaid Sum of** Twenty Pounds **Lawful Money, in Spanish mill'd Dollars at** Six Shillings **each, or in the several Species of coined Silver and** Gold **enumerated in an Act made and passed in the Twenty third Year of his late Majesty King** George **the Second, entitled an Act for ascertaining the Rates at which coined Silver and** Gold, **English Half Pence and Farthings may pass within this Government, and according to the Rates therein mentioned, with Interest to be paid annually at** Six per Cent.

**Witness my Hand,**

D. Saffrie [or Affre?]
[Illegible] Committee
J. Summer [or Sumner]

[Signature - appears crossed out]

title: Dutch Bond for the French Bourbon Princes in Exile, Amsterdam, 1793
description: This printed Dutch-language bond is headed with a reference to the Peace of Westphalia and bears the names "Louis Stanislas Xavier, Charles Philippe" (i.e., the Comte de Provence and the Comte d'Artois—the exiled Bourbon princes, brothers of Louis XVI) as issuers, with the title "Le Maréchal, Duc de Brolie" (the Maréchal de Broglie) as signing authority. Numbered N° 373, it records that "J. Bourcourd en Wedowe F. Croese & Comp." of Amsterdam acknowledge receipt of "EEN DUIZEND GULDENS Hollands Courant" (One Thousand Dutch Guilders) in exchange for a bond with attached coupons, dated Amsterdam, September 28, 1793. Signed by the Garde du Trésor Royal and countersigned "Par ordre de leurs Altesses Royales" (By order of their Royal Highnesses). This bond was issued during the period when the French princes were raising funds in the Netherlands to finance their counter-revolutionary campaigns against the French Republic, using Amsterdam bankers as their financial agents.
creator: Louis Stanislas Xavier (Comte de Provence); Charles Philippe (Comte d'Artois); J. Bourcourd & Wedowe F. Croese & Comp. (Amsterdam)
notes: Dutch bond (1,000 guilders) issued by French Bourbon princes in exile (Louis Stanislas Xavier and Charles Philippe) through Amsterdam bankers J. Bourcourd & Wedowe F. Croese & Comp., September 28, 1793. Signed on behalf of the Garde du Trésor Royal.

Deze gecontrafigneerd en ten Prothocole aangetekend, [illegible] behorende de door my getekende Aandeclen niet boven het getal van Twaalf Duyzend Nummers. title: Russian Imperial Treasury Note, 50 Rubles, 4%, 1915
description: This ornate printed Russian Imperial Treasury Note ("Билета Государственного Казначейства в Пятьдесят Рублей" – Treasury Note for Fifty Rubles), No. 352814, dated 1915, bears the double-headed imperial eagle and is printed in green and black. The note bears a 4% annual interest rate and is declared valid until August 1, 1929 ("Билет действителен по 1 Августа 1929 г."). Four attached coupons at the left side are numbered 5 through 8 and each represent one ruble of interest per coupon. The note is signed by the Director of the Imperial State Treasury and a Bookkeeper. Russian Short-term Treasury Notes (казначейские билеты) were issued throughout the late Imperial period as interest-bearing short-term government obligations; this 1915 example was issued during World War I as Russia's financial situation became increasingly strained. The note was never redeemed, as the Imperial government collapsed in the revolutions of 1917.
creator: Imperial Russian State Treasury
notes: Russian Imperial Treasury Note, 50 rubles, 4%, No. 352814, 1915. Valid until August 1, 1929. With four interest coupons. Never redeemed due to collapse of Imperial government in 1917.

title: Kingdom of Greece Gold-Guaranteed Loan Bond, 2½%, 2,500 Drachmas, 1898
description: This elaborately printed bond certificate is headed "Βασίλειον της Ελλάδος" (Kingdom of Greece) and titled "Δάνειον εις Χρυσόν Ηγγυημένον 2½ θό 1898 / Ομολογία Ανωνύμου 2,500 Δραχμών" (Gold-Guaranteed Loan 2½% 1898 / Bearer Bond of 2,500 Drachmas). No. 30,731. The bond is also labeled "Emprunt Hellénique Garanti 2½% Or de 1898" (Greek Guaranteed Gold Loan 2½% of 1898) in French along the left border. Printed in blue and red with classical allegorical figures (a male warrior and a draped female figure flanking the central text) and the Greek royal coat of arms. The bond is signed by the Greek Minister of Finance and authenticated by the Governor and Company of the Bank of England. The 1898 Greek Guaranteed Loan was arranged under international financial supervision following Greece's default of 1893 and defeat in the Greco-Turkish War of 1897, with France, Great Britain, and other powers guaranteeing repayment as part of an International Financial Control Commission imposed on Greece.
creator: Kingdom of Greece (Ministry of Finance); guaranteed by Bank of England
notes: Kingdom of Greece Gold-Guaranteed Loan bond, 2½% 1898, 2,500 drachmas, No. 30,731. International guarantee; authenticated by Bank of England. Issued under International Financial Commission supervision following Greek default of 1893.

title: Romania: Army Equipment Bond (Bon pentru Înzestrarea Armatei), 500 Lei, 1940
description: This ornate multi-colored bond certificate is issued by "România, Casa Autonomă a Monopolului Apărării Naţionale" (Romania, Autonomous Administration of National Defense Monopolies) and titled "Bon pentru Înzestrarea Armatei" (Bond for Army Equipment), Series A, No. 361,551, for 500 lei. The bond is dated 1940 and authorized by a law of April 6, 1940. The text indicates payment on March 1, 1945 by the Autonomous Administration of National Defense Monopolies. The certificate is signed by the Governor and Director General of the Autonomous Administration. The design is richly printed in red, green, and gold with Romanian national ornamental patterns, the Romanian coat of arms, and three small vignette images of military scenes at the bottom (artillery, infantry, cavalry). The bond was issued to finance Romanian military rearmament as World War II began, at a time when Romania was attempting to strengthen its defenses before the Nazi-Soviet Pact pressure forced it to cede Bessarabia and northern Transylvania in 1940.
creator: Casa Autonomă a Monopolului Apărării Naţionale (Romania)
notes: Romanian Army Equipment Bond, Series A, No. 361,551, 500 lei, 1940. Issued to finance military rearmament. Due March 1, 1945.

title: American & British Securities Company Common Stock Certificate, 100 Shares, 1919
description: This printed stock certificate, No. 18, certifies that "Hattie J. Barnhill" is the owner of one hundred shares of Common Stock of the American & British Securities Company, incorporated under the laws of the State of Delaware. The certificate is stamped "COMMON" in large red letters diagonally across the center. Terms specify that each share of preferred stock and each share of common stock outstanding shall be entitled to one vote at stockholders' meetings. The certificate is dated September 1919 (a "29" day is partially legible), signed by the Secretary and another officer, with a transferred signature "Edward P[?] Holmes" on the back. A handwritten annotation "98-0186.06b" appears at top right, indicating a museum or archive accession number. The American & British Securities Company was a Delaware investment holding company of the type common in the early 20th century, capitalizing on Anglo-American financial connections in the immediate post-World War I period.
creator: American & British Securities Company
notes: American & British Securities Company common stock certificate, 100 shares, No. 18, issued to Hattie J. Barnhill, September 1919. Incorporated in Delaware.
transcription:
# Transcription

**No. 18                                                                                                           100 Shares**

## AMERICAN & BRITISH SECURITIES COMPANY
INCORPORATED UNDER THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF DELAWARE.

This Certifies that **Hattie J. Barnhill**

is the owner of **ONE HUNDRED (100)** shares of COMMON STOCK of AMERICAN & BRITISH SECURITIES COMPANY, each share without nominal or par value, fully paid and non-assessable, transferable only on the books of the corporation, in person or by attorney, upon the surrender of this certificate properly endorsed.

The holders of preferred stock are entitled to receive on or before the first day of each annum, payable semi-annually in each year, at such times as the board of Directors shall determine, and which said dividends shall be in preference to and shall be payable before any dividends shall be apart on paid upon the common stock. The dividends due upon the preferred stock semi-annually from the date of issue to and including September 15th, 1919, shall not be cumulative, but thereafter, to-wit, after September 15th, 1919, dividends at the rate of seven per cent. (7%) per annum on any deficiency in any of the dividends to be paid on the preferred stock on or before October 15th, 1919, shall be made good out of the earnings of subsequent years before dividends shall be paid upon the common stock.

In the event of a dissolution or liquidation of the corporation or a voluntary or involuntary, or upon any distribution of its capital, there shall be paid to the holders of the preferred stock the par value thereof, to wit, one hundred dollars ($100) per share, and also any assets distributed among the holders of the common stock, after payment to the holders of the preferred stock of its accrued and unpaid, accrued and accumulated dividends as above provided, the remaining funds of the corporation shall be divided among and paid to the holders of the common stock, according to the shares held by each respectively.

The corporation shall have the option of redeeming the preferred stock, in whole or in part, at any time after issue, on any semi-annual dividend period, by payment to the holders thereof of the sum of one hundred dollars ($100) per share for each share of such preferred stock so redeemed, together with all accrued and accumulated dividends thereon, upon thirty days actual or published notice to the holders thereof.

Each share of preferred stock and each share of common stock outstanding shall be entitled to one vote each at all meetings of stockholders for any purpose. The common stock shall have no nominal or par value, and each share of the same shall be entitled in the event of the sale or other disposition of all or substantially all of the assets of the corporation to vote thereon, subject to the payment of all sums due and payable upon the preferred stock, cumulative or non-cumulative according to their nature.

IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the corporation has caused this certificate to be signed by its duly authorized officers, and its corporate seal to be hereunto affixed this _____ day of _____________, 19___.

**Edward P. Johnson**                                                                                              [illegible signature]
Secretary                                                                                                                    President

---

## Notable markings:

- **Serial Number:** No. 18
- **Stock quantity:** 100 Shares
- **Large red diagonal stamp:** "COMMON"
- **Shareholder name:** Hattie J. Barnhill (handwritten)
- **Date:** September 1919 (day partially illegible, appears to be "29")
- **Secretary signature:** Edward P. Johnson (printed name with signature)
- **President signature:** [illegible, handwritten]
- **Archive accession number:** 98-0186.06b (handwritten, top right corner)
- **Printer mark:** HAMILTON BANK NOTE CO. (bottom center)
- **Back notation:** Transferred signature of "Edward P[?] Holmes"

title: Anglo-Argentine Tramways Company Limited: 5% Debenture Stock Certificate, No. A/68802 (£20, 1910)
description: This document is the reverse side of a £20 5% Debenture Stock Certificate (No. A/68802) issued by the Anglo-Argentine Tramways Company Limited. Printed in red on white, the document reproduces a dense two-column "Extract from the Trust Deed, Dated 15th June, 1910," which outlines the terms and conditions of the debenture stock, the powers of the trustees, and the rights of stockholders. Below the Trust Deed extract appears a "Certificate of the Registration of a Mortgage or Charge" pursuant to the Companies (Consolidation) Act, 1908, No. 49(1), signed by F. Atherton as Assistant Registrar of Joint Stock Companies. The Anglo-Argentine Tramways Company was a British-registered firm operating urban tramway lines in Buenos Aires, Argentina, and is an example of British investment in South American infrastructure during the Edwardian era.

title: Acts of Parliament, Anno Regni Georgii Regis Octavo, 1722 (Page 1 of 38)
description: This is the title page of the printed volume of Acts of Parliament for the eighth year of the reign of King George I of Great Britain. The title reads 'Anno Regni GEORGII REGIS Magnæ Britanniæ, Franciæ, & Hiberniæ OCTAVO,' noting that Parliament was begun and holden at Westminster on the seventeenth day of March, Anno Dom. 1714—in the first year of the reign—and continued by several prorogations to the nineteenth day of October, 1721, being the eighth session of this present Parliament. The royal arms of Great Britain with the 'G R' cypher are printed at the centre. The imprint at the foot reads: 'LONDON, Printed by John Baskett, Printer to the Kings most Excellent Majesty, And by the Assigns of Thomas Newcomb, and Henry Hills, deceas'd. 1722.' This volume is of particular interest as it contains the main parliamentary legislation enacted in direct response to the South Sea Bubble collapse of 1720.

An Act for Paying off and Cancelling One Million of Exchequer Bills, and to give Ease to the South-Sea Company, in respect of its present Obligation, to circulate or contribute towards Circulating Exchequer Bills; and to give further Time to that Company for Repayment of One Million, which was lent to them; and for issuing a further Sum in New Exchequer Bills, towards His Majesties Supply, to be Discharged and Cancelled, when the said Company shall repay the Million owing by them; and that the Exchequer Bills, which are to Continue, may be Circulated at sale and moderate Rates; and for Appropriating the Supplies granted to His Majesty in this Session of Parliament; and for Relief of the Sufferers at Nevis and Saint Christophers, by an Invasion of the French in the late War; and for laying a further Duty on Apples Imported; and for Ascertaining the Duties on Pictures Imported.

**WHEREAS** amongst divers Matters and Things contained in an Act of Parliament, made and passed in the Sixth Year of Your Majesties Reign; Intituled, An Act for Enabling the South-Sea Company to increase their present Capital Stock and Fund, by Redeeming such Publick Debts and Incumbrances as are therein mentioned; and for raising Money to be applied for Lessening several of the Publick Debts and Incumbrances; and for Calling in the present Exchequer Bills remaining Uncancelled; and for making forth New Bills in lieu thereof, to be Circulated and Exchanged upon Demand at or near the Exchequer: It was recited or mentioned, That after the Application of such Monies as are therein specified, the Principal Monies

for the Garrisons at Annapolis Royal, Placentia, and Gibraltar, for the Year One thousand seven hundred and twenty two; and any Sum and Sums of Money, not exceeding Fifteen thousand Pounds, upon Account of Out-Pensioners of Chelsea Hospital, for the Year One thousand seven hundred and twenty two; and the Sum and Sums of Money, not exceeding Twenty thousand seven hundred ninety five Pounds, Shillings, and Two pence; to Extraordinary Expenses to the Service of His Majesties Land Forces, and to Provisional and Forage feveral Soldiers Assignments not before provided for by Parliament; and any Sum and Sums of Money, not exceeding Eighty nine thousand Pounds, upon Account of half-pay to the Reduced Officers of His Majesties Land Forces and Ordnances; Subjected nevertheless to such Rules to be observed in the Application of the said half-pay, as are herein after prescribed concerning the same.

And be it Enacted, That the said Aids or Supplies provided, as aforesaid, shall not be issued or applied to any Use, Intent, or Purpose whatsoever, other than the Uses and Purposes before mentioned.

Provided always, That such Sums as by and in pursuance of any other Act of Parliament, are or shall be due or payable to any Commissioners for Sick, Grammatical, Shipping, and Determining the Debts due to the Army, for there Salaries, or to such Clerks, or other Incident Charges, shall or may be paid out of the Uses of Supplies aforesaid, or any of them; any thing herein contained to the contrary notwithstanding.

And as to the said Sum of Eighty nine thousand Pounds, by this Act appropriated on Account of half-pay, as aforesaid, It is hereby Enacted and Declared by the Authority aforesaid, That the Rules herein after prescribed, shall be duly observed in the Application thereof, That is to say,

That no Person shall have of receive any Part of the same, who was a Pinor under the Age of Sixteen Years, at the time when the Regiment, Troop, or Company in which he served, was reduced.

That no Person shall have of receive any Part of the same, except such Persons who did actual Service in some Regiment, Troop, or Company.

That no Person having any other Place of Employment of Profit, Civil or Military, under His Majesty, shall have or receive any Part of the half-pay.

That no Chaplain of any Regiment or Troop, or Company, who has any Ecclesiastical Benefice or other Preferment in Great Britain or Ireland, shall have or receive any Part of the said half-pay.

**336          Anno Regni octavo Georgii Regis.**

**Which Duties shall be paid Down in Ready Money by the respective Importers of such Plates, from time to time, before Landing of the same respectively.**

And be it Enacted by the Authority aforesaid, That the said Duties upon Plates Imported, shall be raised, levied, recovered, and paid, and be brought into the Exchequer, by such Rules, Maps, Means, and Methods, and under such Penalties and Forfeitures, and in such Manner and Form, as the Duties upon Plates hereby taken away, or any of them, might have been raised, levied, recovered, and paid, if the same or any of them had continued.

**How appropriated.**

Provided always, and it is hereby Enacted by the Authority aforesaid, That the Duties to arise upon the Importation of Plates, pursuant to this Act (the necessary Charges of Management excepted) shall be appropriated and applied, as near as may be, to the same Uses and Purposes, to which the former Duties upon Plates by this Act taken away, were applicable or ought to have been applied if this Act had not been made.

**How redeemable.**

Provided also, and it is hereby Enacted, That the Duties by this Act charged upon Plates Imported, or any part thereof, shall be redeemable by Parliament, in the time and Manner as the said former Duties on Plates hereby taken away, or any of them, were redeemable; and in all Cases where any of the said former Duties on Plates were to cease or determine, a proportional Part of the Duties on Plates hereby charged, shall likewise cease and determine.

D

**FINIS.**

title: Acts of Parliament, Anno Regni Georgii Regis Octavo, 1722 (Page 2 of 38)
description: This is the verso (reverse side) of the title page of the 1722 volume of Acts of Parliament for the eighth year of King George I. The printed title page is faintly visible in mirror image through the paper, a characteristic of early-eighteenth-century printing on hand-laid paper. No additional text or content is printed on this side. The page serves as the blank reverse leaf before the substantive Act texts begin on the following pages.
creator: Great Britain. Parliament
notes: Printed by John Baskett, Printer to the Kings most Excellent Majesty, and by the Assigns of Thomas Newcomb, and Henry Hills, deceas'd. London, 1722. Acts of the Eighth Session of Parliament begun 17 March 1714, continued by prorogation to 19 October 1721.

# Transcription

This page is the verso of the title page of "Acts of Parliament, Anno Regni Georgii Regis Octavo" (1722). The text visible is a faint mirror image of the title page printed through the paper, characteristic of early 18th-century hand-laid paper printing.

The faintly visible text includes:

**Anno Regni
GEORGII REGIS
Octavo.**

[Additional body text is present but too faint and reversed to read with confidence]

Below this is a coat of arms or royal seal impression.

Below the seal:

**LONDON**

[Publication information text follows but is largely illegible due to the reversed/mirror image nature]

---

**Notable markings:**

- Royal coat of arms/heraldic seal (centered, mid-page)
- This is a verso (blank reverse) leaf, with only the faint mirror image of the recto title page visible
- No signatures, stamps, or serial numbers are present on this side

title: Acts of Parliament, Anno Regni Georgii II Regis Vicesimo Quarto, 1750 (Page 1 of 18)
description: This is the title page of the printed volume of Acts of Parliament for the twenty-fourth year of the reign of King George II of Great Britain. The title reads 'Anno Regni GEORGII II. REGIS Magnæ Britanniæ, Franciæ, & Hiberniæ, VICESIMO QUARTO.' The page records that Parliament was begun and holden at Westminster on the tenth day of November, Anno Dom. 1747, in the twenty-first year of the reign, and continued by several prorogations to the seventeenth day of January, 1750—being the fourth session of this Parliament. The royal arms of Great Britain with the 'G R' cypher are printed at the centre. The imprint at the foot reads: 'LONDON: Printed by Thomas Baskett, Printer to the King's most Excellent Majesty; and by the Assigns of Robert Baskett. 1750.' This volume is notable for containing legislation to finally extinguish the residual South Sea Annuities, three decades after the South Sea Bubble.

transcription:
# Transcription

Anno Regni
GEORGII III.
REGIS

*Magnæ Britanniæ, Franciæ, & Hiberniæ,*
VICESIMO QUARTO.

At the Parliament begun and holden at *Westminster*, the Tenth Day of *November, Anno Dom.* 1747, in the Twenty first Year of the Reign of our Sovereign Lord *GEORGE* the Second, by the Grace of God, of *Great Britain, France, and Ireland*, King, Defender of the Faith, &c.

And from thence continued by several Prorogations to the Seventeenth Day of *January*, 1750, being the Fourth Session of this present Parliament.

---

G [Royal Coat of Arms] R

---

LONDON:
Printed by *Thomas Baskett*, Printer to the King's most Excellent Majesty; and by the Assigns of *Robert Baskett*. 1750.

---

- Royal coat of arms (GR cipher with lion and unicorn supporters)
- Ornamental border frame around entire page
- Binding visible on left edge

# Transcription

Anno vicefimo quarto

**Georgii II. Regis.**

title: Baltimore and Ohio Railroad Company, Common Stock
description: This engraved stock certificate represents 100 shares of Common Stock in the Baltimore and Ohio Railroad Company, Certificate No. C243475, issued to Evans Stillman & Co. on June 19, 1934. The Baltimore and Ohio Railroad, chartered in 1827, was the first commercial railroad in the United States, connecting Baltimore to the Ohio River. By 1934 the B&O had become one of the great trunk railroads of the eastern United States, though it faced severe financial strain during the Great Depression.
creator: Baltimore and Ohio Railroad Company
notes: Baltimore and Ohio Railroad Company, Common Stock, 100 shares, No. C243475, Evans Stillman & Co., June 19, 1934
transcription:
# Transcription of Baltimore and Ohio Railroad Company Stock Certificate

**COMMON STOCK**

100 [top left and right corners]

NUMBER: C243475

SHARES: 100

---

**The Baltimore and Ohio Railroad Company**

*Incorporated by the State of Maryland, February 28, 1827.*

This Certifies that

**EVANS, STILLMAN & CO.**

is the owner of

*One Hundred Shares of the Perpetual Preferred Letters and half paid and assessable of the Common Capital Stock in and Ohio Railroad Company transferred to and standing on the books of the Company in the name of [the holder hereof upon and subject to all the conditions upon which the same were issued and to all assessments and calls made thereon. The holder of Preferred Capital Stock is the owner of Forty Million Dollars ($40,000,000) now issued and such additional amounts as may be lawfully issued from time to time by the Board and Directors of said Railroad Company the property and franchise and rights granted to receive in each year out of the surplus net profits of the Company for the current year such yearly dividend (commutative as the Board of Directors of said Railroad Company may declare) to be not exceeding [for per centum] upon all dividends then declared. No transfer of said stock shall be valid until endorsed by or under the direction of the Transfer Agent. The holder of Preferred Capital Stock is the owner of Forty Million Dollars ($40,000,000) now issued and such additional amounts as may be lawfully issued from time to time by the Board and Directors of said Railroad Company.*

Dated: **JUN 19 1934**

---

**SHARES $100. EACH**

Transfer Agent signature line [signed but illegible]

- Ornamental border design throughout with decorative vignette depicting a steam locomotive and railroad scene at top center

title: Baltimore and Ohio Rail Road Company, 6% Preferred Stock
description: This engraved stock certificate represents shares of 6% Preferred Stock in the Baltimore and Ohio Rail Road Company, Certificate No. 466, issued circa September 1863. The B&O Railroad, the oldest commercial railroad in America, issued preferred stock during the Civil War era to fund operations and repairs as military activity along its lines—which ran through the border states of Maryland and Virginia—caused enormous damage to its infrastructure.
creator: Baltimore and Ohio Rail Road Company
notes: Baltimore and Ohio Rail Road Company, 6% Preferred Stock, No. 466, ca. September 1863
transcription:
# Baltimore and Ohio Rail Road Company Stock Certificate - Transcription

**TITLE AND HEADER:**
The Baltimore and Ohio Rail Road Company.
Six Per Cent. Preferred Stock.
Shares

**MAIN TEXT:**

This is to Certify, That A. Gregg & Co. [handwritten names]
are entitled to [handwritten number] Sixty [unclear] Shares
in the Joint Purse of the Preferred Stock of the BALTIMORE AND OHIO RAILROAD COMPANY, being a part of the Twenty Thousand Shares of the said Preferred Stock, which were authorized by a general meeting of the Stockholders of the said Company, held on the eleventh day of May, in the year Eighteen Hundred and Sixty Eight, under and by virtue of the provisions of an Act of the General Assembly of Maryland, passed at the January Session, Eighteen Hundred and Sixty Eight, enacting a substitute for Article Twenty-Six, of the Public General Laws of the said State, relating to Corporations; upon which the said [handwritten] A. Gregg & Co.
and [unclear] assigns are entitled, under all the terms of the creation of the said second series of said Preferred Stock, and of the agreement made by the subscribers thereto, and of the guarantee of the said Company, dated on the 25th day of May, in the year Eighteen Hundred and Sixty Eight, to a perpetual annuity of six per centum per annum, and so long as the same shall be payable out of the Net Income of the Stock and Profit in each and every year, before any dividend is declared to any of the Stockholders of the said Company. When the holders of the Preferred Stocks thereof, or their assigns, in the order in which the said Preferred Stocks were issued, it being hereby expressly understood to be the condition of the issue of this Certificate, and of the guarantee aforesaid, that the holder of this Certificate, and their assigns shall not be entitled to any dividend, of any nature or description, declared upon the Stock of the said Company, other than the said dividend of six per centum per annum, and no more, guaranteed as aforesaid, to be paid upon said Preferred Stock; and that the Stock evidenced by this Certificate is held subject to the conditions of its original issue, and of the subscription thereto, and of the guarantee thereof. The said Stock is transferable on the Books of the Company on or before [unclear] with an assignment endorsed thereon.

**WITNESS,** the Seal of the Company, attested by the Signature of the Treasurer thereof.

Baltimore, Sept. 7th, 1863 [handwritten date]

**SIGNATURES:**
[Illegible] Treasurer [handwritten signature]
[Illegible] President [handwritten signature]

**PRINTER MARK:**
Kell & Fitt, Print, Baltimore.

**CERTIFICATE NUMBER:**
No. 466

- Multiple decorative ovals along left and right margins containing "Six Per Cent Preferred Stock" repeated text
- Handwritten signatures of Treasurer and President
- Handwritten date: September 7th, 1863
- Handwritten names: A. Gregg & Co. and additional assignee names (partially legible)
- Printer attribution: Kell & Fitt, Print, Baltimore
- Serial number references visible in decorative margins

title: Banca Românească Share Certificate, Emisiunea VIII-A (1938)
description: This engraved Romanian share certificate represents one share of 500 lei par value in Banca Românească (Romanian Bank), Emisiunea VIII-A (8th-A Emission), issued in Bucharest in 1938, when the company's total capital stood at 350,000,000 lei. Banca Românească, founded in 1911, was one of Romania's leading commercial banks, playing a central role in financing Romanian industrial and commercial development during the interwar period. The 1938 issuance occurred under the royal dictatorship of Carol II, in the last years before Romania was drawn into World War II.
creator: Banca Românească
notes: Banca Românească, 500 lei share, Emisiunea VIII-A, Capital 350,000,000 lei, Bucharest 1938
transcription:

BANCA ROMÂNEASCĂ
SOCIETATE ANONIMĂ
CAPITAL SOCIAL LEI 350.000.000.
CHISISHCA VIII-A

CONFORM DECIZIUNI ADUNĂRII GENERALE EXTRAORDINARE DIN 12 FEBRUARIE 1938, PUBLICATA IN MONITORUL OFICIAL Nr 57 DIN 10 MARTIE 1938, SI INREGISTRATU IN REGISTRUL COMERCULUI DIN 12 FEBRUARIE 1938, CAPITALUL SOCIAL AL BANCII S'A SPORLT PRINTRO NOUA CHISIUNNE DE 140.000 LEI. DE CARE S'A PLATIT IN NUMERARIU 35% SI IN TITLURI DE STAT 65%. CONFORM HOTARARI A 2000 ACTIUNI A 500 LEI FIECARE.

DE ORDINUL TRIBUNALULUI IFOV SOCIETIA DE NOTAR PUBLICA LA NR 4437 DIN 11 DECEMBRIE 1910, PUBLICATA IN MONITORUL OFICIAL Nr 232 DIN 8 IANUARIE 1911...

UNA ACTIUNE

OF WHICH 35% WAS PAID IN CASH AND 65% IN STATE SECURITIES. IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE DECISION OF 2000 SHARES OF 500 LEI EACH.

title: Banca Românească Share Certificate (1920)
description: This engraved Romanian share certificate represents one share of 500 lei par value in Banca Românească (Romanian Bank), No. 011666, with a capital of 100,000,000 lei, issued in Bucharest in 1920. This emission dates from the immediate postwar period, when Romania—greatly enlarged after World War I by the acquisition of Transylvania, Bessarabia, Bukovina, and southern Dobruja—was reorganizing its banking sector to serve the expanded national economy. Banca Românească was one of the principal institutions financing the industrialization of Greater Romania.

title: Banco Territorial de Cuba (Crédit Foncier Cubain), Acción Beneficiaria
description: This certificate represents an Acción Beneficiaria (Beneficiary Share) No. 22752 of the Banco Territorial de Cuba, also known as the Crédit Foncier Cubain, issued in Havana on March 1, 1911. The Banco Territorial de Cuba was a French-backed mortgage bank financing land transactions and real estate in post-independence Cuba. Beneficiary shares (acciones beneficiarias) were a founders' share form common in Latin American financial institutions, entitling holders to a portion of profits without a fixed par value.
creator: Banco Territorial de Cuba
notes: Banco Territorial de Cuba (Crédit Foncier Cubain), Acción Beneficiaria No. 22752, Havana, March 1, 1911
transcription:
# Banco Territorial de Cuba - Stock Certificate Transcription

## Original Text:

**Header and Title:**
ACCION BENEFICIARIA | ACCION BENEFICIARIA

BANCO TERRITORIAL DE CUBA
DOMICILIO: HABANA, CUBA — CRÉDIT FONCIER CUBAIN — SOCIEDAD ANÓNIMA

**Serial Number:**
Nº 22752 (appears twice)

**Capital Information:**
CAPITAL PAGADO: $5,000,000 ORO | CAPITAL ENTIRELY VERSÉ: $ 5,000,000
DE LOS ESTADOS UNIDOS DE AMERICA. | POSSÉSS DES ÉTATS-UNIS D'AMÉRIQUE.
CREADO POR LAS LEYES DE LA REPÚBLICA DE CUBA | CRÉÉ PAR LES LOIS DE LA REPUBLIQUE DE CUBA
DE 20 DE JULIO DE 1910 Y 21 DE FEBRERO DE 1911. | DU 20 JUILLET 1910 ET DU 21 FÉVRIER 1911.
ACCIÓN BENEFICIARIA AL PORTADOR. | ACTION BÉNÉFICIAIRE AU PORTEUR.

**Capital Stock Information:**
CAPITAL STOCK FULLY PAID UP: $5,000,000 | EINGEZAHLTES STAMMKAPITAL:
IN GOLD OF THE UNITED STATES CURRENCY. | $5,000,000 IN DER GOLDWÄHRUNG DER
INCORPORATED UNDER THE LAWS OF JULY 20TH 1910 | VEREINIGTEN STAATEN VON AMERIKA.
AND FEBRUARY 21ST 1911. | GEGRÜNDET NACH DEN GESETZEN DER REPUBLIK KUBA
DIVIDEND SHARE OF STOCK ISSUED TO BEARER. | VOM 20.JULI (BEZW.AM 21.FEBRUAR 1911.
| TRÄGER-GEWINNANTEILSCHEIN.

**Main Body Text (Spanish - Left Column):**
"Este título representa una división de los 5,000 Beneficiarios de la Sociedad Anónima por acción Beneficiaria al Portador inscrita en el Registro Mercantil de la Isla de Cuba.

El tenedor de preferencia de este Título recibirá anualmente de entre los beneficios netos de la Sociedad, después de haber distribuido los dividendos ordinarios a las acciones ordinarias de la Sociedad, la parte que le corresponda en la cantidad que anualmente distribuya la Sociedad entre sus accionistas Beneficiarios.

Lo tenedor gozará de las mismas derechos y privilegios que confiere este Título a cualquiera otro tenedor, salvo que la transferencia de este Título se hará de conformidad con los términos que expresamente se requieren en los estatutos de la Sociedad."

**English Translation (Center Column):**
"This certificate represents one of the 5,000 Beneficiary Dividends of the Société Anonyme per Beneficiary Share to bearer registered in the Commercial Register of the Island of Cuba.

The holder of preference of this Title shall receive annually from among the net profits of the Company, after having distributed ordinary dividends to the ordinary shares of the Company, the part that corresponds to it in the amount that annually distributes the Company among its Beneficiary shareholders.

The holder shall enjoy the same rights and privileges that this Title grants to any other holder, except that the transfer of this Title shall be made in accordance with the terms that are expressly required in the statutes of the Company."

**French Translation (Center-Right Column):**
"A représente une fraction des 5,000 parts bénéficiaires de la Société Anonyme par action bénéficiaire au porteur inscrite au Registre de Commerce de l'Île de Cuba.

Le porteur de préférence de ce Titre recevra annuellement sur les bénéfices nets de la Compagnie, après avoir distribué les dividendes ordinaires sur les actions ordinaires de la Compagnie, la part qui lui correspond dans la somme que la Compagnie distribue annuellement entre ses actionnaires bénéficiaires.

Le porteur jouira des mêmes droits et privilèges que confère ce Titre à tout autre porteur, sauf que le transfert de ce Titre se fera conformément aux conditions qui sont expressément requises aux statuts de la Compagnie."

**German Translation (Right Column):**
"Diese Urkunde stellt einen Anteilschein dar von den 5,000 Gewinnanteilscheinen der Aktiengesellschaft pro Gewinnanteilschein auf den Inhaber, eingetragen im Handelsregister der Insel Kuba.

Der Inhaber dieses Anteilscheins wird jährlich aus dem Reingewinn der Gesellschaft, nachdem die ordentlichen Dividenden auf die ordentlichen Aktien der Gesellschaft verteilt worden sind, seinen Anteil an der Summe erhalten, die die Gesellschaft jährlich unter ihre Gewinnanteilschein-Inhaber verteilt.

Der Inhaber genießt die gleichen Rechte und Privilegien, welche dieser Anteilschein jedem anderen Inhaber gewährt, ausgenommen daß die Abtretung dieses Anteilscheins nach Maßgabe der ausdrücklich in den Satzungen der Gesellschaft festgelegten Bedingungen erfolgt."

**Date:**
Habana, 1º de Marzo de 1911 (Havana, March 1, 1911)

**Signatures:**
[Signature - left side] Francisco B. Reyes
[Signature - center/right side] [illegible but present]

**Coupon Information:**
COUPON TRANSLATION | COUPON ÜBERSETZUNG

"COUPON FOR DIVIDEND NUMBER_____
This coupon entitles the bearer to receive from the Banco Territorial de Cuba, or its agents or correspondents in Havana, Paris, Berlin, London, New York and other cities, the dividend corresponding to this share for the year [blank], with the presentation of this coupon duly endorsed at the offices of the Company."

DIVIDENDENANTIE NO._____
[German text describing dividend coupon procedures]

**Certificate Number:**
TITRE Nº22752
BON POUR RÉCLAMER LA FEUILLE DE COUPONS

## Notable Markings:

- **Serial Number:** No. 22752 (printed twice - top corners and bottom)
- **Ornamental Border:** Elaborate Art Nouveau green decorative border with geometric and scrollwork patterns
- **Vignettes:** Three engraved scenes:
  - Left: Municipal building in Havana with plaza
  - Center: Coat of arms/heraldic shield with dual flags
  - Right: Rural scene with ox-drawn cart and agricultural workers
- **Watermark/Circular Design:** Large decorative circular seal/watermark in bottom center
- **Languages:** Spanish, English, French, and German text
- **Official Seals:** Company seal visible at bottom
- **Signatures:** Two signatures present (one clearly reads "Francisco B. Reyes")
- **Typography:** Mix of decorative fonts and standard serif typeface
- **Color:** Green and black printing on cream/off-white stock

Bank Małopolski was a regional bank serving the formerly Austrian Galicia, a territory incorporated into the reconstituted Polish state after World War I. The dual denomination (Austrian Koron and Polish Marks) reflects the currency transition underway as Poland consolidated financial institutions inherited from three different partitioning empires.

title: Siberian Commercial Bank, Dividend Coupon Sheet
description: This document is a sheet of five dividend coupons (6th through 10th) for shares Nos. 17747–17748 of the Siberian Commercial Bank (Сибирский Торговый Банк / Banque Commerciale de Sibérie), covering dividend years 1917 through 1921. The Siberian Commercial Bank was one of Russia's major regional banks, headquartered in St. Petersburg with extensive Siberian operations. These coupons—representing dividend payments spanning the Bolshevik Revolution—were likely never redeemed, as the bank was nationalized after October 1917.

title: Sofiyska Banka (Banque de Sofia), Share Certificate
description: This share certificate represents one share of 100 gold leva in the Sofiyska Banka (Banque de Sofia / Bank of Sofia), No. 035685, issued in Bulgaria in 1917. The denomination in gold leva—rather than regular currency—protected investors against the wartime inflation ravaging Bulgaria during World War I, when the country fought alongside the Central Powers. The Bank of Sofia was one of Bulgaria's principal private financial institutions in the early twentieth century.

title: Banque Industrielle de Chine (中国实业银行), 500 Franc Share
description: This engraved share certificate represents one action of 500 Francs in the Banque Industrielle de Chine (中国实业银行 / Industrial Bank of China), No. 47221, issued in Paris. Founded in 1913 with Franco-Chinese capital, the Banque Industrielle de Chine aimed to finance Chinese industrial development and had branches in major Chinese cities. The bank ultimately failed in 1921 during the post-war global financial crisis—its collapse caused a major scandal in France, where thousands of small investors lost their savings.

title: Banque Territoriale d'Espagne, 500 Franc Share
description: This engraved share certificate represents one action of 500 Francs in the Banque Territoriale d'Espagne, No. 011,824, issued in Madrid on May 30, 1871. The Banque Territoriale d'Espagne was a French-Spanish land mortgage bank providing long-term financing for real estate and agriculture in Spain. It was modeled on France's Crédit Foncier and part of a broader European movement to establish mortgage banking institutions that could mobilize capital for agricultural improvement and urban development.

notes: Banque Territoriale d'Espagne, 500 Franc action No. 011,824, Madrid, May 30, 1871

title: Beate Uhse Aktiengesellschaft, 1 Euro Share
description: This stock certificate represents one share of 1 Euro par value in Beate Uhse Aktiengesellschaft, No. 000069560, issued in Flensburg, Germany in May 1999. Beate Uhse AG—founded in 1946 by former Luftwaffe test pilot Beate Uhse—was a pioneering German adult entertainment retailer. When the company went public on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange in May 1999, it became one of the first businesses in its industry to do so, attracting global media attention and representing a landmark in postwar German cultural liberalization. The company later went bankrupt in 2017.

title: Black-Sea-Kuban Railway Company, 4½% Bond
description: This bond certificate represents one obligation of £20 (equivalent to 189 Rubles) at 4½% of the Black-Sea-Kuban Railway Company, Bond No. A13852, issued at Yekaterinador (now Krasnodar) in 1911. The Black-Sea-Kuban Railway served the fertile Kuban agricultural region of southern Russia, connecting it to Black Sea ports. The bond's dual denomination in British pounds and Russian rubles reflects the practice of marketing Russian railway bonds to international investors through London and other European capital markets.
creator: Black-Sea-Kuban Railway Company
notes: Black-Sea-Kuban Railway Company, 4½% Bond £20 = 189 Rubles, No. A13852, Yekaterinador, 1911

ЖЕЛЕЗНОЙ ДОРОГИ

title: Boston and Providence Rail Road Corporation, Stock Certificate
description: This engraved stock certificate represents ten shares in the Boston and Providence Rail Road Corporation, Certificate No. 122, issued to Haliburton Fales on August 15, 1839. The Boston and Providence Railroad, incorporated in 1831 and opened in 1835, was among the earliest railroads in the United States, connecting Boston to Providence, Rhode Island. By the time this certificate was issued, the railroad had already proven its commercial success and was attracting substantial investment.

notes: Boston and Providence Rail Road Corporation, 10 shares, No. 122, Haliburton Fales, August 15, 1839

The British Honduras Company was a joint-stock company formed to exploit the colony's timber resources and promote commercial settlement, exemplifying the mid-Victorian use of the limited liability company form to finance colonial enterprise.

title: Compagnie Universelle du Canal Interocéanique de Panama, 500 Franc Share
description: This engraved share certificate represents one action of 500 Francs in the Compagnie Universelle du Canal Interocéanique de Panama, No. 185,229, accompanied by a 1886 billet de finance coupon. The Panama Canal Company was organized in 1879 by Ferdinand de Lesseps, who mobilized capital from hundreds of thousands of French small investors to build a sea-level canal across the Isthmus of Panama. The project collapsed in 1889 amid engineering failures and financial fraud, triggering the Panama Scandal—the greatest financial and political scandal of nineteenth-century France and the ruin of hundreds of thousands of investors.

title: Compagnie Universelle du Canal Interocéanique de Panama: Titre Provisoire, 500 Francs (No. 0,269,304, Paris, June 20, 1888) [Redeemed via Receivership]
description: This printed provisional bearer bond (titre provisoire au porteur négociable) for one fully paid 500-franc obligation is No. 0,269,304 of the Compagnie Universelle du Canal Interocéanique de Panama (Universal Interoceanic Canal Company of Panama), Société civile with limited liability. Part of an 720-million franc loan authorized by the laws of May 21, 1886, and June 8, 1888. Paris, June 20, 1888. Signed by H. Cottu and J.F. de Lesseps (Ferdinand de Lesseps's son). Stamped multiple times "REMBOURSE PAR LE SEQUESTRE" (redeemed by the receivership administrator). The Panama Canal Company had been launched in 1881 under Ferdinand de Lesseps, builder of the Suez Canal, with great fanfare and public investment. It collapsed in 1889 in one of the nineteenth century's greatest financial disasters, with hundreds of thousands of small French investors losing their savings—a scandal that implicated French politicians and led to a major political crisis. This bond represents one of the company's last capital-raising efforts before bankruptcy.
creator: Compagnie Universelle du Canal Interocéanique de Panama
notes: Compagnie Universelle du Canal Interocéanique de Panama. Titre provisoire 500 francs. No. 0,269,304. Paris, June 20, 1888. Signed by H. Cottu and J.F. de Lesseps. Stamped REMBOURSE PAR LE SEQUESTRE. Panama Canal Company scandal.

title: Compagnie Universelle du Canal Maritime de Suez, 3% Obligation
description: This engraved bond certificate represents one 3% obligation of 500 Francs, 2e Série, No. 271,266, of the Compagnie Universelle du Canal Maritime de Suez. The Suez Canal Company, founded in 1858 by Ferdinand de Lesseps, opened the Suez Canal in 1869, transforming global trade by linking the Mediterranean and Red Seas. The company issued multiple series of bonds to finance construction and maintenance of one of the most consequential infrastructure projects of the nineteenth century.

title: Central Trust Company, Stock Certificate
description: This stock certificate represents 1,030 shares of the Central Trust Company of Cambridge, Massachusetts, Certificate No. 1636, issued to Edgar R. Champlin on July 21, 1929—just three months before the Wall Street Crash of October 1929. The Central Trust Company was a Massachusetts state-chartered trust company serving the Greater Boston area. Trust companies of this era operated as both commercial banks and investment institutions, making them highly vulnerable to the bank runs and asset deflation of the subsequent Depression.

title: Amsterdam Depositary Certificate for Bank of the United States Shares
description: This Amsterdam depositary receipt, No. 36,013, certifies that shares of the Bank of the United States are held in deposit by the Dutch banking houses Hope & Co., Ketwich & Voombergh, and the Widow Willem Borski on behalf of the bearer, dated January 31, 1844. The Second Bank of the United States (1816–1836) had attracted major Dutch investment, and Amsterdam banking houses facilitated European participation in American securities. By 1844 the bank had lost its federal charter and was operating under a Pennsylvania charter before its final failure; these certificates represent the continued settlement of residual claims by foreign investors.
creator: Hope & Co.; Ketwich & Voombergh; Widow Willem Borski
notes: Amsterdam depositary certificate No. 36,013 for Bank of the United States shares, Hope & Co./Ketwich & Voombergh/Widow Willem Borski, January 31, 1844
transcription:
# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

CERTIFICAAT.

N°. 36012

Houder dezes is geregtigd tot eene Actie, groot HONDERD DOLLARS, in de Bank, genaamd de Bank der Vereenigde Staten van Amerika, te PHILADELPHIA, onder meerderen ingeschreven, op Naam van het Kantoor van Administratie, opgerigt te Amsterdam, onder directie van

HOPE & COMP.,
KETWICH & VOOMBERGH.
EN
WEDUWE WILLEM BORSKI,

en gedeponeerd bij de Notarissen Commelin en Weyland.

De UITTEELINGEN op de gedeponeerde Actien, verschijnende d Januari en d Juli van elk jaar, zullen voor rekening en risico van den Houder, in AMERICA ontvangen, overgemaakt en alhier op COUPONS uitbetaald worden, volgens Art. 6, van het uitgegeven Bericht, in dato 30 Junij 1824.

De HOUDER zal ten allen tijde geregtigd zijn, om tegens intrekking van dit Certificaat en de als dan nog Onverschenen Coupons, met het Bewijs tot bekoming van Afentie Couponj, en tegen betaling van de KOSTEN, terug te vorderen, eene Actie van HONDERD DOLLARS, in gemeelde BANK, alles overeenkomstig Art. 10, van het voormelde Bericht.

Amsterdam den 31 Januari 1844

Gezien bij mij
S. Commelin
Notaris.

[Signatures - illegible but present]
H. Voombergh
[unclear name]

Hierbij afgegeven HALFJAARIGE COUPONS, tot d Januari 1845, en Bewijs voor volgende Coupons.

---

**English translation:**

CERTIFICATE.

No. 36012

The holder hereof is entitled to one Share, of the amount of ONE HUNDRED DOLLARS, in the Bank, named the Bank of the United States of America, at PHILADELPHIA, registered among others, in the Name of the Administration Office, established at Amsterdam, under the direction of

HOPE & COMP.,
KETWICH & VOOMBERGH.
AND
WIDOW WILLEM BORSKI,

and deposited with the Notaries Commelin and Weyland.

The DISTRIBUTIONS on the deposited Shares, falling due in January and July of each year, shall be received for the account and risk of the Holder in AMERICA, remitted and paid out here on COUPONS, according to Art. 6, of the issued Notice, dated 30 June 1824.

The HOLDER shall at all times be entitled, upon surrender of this Certificate and the then outstanding Coupons, with the Proof of receipt of Agency Coupons, and upon payment of the COSTS, to recover one Share of ONE HUNDRED DOLLARS, in the said BANK, all in accordance with Art. 10, of the aforesaid Notice.

Amsterdam the 31st January 1844

Seen by me
S. Commelin
Notary.

[Signatures]
H. Voombergh
[unclear name]

Hereby issued SEMI-ANNUAL COUPONS, until January 1845, and Proof for following Coupons.

---

**Notable markings:**

- Serial number: N°. 36012 (handwritten at top)
- Date: Amsterdam den 31 Januari 1844 (31 January 1844)
- Signatures of notary Commelin and banking house representatives (H. Voombergh and others)
- Handwritten notations and signature marks
- Ornate decorative border frame typical of 19th-century financial documents
- Reference to coupons valid until January 1845

title: Hamburg Depositary Certificate for Russian 5% State Bonds
description: This Hamburg depositary receipt, No. 14,463, certifies that bonds of 500 Rubles at 5% of the Russian state are held in deposit by the Hamburg banking houses Sillem Benecke & Co. and H.J. Stresow on behalf of the bearer, dated April 23, 1851. Russian state bonds were widely held across Western Europe in the nineteenth century, and German banking houses in Hamburg served as intermediaries providing depositary and custody services for securities that were difficult to hold and transfer across national borders.
creator: Sillem Benecke & Co.; H.J. Stresow
notes: Hamburg depositary certificate No. 14,463 for 500 Rubles 5% Russian bonds, Sillem Benecke & Co./H.J. Stresow, April 23, 1851

# Document Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

CERTIFICAAT.

No. 14463.

Goed voor eene Inscriptie van Vyfhonderd Roebels 5 pct. Russische fondsen, betaalbaar in Metalick, ingeschreeven in het grootboek der Russische Inschrijvingen ten Naame van de administratie opgericht te HAMBURG onder directie van de Heeren SILLEM, BENECKE & Comp. en de Heer H. J. STRESOW,

waarvan de Intressen voor Rekening van den Houder ontfangen & uitbetaald zullen worden op Coupons, in gevolge Art 5. van het uitgegeven bericht.

De houder van dit Certificaat zal ten allen tyde en op deszelfs eerste aanvrage, gerechtigd zyn, de voorschreven Som van Vyfhonderd Roebels terug te vorderen, in Originecle Inscriptiën, tegen terug gave van dit Certificaat, met de overscheene Coupons, en het daarbij afgegeven bewys voor de afgift van volgende Coupons, alles in gevolge Art. 2. van het bericht.

Hamburg 23 Maart 1851.

[Signatures: Willem Benecke, H.J. Stresow]

Voor depot ingevolge Art. 4. van het bericht

M. Sanghans
Notaris.

---

**English translation:**

CERTIFICATE.

No. 14463.

Valid for one Registration of Five Hundred Rubles at 5 percent Russian funds, payable in Metallic currency, registered in the ledger of Russian Registrations in the Name of the administration established in HAMBURG under the direction of the Gentlemen SILLEM, BENECKE & Company and Mr. H. J. STRESOW,

whereby the Interest for the Account of the Holder shall be received and paid out on Coupons, in accordance with Art. 5 of the issued notice.

The holder of this Certificate shall at all times and upon his first request be entitled to demand return of the aforementioned Sum of Five Hundred Rubles in Original Registrations, against return of this Certificate, with the remaining Coupons, and the accompanying proof for the delivery of following Coupons, all in accordance with Art. 2 of the notice.

Hamburg 23 March 1851.

[Signatures: Willem Benecke, H.J. Stresow]

For deposit in accordance with Art. 4 of the notice

M. Sanghans
Notary.

---

- Handwritten date: 23 Maart 1851 (23 March 1851)
- Two handwritten signatures: Willem Benecke and H.J. Stresow
